comments.commenter: Comment from Onondaga County Water Authority (OCWA)
comments.comment_text: This rule is necessary and important. Restricting drone operation around public water supply and wastewater infrastructure has been a significant gap in security, and this rule will help resolve that gap.

I am submitting this comment as a Part 107 remote pilot and owner of AeroScan3D LLC, a Maryland small business providing drone mapping, inspection support, aerial documentation, and 3D modeling services.

I oppose the proposed rule as written.

The proposal would create a process for fixed-site facility operators and proprietors to request unmanned aircraft flight restrictions, or UAFRs. Even if FAA retains final approval authority, this risks moving the National Airspace System toward a patchwork of facility-requested drone exclusion zones.

The National Airspace System should remain national. Airspace restrictions should remain rare, federal, evidence-based, and tightly controlled. Private or non-federal facility operators should not be given a process that effectively creates private drone exclusion zones over commercial, industrial, utility, rail, entertainment, or infrastructure-adjacent property.

FAA proposes to allow Part 107 operations to access Standard UAFR airspace under conditions such as Remote ID, notification, and shortest practicable transit. That access is essential and must remain. However, notification must not become permission. A facility manager should receive situational awareness, not veto power over lawful FAA-regulated operations.

If FAA creates UAFRs, access and notification should be integrated directly into LAANC or an FAA-approved equivalent digital airspace system. If the restriction is important enough to limit lawful Part 107 operations, it is important enough to be managed through the standardized infrastructure pilots already use for controlled airspace.

UAFR notification should not rely on calling, emailing, or negotiating with facility managers. A Part 107 operator should submit required notice through LAANC or an FAA-approved connected system. FAA should record the notice, the facility should receive automatic awareness, and the pilot should receive a confirmation record. The facility should not be able to approve, deny, delay, negotiate, or add site-specific requirements.

UAFRs must be visible in the normal preflight environment. They should not exist as a separate or disconnected notice system. They should be published in official FAA airspace data and integrated into LAANC, FAA facility-map-style tools, B4UFLY/Air Aware-type applications, and common UAS mission-planning platforms. A Part 107 pilot should see a UAFR during normal preflight planning, not discover it through a separate website, facility contact list, Federal Register search, or after-the-fact enforcement action.

This proposal would create negative business impacts for small Part 107 operators. Drone service businesses rely on predictable access to lawful airspace to plan jobs, quote work, schedule clients, maintain insurance confidence, and invest in aircraft, software, training, registration, and insurance. If restrictions spread across commercial, industrial, utility, rail, amusement, prison, financial, healthcare, communications, or transportation-adjacent locations, small operators may lose work not because the operation is unsafe, but because nearby facilities requested restrictions.

The economic harm would fall hardest on compliant operators. Part 107 pilots are certificated, aircraft are registered, Remote ID is being implemented, and controlled airspace already requires authorization. Bad actors are unlikely to be stopped by another mapped restriction. The rule would mostly burden lawful pilots who already follow FAA requirements.

If FAA proceeds, I strongly oppose allowing UAFR lateral boundaries to extend beyond the facility property line. UAFRs should never extend beyond the applicant’s property line, and in many cases they should be smaller than the property line and limited only to the specific vulnerable asset.

Special UAFRs should be reserved only for truly sensitive federal, military, intelligence, or federally sponsored national-security sites. They should not become the default model for private infrastructure or ordinary commercial facilities.

I recommend that FAA withdraw the proposed rule as written. If FAA proceeds, the final rule should: keep UAFRs inside property lines; require the smallest practicable footprint; preserve Part 107 access through Standard UAFRs for lawful Remote ID-compliant operators; make notification informational only, not private permission; integrate UAFR notification directly into LAANC or an FAA-approved equivalent system; publish UAFRs in official airspace data and common UAS planning tools; require FAA oversight so facility operators do not become de facto airspace controllers; require periodic review and expiration if the documented need no longer exists; and prevent UAFRs from being used for privacy, nuisance, competition, or property-control reasons.

The National Airspace System should remain national. It should not become a patchwork of private drone exclusion zones that harms lawful small businesses while doing little to stop bad actors.

I am a former Part 107 drone pilot, and I know important clear rules are for our industry.

The main reason I support this is safety. Right now, there is a lot of risk when people fly drones too close to infrastructure that is vital to the country. If a drone causes an accident at something like a refinery or a prison, it is not just the pilot who gets in trouble. The whole community could be put in danger or end up paying for the damages. It is only fair that we have federal rules to prevent these kinds of risks before they happen.

I have some personal experience with these kinds of rules. I used to live right next to a military base that had very strict no-fly zones. Honestly, it did not ruin the hobby for me at all. As long as the boundaries were clear, it was easy to follow the rules and keep flying safely. I think this proposal is a smart way to protect important facilities without making it too hard for responsible pilots to do their jobs.

My one suggestion is to make sure these new restricted areas show up on our flight maps and apps like LAANC immediately. It is much easier for everyone to stay out of trouble when the boundaries are easy to see on a phone or a tablet while out in the field. When the rules are clear and the information is easy to find, everything runs a lot smoother for both the government and the pilots.

Thank you for the chance to comment. I think this rule is a necessary step to keep our infrastructure safe while the drone industry keeps moving forward.
Please see attached document due to comment length.

While I can appreciate the security concerns listed by the federal government, the ability for a private corporation to effectively claim ownership of a block of airspace is outrageous in the extreme. The public's airspace should be for the public, not corporations.

An outright ban on Unmanned Aircraft (sUAS in particular) over non-military, non-governmental sites is draconian.

A reasonable compromise should be considered.

Drone operations less than 100 feet AGL could be considered an unsafe distraction for people working at these sites, and a prohibition on flight below 100' AGL would be reasonable. But above 100' AGL to the sUAS ceiling of 400' AGL poses no immediate safety threat to personnel.

The "national security threat" of sUAS photography at non-military, non-governmental sites is a dubious assertion, especially when General Aviation aircraft can operate above these same sites with no restrictions.

Bottom line: Prohibit sUAS operations from the surface up to 100 AGL. Above 100 AGL (to the allowable max of 400 AGL), do not allow any prohibitions.

Enforcement should be automated through a approved platform. This will eliminate virtually all possible concerns with violations while simultenaously paying for the entirety of the proposed rules. There is a solution that can handle this enforcement today. We would welcome to demo this solution with the FAA.

To the Federal Aviation Administration,
I am writing to provide professional feedback on the proposed rule regarding Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). As a professionally certified Part 107 Remote Pilot and veteran with over seven years of experience in the National Airspace System (NAS), I fully support the initiative to protect truly sensitive critical infrastructure. However, I have concerns regarding the potential for over-broad lateral restrictions, particularly as they apply to the transportation sector and railroad main lines.
Background and Qualifications
I operate a professional aerial photography and drone services business. My background as a veteran has instilled in me a deep respect for security protocols and the necessity of protecting our nation’s infrastructure. Throughout my seven years of flight experience, I have maintained a perfect safety record by adhering strictly to Part 107 regulations and maintaining high situational awareness.
Concerns Regarding Lateral Boundaries on Main Lines
While I recognize the inherent security and safety risks associated with high-activity "fixed-site facilities" such as major rail yards, I am concerned about the application of lateral boundaries along thousands of miles of "main line" tracks.
1.Definitions of "Fixed Site": I urge the FAA to strictly define what constitutes a "fixed-site facility." A sprawling main line in a rural area should not be subject to the same blanket restrictions as a high-security industrial hub or chemical plant.
2.Lateral Encroachment: The proposed rule suggests lateral limits typically drawn within property lines. In many cases, railroad rights-of-way extend significantly beyond the tracks. If these boundaries are strictly enforced without a "transit allowance," it could create massive, impassable "walls" in the NAS for responsible Part 107 operators who are simply transiting the area or performing unrelated work nearby. 
3.Distinguishing Risk: There is a fundamental difference between a drone hovering over a critical signaling hub and a drone capturing a cinematic shot of a train on a rural main line from a safe distance. I suggest that lateral restrictions be limited to specific, high-value assets (bridges, tunnels, yards) rather than the entirety of a railroad’s linear property.
Recommendations
• Safe Transit Provisions: I recommend that the FAA include provisions for Part 107 pilots to transit the outer edges of a UAFR lateral boundary as long as they do not dwell over the property.
• Tiered Implementation: Apply Special UAFRs (total bans) only to high-threat hubs, while utilizing Standard UAFRs for other areas to allow for "compliant operator" access through existing waiver processes.
Thank you for the opportunity to comment. As a responsible member of the aviation community, I want to ensure we protect our infrastructure without unnecessarily crippling the utility and growth of the commercial drone industry.
Respectfully,
Javid A. Beykzadeh
FAA Part 107 Certified Remote Pilot

Proposed Rule
Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility

The ONLY effect this proposed UAFR will have is on already-law-abiding drone operators, particularly those who might be interested in photographing railroads, bridges or structures of any kind, for hobby or artistic purposes. As noted in many of the comments, multiple methods of surveillance already exist which have their own (or no) oversight in the use thereof, such as airplanes and Google Earth (the latter being available on any cell phone or computer).

Bad actors will always find a way to achieve their nefarious goals and there is no need to criminalize the entire population of legitimate hobbyist drone operators in attempting to prevent a potential action that was already illegal in its intent.There is no way to outlaw every potential tool a bad actor might use, as we found out on 9/11 with box knives. Jason Bourne in the movies showed how a ballpoint pen and a rolled-up newspaper could be used as weapons.

Personally, although I am a hobbyist photographer of railroads, I have no interest in using drones for that purpose. But, if drone use is severely restricted in proximity to a railroad, bridge or structure, can ground-based cameras be far behind? And when those are similarly restricted, what about sketch pads and pencils?

Also noted by other commenters is the issue of airspace ownership rights. The idea that a private corporation could restrict any use of drones over its property (or even the airspace nearby), arbitrarily, is thoroughly absurd. The obvious motive is not entirely on potential security concerns, but on legal liability for current and future safety infractions or questionable corporate business practices. That drones would be affected and not aerial flight or satellite views is also inconsistent.

I've never made a public comment on a proposed rule before, and wonder how effective such comments can be, but am hopeful that millions of dollars a year in administrative costs might be used in a manner more useful to American society than is likely with this proposed UAFR.

If enacted, the new regulations described here would cede a critical portion of the FAA's authority to non-governmental private organizations who are not answerable to taxpayers. This is a huge mistake, because once that cat is out the bag, I do not believe it will be possible to ever put it back in. The reason Congress delegates its authority in such cases to the FAA, is because the FAA are the recognized subject matter experts. Enacting these regulations would also set a dangerous precedent for other agencies (subject matter experts in their respective fields) to delegate their own authority to the private sector, and could ultimately set a precedent to neuter the authority of agencies like the FAA.

Furthermore, if enacted, these new regulations will undoubtedly be misused by companies claiming a national security or homeland security case, where no such case can be justified. The only organizations that should have authority to request and/or maintain unmanned aircraft restrictions are the FAA, the U.S. military through its respective branches and agencies, and (arguably) the operators of nuclear power plants. I also firmly believe that nuclear plant operators should only be able to do so through cooperation with the Department of Energy.

I. Economic and Recreational Impact of Asset Density
As a taxpayer and a responsible UAS pilot (TRUST-certified and Remote ID-compliant), I strongly oppose the scope of the proposed 14 CFR Part 74. The FAA’s own analysis in Section IV of the NPRM identifies approximately 125,000 fixed-site facilities as eligible for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). By including 16 massive sectors—including "Critical Manufacturing" and "Financial Services"—the FAA is effectively endorsing the de facto privatization of the National Airspace System (NAS). This creates a "Swiss cheese" airspace that will make legal recreational flight in suburban and urban environments technically impossible.
​II. Violation of Privacy and Misuse of Remote ID (§ 74.56)
I specifically contest Proposed § 74.56, which mandates that any facility applying for a UAFR must demonstrate the capability to "receive and log broadcast Remote Identification messages." This is a radical departure from the FAA’s original stated intent for Remote ID. Remote ID was presented to the public as a safety and air traffic management tool; under Section 2209, it is being weaponized into a permanent surveillance and logging mandate for private and local government entities. This creates a significant privacy risk for law-abiding citizens whose flight data will now be stored in decentralized, private databases without federal oversight.
​III. Redundancy and State-Level Overreach (e.g., Ohio HB 77)
The implementation of Section 2209 provides a "federal shield" for increasingly aggressive state-level laws, such as Ohio House Bill 77 (effective April 2025). HB 77 already criminalizes drone operations near "critical facilities." Layering a federal UAFR on top of state-level criminal penalties—monitored by local assets using Remote ID data—creates an environment of extreme legal jeopardy for the hobbyist. The FAA must provide a carve-out for recreational Visual Line of Sight (VLOS) operations that pose zero risk to the physical security of these sites.
​IV. Conclusion
The FAA has failed to provide empirical data suggesting that recreational VLOS operations under the Section 44809 exception pose a unique threat to these 125,000 sites. I urge the FAA to narrow the scope of eligible facilities strictly to high-security "Special UAFRs" and to remove the Remote ID logging mandate for Standard UAFR applicants.

Sky Photos LLC
Craig Weston, Owner & Remote Pilot in Command
FAA Part 107 Certificate Holder
Capital Region, New York
craigw@skyphotosllc.com
May 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, S.E., Room W58-213
Washington, D.C. 20590-0001
RE: Comments on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 — Designation –
Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility (NPRM)
Dear FAA Rulemaking Office,
I am submitting these comments on behalf of Sky Photos LLC, a solo-operated
commercial unmanned aircraft services company located in New York’s Capital Region.
I hold a Part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate and operate commercially under that
certification. Sky Photos LLC provides aerial inspection and documentation services to
commercial clients, including solar energy contractors and construction firms in the
Northeastern United States.
I support the FAA’s effort to implement section 2209 of FESSA and appreciate the
careful balance the agency has attempted to strike between protecting critical
infrastructure and preserving access to the National Airspace System (NAS) for
compliant commercial operators. However, I write to raise several concerns about how
this rule, as proposed, could materially harm the economic viability of legitimate
commercial UAS operations — particularly recurring inspection services performed
under contract at energy sector facilities — without meaningfully improving safety or
security.
I offer these comments in the spirit of constructive engagement and in direct response
to the FAA’s explicit requests for public input in Sections I.F and IV of the NPRM.
I. Background and Relevant Operational Context
Sky Photos LLC holds active commercial service relationships with solar energy
contractors, including work supporting solar installation documentation and operational
inspection services. My clients include engineering, procurement, and construction
(EPC) contractors who develop and maintain utility-scale solar generation facilities.
These facilities are precisely the type of critical infrastructure the Energy Sector criteria
in this NPRM would cover.

My business model is built around recurring retainer-based inspection contracts. Under
this model, I am an authorized vendor contracted directly by the facility owner or their
EPC contractor to conduct scheduled aerial inspections using FAA-compliant
equipment, full Remote ID broadcast, and coordinated site access. This is not incidental
drone activity near a facility — it is a pre-authorized, operationally necessary service
that the facility itself has procured.
The distinctions between this type of operation and the problematic UAS activity the
NPRM is designed to address — unauthorized surveillance, contraband delivery,
malicious interference — could not be starker. Yet as currently written, this rule does not
sufficiently distinguish between these two categories of operators in a way that
preserves the commercial utility of contracted inspection services.
II. The Per-Flight Notification Requirement Creates Unworkable
Friction for Contracted Inspection Services
The proposed § 74.255 would require UAS operators accessing UAFR airspace to
provide notification to the fixed site facility prior to each operation. While I understand
the security rationale, this requirement — as written — fails to account for operators
who are already under contract with the facility and whose access has been pre-
authorized at the contract level.
For a commercial solar inspection retainer, I may conduct multiple flights per site visit,
across multiple sites, on a monthly or quarterly schedule. A per-flight notification
requirement that is disconnected from the pre-existing contractual relationship between
the operator and facility would:
• Add administrative burden with no corresponding safety benefit, since the facility
has already authorized and invited the operator;
• Create ambiguity about what form of notification satisfies the requirement and
what consequences follow if procedural details are not met;
• Generate compliance uncertainty that could expose contracted operators to
enforcement risk even when fully authorized by the facility.
The FAA has asked: “What additional requirements, either technological or procedural,
must be in place for these operators to be able to operate in the UAFR?” My response
is that the appropriate mechanism is a standing authorization model — a documented
contractual relationship between the operator and the facility proprietor that satisfies the
notification requirement on an ongoing basis. A signed service agreement, access
authorization letter, or equivalent facility-issued credential should constitute presumptive
compliance with the notification requirement for the duration of the contract term.
I strongly recommend the FAA create a formal category of “Authorized Contracted
Operator” within § 74.250 and § 74.255, with a process by which a facility proprietor can
register authorized operators with FAA in advance, enabling those operators to conduct
contracted flights within the UAFR without per-flight notification so long as the contract
is in effect.

III. The Economic Impact on Commercial UAS Operators Is
Underestimated
The NPRM’s cost-benefit analysis focuses primarily on applicant compliance costs
(facility operators applying for UAFRs) and government review costs. It does not
adequately account for the economic impact on commercial UAS service providers
whose revenue depends on access to the very facilities likely to obtain UAFRs.
The FAA has explicitly asked: “What would the economic impact to commercial UAS
operators if they are not allowed to transit UAFRs?” I can offer a concrete answer from
my operational context:
• Solar energy inspection services constitute a core revenue stream for my
business and for thousands of similar small commercial UAS operations
nationwide. Utility-scale solar sites are natural candidates for UAFRs under the
Energy Sector criteria.
• If accessing UAFR airspace requires ad-hoc per-flight coordination, approval
friction, or ambiguous compliance steps not accounted for in the existing
contractual relationship, clients will face higher costs and longer lead times —
directly reducing the value proposition of contracted aerial inspection services.
• Small commercial operators — sole proprietors, micro-businesses — lack the
legal and compliance resources to navigate an evolving, site-by-site UAFR
approval landscape. Regulatory ambiguity disproportionately harms small
operators relative to large firms with dedicated compliance staff.
• If the friction of UAFR compliance leads facility operators to defer or reduce
inspection frequency, real safety and operational consequences follow — solar
anomalies go undetected, maintenance is delayed, and the safety rationale for
aerial inspection is undermined.
The FAA should conduct a supplemental economic analysis that specifically addresses
the impact on contracted commercial UAS operators providing inspection and
documentation services to UAFR-eligible facilities, including a small business impact
assessment under the Regulatory Flexibility Act.
IV. Remote ID Already Provides Meaningful Operator Credentialing
The FAA has asked: “What information should an unmanned aircraft operator need to
provide to establish their credibility and demonstrate that they are not a security threat?”
I submit that compliant Part 107 operators broadcasting Remote ID already provide the
most operationally meaningful credentialing signal available in the current regulatory
framework. Remote ID broadcasts the operator’s registration number, aircraft location,
and flight information in real time — exactly the information a facility security team
needs to distinguish a contracted, compliant operator from an unauthorized one.
The NPRM should explicitly acknowledge that:

• Active Remote ID broadcast, combined with a valid Part 107 certificate and a
documented facility authorization, should constitute a complete and sufficient
access credential for UAFR airspace under § 74.250;
• Facility operators deploying Remote ID receivers (as required by the rule) will be
able to confirm compliant operator status in real time, making redundant
notification requirements unnecessary for pre-authorized contractors;
• FAA should publish clear technical guidance on how facilities can use Remote ID
infrastructure to verify operator credentials at the time of flight, rather than relying
solely on advance notification paperwork.
V. The Rule Should Not Preempt or Supersede Existing Contractual
Facility Access
Many commercial UAS operators working in the energy sector have already established
contractual, insurance, and safety protocols with facility operators that reflect industry
best practices. These include site-specific safety briefings, hazard assessments,
insurance certificates, and coordination with facility security personnel.
The NPRM is silent on the interaction between a UAFR and a pre-existing contracted
access relationship. This silence creates legal ambiguity: does a UAFR effectively void
or supersede an existing facility-level authorization? Can a facility unilaterally modify the
terms of an existing service contract by applying for and obtaining a UAFR?
FAA should clarify that a UAFR does not supersede, modify, or invalidate pre-existing
contractual access agreements between facility operators and authorized commercial
UAS service providers. The UAFR should operate as an external airspace designation
against unauthorized third parties — not as a mechanism that retroactively alters private
commercial relationships.
VI. Fixed Site Facilities Must Bear Reciprocal Operational
Responsibilities
This rulemaking places significant new compliance obligations on UAS operators
seeking access to UAFR airspace. However, the proposed rule is largely silent on the
corresponding responsibilities of the fixed site facilities themselves. If a facility obtains a
UAFR and thereby assumes authority to restrict access to a designated volume of
national airspace, it must also assume a duty to administer that restriction in a
transparent, timely, and accountable manner.
Fixed sites that obtain UAFRs should be required to:
• Designate and maintain a current, publicly accessible point of contact
responsible for processing UAS access requests, providing approval or denial
decisions, and communicating with operators and FAA;
• Maintain a site operations log documenting all UAS access requests received,
approvals and denials issued, the basis for each decision, and any incidents or
anomalies observed during UAS operations within the UAFR;

• Issue a written or electronic certification of flight permission at the time of each
approval, providing the operator with documentation confirming authorized
access for that operation or contract period — reducing the risk of
misunderstanding, enforcement ambiguity, or post-hoc disputes about whether
access was authorized;
• Update FAA within a defined timeframe whenever designated contact information
changes, to ensure the public-facing UAFR database remains accurate and
operators can reach the appropriate personnel.
The current proposal creates an asymmetric burden: commercial operators face
enforcement consequences for unauthorized access, but facilities face no
corresponding accountability for failing to maintain accessible authorization channels.
This imbalance is operationally and legally problematic. An operator who attempts in
good faith to obtain authorization but encounters an unresponsive or non-existent facility
contact has no meaningful recourse and faces potential enforcement liability regardless.
FAA should incorporate minimum administrative standards for UAFR-holding facilities
as a condition of obtaining and maintaining a UAFR designation, and should include
these obligations in both the regulatory text and the accompanying Advisory Circular.
VII. Specific Recommendations
In summary, I respectfully urge the FAA to incorporate the following modifications to the
proposed rule:
• Create a formal “Authorized Contracted Operator” designation within § 74.250
and § 74.255 that allows facility proprietors to pre-register contracted UAS
service providers, granting standing authorization for the contract term in lieu of
per-flight notification;
• Establish that a valid Part 107 certificate, active Remote ID broadcast, and
documented facility authorization together constitute presumptive compliance
with UAFR access requirements;
• Publish clear guidance — in the accompanying Advisory Circular — on how
Remote ID receivers deployed at UAFR facilities can be used to verify real-time
operator credentials, reducing the need for redundant procedural notification;
• Conduct a supplemental Regulatory Flexibility Act analysis that specifically
addresses the economic impact on small commercial UAS operators providing
contracted services to UAFR-eligible facilities;
• Clarify that a UAFR does not supersede, modify, or invalidate pre-existing
contractual service agreements between facility operators and authorized
commercial UAS vendors;
• Require UAFR-holding facilities to designate and maintain a current UAS point of
contact, maintain a flight operations log, issue written or electronic approval
certifications at the time of each authorization, and update FAA within a defined
timeframe when contact information changes — as conditions of obtaining and
maintaining a UAFR designation.

VIII. Conclusion
The UAS industry has invested significantly in building credible, compliant commercial
operations. Part 107 certification, Remote ID compliance, insurance, and coordinated
site access protocols represent exactly the kind of responsible operator behavior FAA
and Congress have sought to cultivate. This rule should reward that investment by
creating clear, workable pathways for compliant contracted operators to access UAFR
airspace — not by creating new compliance burdens that treat credentialed contractors
the same as unauthorized operators.
I appreciate the FAA’s attention to these concerns and the opportunity to participate in
this rulemaking. I am available to provide additional information or to participate in any
stakeholder engagement process the FAA may convene.
Respectfully submitted,
Craig Weston
Owner & Remote Pilot in Command, Sky Photos LLC
FAA Part 107 Certificate Holder
Capital Region, New York
craigw@skyphotosllc.com
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558

FORMAL PUBLIC COMMENT SUBMISSION
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA)
Docket No.: FAA‑2026‑4558
RIN: 2120‑AL33
Subject:
Designation – Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility (Section 2209 NPRM)
Submitted by:
James P Smith
QTS Data Centers
Converged Security Organization
Date:
May 11, 2026
1. Executive Summary
QTS Data Centers (“QTS”), a leading provider of hyperscale, colocation, and hybrid data
center solutions, respectfully submits this comment in support of the Federal Aviation
Administration’s (FAA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) implementing Section
2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016.
QTS operates mission-critical digital infrastructures supporting data processing,
transactions, and digital services across all major industries to include Banking &
Financial Services, Healthcare, Government / Public Sector, Retail & eCommerce,
Manufacturing / Industrial, IT & Telecommunications. As an active participant within the
Information Technology sector of the Nation’s critical infrastructure framework, QTS
strongly supports the establishment of the Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR)
program.
We assess the NPRM as a strategically necessary advancement in protecting fixed-site
critical infrastructure from emergent aerial threats, and we recommend targeted
enhancements to align the final rule with:

• DHS/CISA National Infrastructure Protection Plan (NIPP) risk management
principles
• Sector Risk Management Agency (SRMA) coordination models
• Modern threat intelligence and converged security practices
2. Alignment with DHS/CISA Critical Infrastructure Frameworks
2.1 NIPP Risk Management Framework Alignment
The NIPP Risk Framework defines risk as a function of:
• Threat × Vulnerability × Consequence
The proposed UAFR framework directly supports this model:
• Threat: Increasing prevalence of unauthorized UAS operations
• Vulnerability: Fixed-site facilities with exposed vertical and perimeter attack
surfaces
• Consequence: Cascading impacts across multiple sectors (financial, healthcare,
government, energy)
QTS supports the FAA’s requirement that applicants demonstrate:
• Aviation safety need
• Protection of people and property
• National security or homeland security justification
These criteria align with NIPP’s risk-informed, consequence-driven approach.
2.2 Sector Risk Management Agency (SRMA) Coordination
QTS recommends formal alignment with CISA as the SRMA for the Information
Technology sector, including:
• Integration of sector-specific threat intelligence
• Recognition of interdependencies across sectors
• Coordination with federal, state, and private stakeholders
This alignment will ensure that UAFR designations reflect systemic risk, not just
localized facility risk.
2.3 CFATS-Style Security Program Parallels
While CFATS applies to chemical infrastructure, its core principles are directly
applicable:

• Risk-based performance standards (RBPS)
• Site-specific security planning
• Threat-informed protective measures
The UAFR application process should incorporate similar elements:
• Documented threat assessments
• Facility criticality classification
• Protective posture validation
3. Role of Data Centers as Critical Infrastructure
Data centers represent core enabling infrastructure across the majority of the 16 DHS-
designated sectors. QTS facilities directly support:
3.1 National Security and Defense
• Hosting of federal agency workloads
• Infrastructure supporting classified and defense operations
3.2 Financial System Stability
• Processing capital markets transactions
• Supporting banking and payment systems
3.3 Healthcare and Public Safety
• Enabling hospital IT systems and medical data infrastructure
• Supporting emergency services data exchange
3.4 Energy and Utility Operations
• Supporting grid control systems and telemetry
• Enabling SCADA and industrial control integration
3.5 Government Continuity
• Hosting state, local, and federal IT systems
• Supporting public safety and regulatory systems
Conclusion: Data centers are not isolated facilities—they are systemically critical
nodes whose disruption produces cross-sector cascading effects.
4. UAS Threat Landscape and Risk Characterization

In alignment with DHS/CISA threat taxonomy, UAS threats to data centers fall into the
following categories:
4.1 Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (ISR)
Unauthorized drones enable adversaries to conduct:
• Persistent surveillance of perimeter security
• Mapping of critical infrastructure components
• Observation of operational patterns
4.2 Physical Security Threats
UAS platforms present potential vectors for:
• Payload delivery (explosive or disruptive devices)
• Interference with rooftop mechanical systems
• Compromise of external infrastructure (power, cooling)
4.3 Cyber-Physical Threat Convergence
Drones may be used to:
• Introduce wireless attack platforms
• Exploit proximity-based vulnerabilities
• Conduct RF-based reconnaissance or exploitation
4.4 Operational Disruption and Safety Risks
Unauthorized UAS activity may:
• Trigger safety protocols or emergency responses
• Interrupt operations
• Endanger personnel safety
These threat vectors clearly meet the FAA’s criteria for national security, homeland
security, and protection of people and property.
5. Support for the UAFR Framework
QTS strongly supports the following NPRM components:
5.1 Petition-Based UAFR Designation
The structured application process ensures:
• Risk-based justification

• Facility-specific tailoring
• Accountability and transparency
5.2 Two-Tier UAFR Model
The distinction between:
• Standard UAFR (controlled access environment)
• Special UAFR (heightened national security posture)
• …provides scalable protection aligned to threat severity.
5.3 Defined Airspace Geometry
The use of:
• Property-bound boundaries
• Vertical ceilings (generally up to 400 feet AGL)
• …ensures precise enforcement while minimizing unintended operational impact.
6. Recommendations for Final Rule Enhancement
6.1 Explicit Recognition of Data Center Campuses
The FAA should:
• Explicitly define hyperscale and colocation data centers as eligible fixed-site
facilities
• Enable multi-parcel campus aggregation under a single UAFR
• Recognize shared infrastructure environments
6.2 Risk-Based Expedited Approval Pathways
Consistent with NIPP and SRMA principles:
• Establish priority processing for high-consequence facilities
• Allow pre-validated templates for critical sectors
• Recognize facilities supporting federal or defense missions
6.3 Integration with DHS/CISA Risk Frameworks

The FAA should:
• Incorporate CISA threat intelligence feeds
• Align with NIPP sector risk scoring methodologies
• Enable cross-agency evaluation for high-impact facilities
6.4 Enforcement and Counter-UAS Coordination
While UAFRs are regulatory constructs, effectiveness requires:
• Integration with federal law enforcement response frameworks
• Alignment with DHS counter-UAS authorities
• Defined escalation pathways for violations
6.5 Protection of Sensitive Security Information
QTS strongly recommends:
• Safeguarding of facility security data submitted in applications
• Limiting public disclosure that could create targeting intelligence for adversaries
• Alignment with Protected Critical Infrastructure Information (PCII) standards
6.6 Enablement of Authorized Operational Use
QTS supports limited authorized UAS operations for:
• Infrastructure inspection
• Construction monitoring
• Security operations
The FAA should:
• Provide streamlined authorization pathways
• Enable facility-controlled UAS access governance
7. National Security and Economic Impact
Failure to implement effective aerial protections for data centers introduces risk to:
• Financial markets and economic stability
• Government and defense operations
• Healthcare and emergency services
• Energy and utility infrastructure
Conversely, the UAFR framework will:

• Enhance airspace sovereignty
• Improve threat deterrence
• Enable clear regulatory governance
• Strengthen public-private risk mitigation partnerships
8. Conclusion
The Section 2209 NPRM represents a critical advancement in securing the Nation’s
most essential infrastructure against emerging aerial threats.
QTS supports the FAA’s proposed rule and urges adoption of enhancements that:
• Reflect data centers as systemically critical infrastructure
• Align with DHS/CISA risk management frameworks
• Enable efficient and scalable implementation
• Strengthen national security posture
Respectfully Submitted,
James Smith
Converged Security Manager
QTS Data Centers
On behalf of the QTS Converged Security Organization

This is an excellent and long-awaited rulemaking which will help close a major security gap. However, I have some suggestions for improvement.

The rulemaking notes that an UAFR is a "virtual no trespassing sign", and further notes that "FAA would expect the applicant to provide a description of how the facility would identify and respond to UAFR incursions, how employees would be trained, and how incursions would be reported. For example, would the facility have a way to ... report incursions, post signage communicating the airspace restriction ...?"
I certainly agree with this! Especially in the case of facilities that are open to the public (theme parks, passenger transportation facilities, etc.), it may be a good idea to require signage including a clear notice of the UAFR, and contact details for rapid reporting of suspected violations. For instance, something like this would be good: "NO DRONE ZONE - Unauthorized use of drones in this area is strictly prohibited. Violators subject to significant penalties. 14 CFR Part 74, § 91.134, 107.45 and/or 49 USC § 46307, as applicable. If you see someone preparing to fly, or flying a drone, report it immediately to any [employee or equivalent term used by the facility e.g. associate/Team Member/Cast Member/etc.] or call [phone number for facility's security control center, etc.] [or press [button] on a courtesy phone/house phone/assistance intercom/etc.]."
If signage will be required, it should be allowed to "fit in" with other signage at the facility (e.g. avoid overly prescriptive requirements for background color, typeface, etc.)

Additionally, as FAA noted, "proposed § 74.66 would require the applicant to submit information describing existing unmanned aircraft traffic in close proximity to the facility, the facility's specific vulnerabilities to unmanned aircraft, what could happen if these vulnerabilities were exploited, and how the UAFR would provide relief. In addition, proposed § 74.64 would require the applicant to submit the facility's existing plan for addressing those vulnerabilities and the potential consequences if an unmanned aircraft were to exploit them."
Some facility owners may be worried about submitting information about security vulnerabilities due to concerns about potential disclosure if someone files a FOIA request, particularly if the facility is not a transportation facility and the documents might not fall within the definition of Sensitive Security Information (SSI) as provided in 49 CFR Part 1520. Will these documents be withheld/redacted from FOIA responses even if the documents do not fall within the legal definition of SSI?

Lastly, FAA proposed to "require that the requested UAFR boundary not exceed the operator or proprietor's property boundary". This does not fully address cases where public ways (e.g. roads) or portions thereof pass through, and are surrounded on both sides by, the fixed site facility. This part of the proposed rule should be revised to close this loophole - e.g. allow the UAFR to encompass public ways or portions thereof that pass through and are surrounded on both sides by the fixed site facility. Concerns about "how does someone know they're in the UAFR if they're not actually on the property" in such cases could be addressed by requiring signage (such as that discussed above), particularly where the public way is walkable (e.g. footpath, road with a sidewalk).

comments.commenter: Comment from University of Nebraska Medical Center
comments.comment_text:
As the FAA continues to evaluate the designation and restriction of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) operations near fixed-site facilities, it is important to strike a balance between safety, security, and the growing operational value of drones in both commercial and educational environments.

UAS technology has become a critical tool across multiple sectors. Universities use drones for research, mapping, and training. Hospitals and emergency services are increasingly exploring their use for rapid delivery and situational awareness. Private industry relies on UAS for inspection, data collection, and infrastructure support. Any regulatory framework must recognize that these operations are not fringe activities—they are now part of standard practice.

A key component supporting safe and compliant operations today is the FAA’s Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC) program. LAANC has significantly improved the ability for operators to request and receive near real-time authorization to operate in controlled airspace under Part 107 rules. It provides structure, accountability, and traceability for UAS activity in sensitive areas.

However, there is a gap that deserves attention.

At present, LAANC authorization does not consistently ensure that local Authorities Having Jurisdiction (AHJs)—such as law enforcement agencies, emergency management offices, hospitals, or critical infrastructure operators—are aware of approved drone operations occurring in proximity to their facilities. This can lead to confusion, unnecessary dispatches, or heightened concern when a legally authorized UAS is observed operating nearby.

To address this, the FAA should consider enhancements to the LAANC process that improve transparency at the local level. Specifically:

When a UAS operation is authorized through LAANC (or via waiver under Part 107), there should be a mechanism to notify relevant local stakeholders.
Local and state agencies should have the option to register within the system to receive notifications of authorized UAS activity within a defined proximity to their facilities or jurisdictions.
Notifications do not need to be operationally burdensome—simple alerts with basic flight parameters (time window, general location, operator authorization) would significantly improve situational awareness.

This approach would not restrict legitimate drone use, nor would it create unnecessary barriers for operators who are already complying with FAA regulations. Instead, it would strengthen coordination, reduce friction between operators and local agencies, and enhance overall safety.

FAA Part 107 provides a solid regulatory foundation, and LAANC has proven to be an effective tool for managing access to controlled airspace. The next step is ensuring that those responsible for public safety and critical infrastructure are not left out of the information loop when authorized operations occur nearby.

comments.commenter: Comment from Valero Oil Refinery Wilmington CA
comments.comment_text:
I am submitting this comment in support of the FAA’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking regarding the designation process to restrict unmanned aircraft operations in close proximity to fixed site facilities under Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016.

As a security professional working within the critical infrastructure sector, I strongly support the establishment of a formal process allowing qualifying facilities to petition for drone flight restrictions or no-fly designations around sensitive operational areas.

Critical infrastructure facilities, including refineries, marine terminals, chemical processing sites, and energy production facilities, face increasing operational and security concerns related to unmanned aircraft systems (UAS). The rapid growth in commercially available drone technology has significantly outpaced existing protective airspace measures available to facility operators and security personnel.

Many critical infrastructure locations contain:

hazardous chemical processes,
flammable or explosive materials,
restricted operational areas,
maritime transportation security operations,
and nationally significant energy infrastructure.

Unauthorized drone activity near these facilities presents potential risks including:

operational disruption,
industrial espionage,
cybersecurity reconnaissance,
safety hazards,
interference with emergency response operations,
and broader national security concerns.

Facilities operating under Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) requirements and Coast Guard oversight already maintain extensive physical security, access control, and emergency response programs. However, there remains a significant gap regarding practical airspace protection from low-altitude unmanned aircraft operations near sensitive infrastructure.

I encourage the FAA to ensure the Final Rule:

provides a clear and practical petition process for qualifying critical infrastructure owners and operators,
allows reasonable restricted airspace boundaries based on operational risk and facility footprint,
supports both permanent and temporary restriction options where justified,
incorporates coordination with federal, state, and local law enforcement partners,
and recognizes the unique security concerns associated with energy, refining, maritime, and chemical sectors.

I also encourage consideration for facilities that have documented drone activity, implemented drone detection technologies, or conducted coordinated drone response exercises as part of their overall security programs.

The ability to establish FAA-recognized drone restriction zones around qualifying critical infrastructure would greatly improve proactive risk mitigation, operational safety, and national security preparedness.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments on this important rulemaking effort.

Respectfully,

comments.commenter: Comment from Texas Biomedical Research Institute
comments.comment_text:
In the mid-1990s, Texas Biomedical Research Institute (Texas Biomed) set out to build a biosafety level 4 (BSL-4) lab, which represents the highest level in biocontainment and provides a safe space to study infectious diseases that can spread in the air and have no vaccines or cures. No one had done it before. Built a maximum containment laboratory, that is, outside of the federal government or the military in the United States.

Since 2000, Texas Biomed has been at the forefront of studying deadly pathogens and testing vaccines and treatments against them, including anthrax and Ebola virus. Today, the Institute is an internationally recognized partner in defending the nation and world from potential biological threats.

When the lab “went hot” in the spring of 2000, it was the first BSL-4 west of the Mississippi and only one – still – privately owned and operated by a nonprofit research institute in the U.S.

Over the past 25 years, Texas Biomed has been at the forefront of safely studying some of the world’s deadliest pathogens and developing vaccines and treatments to protect against them – all thanks to having the specialized facilities along with the scientific and engineering expertise to do so. Today, the government, industry and global organizations are partnering with Texas Biomed on national security, pandemic preparedness and the development of medical countermeasures.

“Texas Biomed is being increasingly recognized as an essential part of the nation’s defense infrastructure to prepare for future threats,” said President/CEO Larry Schlesinger, M.D. “As an independent nonprofit, we can pivot quickly to respond during a global health emergency. As we mark 25 years of accomplishments, we are looking to the future and how we meet the growing needs projected over the next 25 years and beyond.”

As a designated DHS Public Health Critical Infrastructure, Texas Biomed’s high containment research has led to several significant national and global partnerships for pandemic preparedness and led to vaccine discoveries and FDA approval. This partnership extends to the Department of Health and Human Services, the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA), the National Institutes of Health, the Department of Defense and the international Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) along with many commercial partners.

Each of these entail conducting highly regulated preclinical studies to prove a vaccine or medicine is safe and effective in animal models before proceeding to people. This is especially vital for lethal diseases where traditional clinical trials in people are impossible. For such pathogens, the FDA’s “Animal Rule” allows well-characterized animal models to be used in place of human efficacy trials to support regulatory approval of vaccines or therapies.

Biodefense is of critical importance to our country. These partnerships are evidence of the high quality, specialized work we can deliver. It is a testament to the excellent Texas Biomed team and their deep expertise – which includes everyone who works in the lab and outside the lab to design, document and deliver these groundbreaking studies.

The BSL-4 lab is not large. It can typically accommodate one study a month and has been booked solid for years. That is why Texas Biomed is working to expand its high containment facilities to serve growing demand in combatting national security threats, including bioterrorism and infectious diseases. The Institute has commenced construction of a new BSL4 lab with triple capacity, to come online in 2028.

For all these reasons stated, Texas Biomed requests the FAA approval to restrict drone operations over our critical infrastructure for the safety and security of our life saving infectious disease research and for the safety of our animal colonies. In doing so, Texas Biomed is researching and plans to deploy UAS remote ID and tracker technology for detection of unauthorized incursions. Thank you.

Sincerely,
Mark A. Hammargren, CPP
Director, Security and Emergency Preparedness
Texas Biomedical Research Institute

Please don’t go though with this, I have been a railroad photographer using drones for years. Legally and safely, this would kill unique aspects of photographer that capture the diverse landscapes and scenes of America and beyond that aren’t reachable on the ground. Trainmasters and MTO's do not follow the rules that the FAA has set forward when flying drones. Many times when drones are flown at night by a CSX trainmaster they turn the lights of the drone to prevent the drone being seen by the worker that is being observed. If the worker cannot see the drone then the operator cannot see the drone. Also trainmasters do not keep the drone in eye sight as required by FAA rules. During daylight drone operations many workers are paying more attention to the sky looking for a drone watching them than paying attention to the work at hand. Drone use is a big distraction and should be banned at all railroad locations including at industries as well as railroad yards. My name is Corey Spaulding I'm an employee of CSX transportation out of Selkirk, NY. I have been employed as a railroad Conductor for 23 years. Active rail yards are already dangerous enough requiring a railroad worker's undivided attention and situational awareness. Drones flying overhead and around the vicinity of rail yard operations is a distraction to railroad crews safely trying to perform their duties. Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns. Normalizing drones flying over thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals and around various equipment can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. drones used for the purpose of operational testing which has traditionally been conducted with fixed cameras or from a safe distance on the ground. Frontline rail workers are the true experts on rail safety and railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices become normalized in critical infrastructure environments.

There needs to be an exemption for Search and Rescue and disaster relief drone operations. There may not be time to submit paperwork or even gain verbal approval as a life might hang in the balance or significant harm to persons or property may pass.

Recommend that each site submits, as a minimum, a phone number that is manned by a person (not AI, not an answering machine) as part of the requirement to be a fixed drone flight restricted area.

Recommend that a fixed site has a designated person (by name or by title) that can authorize temporary drone flight restriction removal for Search and Rescue AND disaster relief efforts.

Such efforts will only be granted to those personnel who have a Part 107 license in good standing. Do not require more than this as SAR and disaster relief efforts are not always done by professional organizations and increasing requirements may delay search efforts.

I've been a railroader for 12 years, with about one third of my time spent switching cars in a rail yard on a daily basis. Rail yards, inherently, have a lot of moving parts that yardmen must keep in mind at all times throughout the day. Any increase to the number of things going on, at any given time, drastically increases the likelihood of something going seriously wrong. Whether it be multiple trains working at once or drones flying overhead, any change to the status quo not only increases the chances of a derailment but also drastically increases the chance that an employee suffers a catastrophic injury. Railroad managers love to say that they are used for safety, but that is far from the truth. They're used to observe crews who are performing their duties, because management refuses to walk around with crews and provide meaningful feedback in real time to the employees in the field, doing the work. They would rather sit in an office, tune in to another managers drone and wait for something serious to happen and then discipline the employee. We are constantly being told about security threats on the rails, but having no idea who is flying a drone and what their intentions are dramatically increases the chance that a potential threat turns into a catastrophe. Its been well documented that rail yards house hundreds of thousands of tons of hazardous material and the cars hauling such goods are placarded with the potential risk if something were to happen the car. This bullseye on the side of these cars, coupled with increasing national security concerns have disaster written all over them. Further, instead of utilizing the boots on the ground as the eyes in the field to identify and mitigate such risks, an employee is forced to assume the drone is being flown by an employee hoping to find a failure. Drones enable railroad management to do what they do best, sit back and say "I told you so" instead of "you should try to do it this way."

Amusement parks should not be considered critical infrastructure.

They are vulnerable to attacks by bad actors but they do not fit critical infrastructure designation nor are they intelligence facilities. Nuclear power plants are critical, Six Flags over Texas is not critical.

Instead amusement parks can and have, in the past, requested from and received a TFR from the FAA. (Disney Land in Florida for example).

Just because a water slide park gets built next to my house shouldn't mean I can longer access the national air space there nor be prohibited from taking off my drone from own back yard.

If I live next to a nuclear power plant then I should not fly my drone into their buildings but it is a nuclear power plant and that is a very understandable thing.

I’m a conductor for a class 1 railroad with 3 years of experience on the rails. I’ve been subject to the distractions that come from drone surveillance from management on the road and in yards. Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead for myself and fellow trainmen. As a frontline railroader, I know firsthand what true safety is as I live and breathe it everyday to insure I return in one piece to my family. Drones detract from safe operations. Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns for conductors and engineers alike. We work in an industry that could easily be subject to terror attacks and other malicious activities and we’re instructed by our carrier to be on the lookout for suspicious activity, devices, etc around railroad property and equipment. Since drones are widely available to the public, how am I or others supposed to differentiate between a company operated drone or a private drone while switching thousands of tons of hazardous materials and not wonder if I’m about to be attacked or if it’s a company officer watching my every move? It makes me nervous and on edge in the work place and that detracts from my ability to work safely. As a railroader, I deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments and we have not been. Drones are used strictly for punitive purposes and nothing else. Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can’t be allowed to happen.. we won’t know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests. There are cameras already installed in yards and inward and outward facing cameras on locomotives to observe employees and trespassers. Drones on the railroad are excessive, redundant, and a major distraction to railroaders just trying to do our jobs. Please help us remove drones from the railroad so we can focus on our jobs, and easily spot potential threats. Thank you.

Drones are not something you can get use to either when being tested, they will always grab your attention at some point versus someone standing afar watching you work or from a vehicle, they also pose other risks as well. The presence of an unauthorized drone distracts locomotive engineers and yard workers who are operating heavy tonnage equipment, creating fatal blind spots in an environment requiring absolute situational awareness. Finally, many rail yards transport hazardous materials, meaning a drone impact or a dropped battery sparks a direct risk of explosions or toxic chemical spills. Thanks for reading.

comments.commenter: Comment from Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District
comments.comment_text:
May 14, 2026

Docket Operations; U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001

Re: Docket number FAA-2026-4558

To whom it may concern:

I am writing on behalf of the Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District to voice the District's support for the proposed rule: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, Published Document 2026-08943 (91 FR 24650). The proposed action would, inter alia, implement section 2209, of the FAA Extension, Safety and Security Act of 2016, by establishing a process for operators and proprietors of certain fixed site facilities to request and maintain an unmanned aircraft flight restriction.

The District is a state-chartered government entity with a mission to protect public health, property, and the environment by providing wastewater conveyance, treatment, and flood management services within a certain territory. This territory includes virtually all the cities and villages in Milwaukee County, and all or part of nine municipalities in Ozaukee, Racine, Washington, and Waukesha counties. MMSD currently serves 29 municipalities, about 1.1 million people, and countless businesses.

The District has two wastewater treatment facilities, the Jones Island Water Reclamation Facility in the City of Milwaukee and the South Shore Water Reclamation Facility located in Oak Creek, Wisconsin. With a rise in security concerns emanating from home and abroad, the District takes all available measures to ensure the security of its treatment plants and headquarters facility. Moreover, the District has taken a keen interest in the threats posed by unmanned aircraft (drones). The District was at the forefront of recent legislative changes in Wisconsin (2025 Wis Act 194) to regulate the operation of drones over critical District facilities, as well as those critical assets of other utility providers (electric, water, gas, telecom, etc.).

The threat is not merely hypothetical. The District recently suffered a security breach when a drone of unknown origin crashed onto the Jones Island Water Reclamation Facility, which triggered a security response and ultimately led to Homeland Security taking possession of the crashed drone for forensic analysis. Incidents like this are at the forefront of the minds of District staff responsible for ensuring safe operation of our facilities and the safety of our personnel.

Because of the challenges posed by federal preemption of drone operation over US airspace, it can be confusing as to who is ultimately responsible for ensuring the safety of our staff and the more than one million residents who depend upon us to deliver service 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, from the grave risk posed by a malevolent drone operator. The District strongly supports the proposed rule as a key part of a comprehensive effort to ensure critical public utilities may take action to regulate drone use around those critical assets, and that those efforts will be supported both at the state and federal levels.

The technology is evolving faster than the law, and this proposal demonstrates a concerted effort to ensure critical utility providers can take all available measures to protect their assets from the risk of harm posed by unmanned aircraft.

Sincerely,

Joseph T. Ganzer
Senior Staff Attorney
Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District

comments.commenter: Comment from Corona Norco Unified School District
comments.comment_text: As a Director of Safety & Security in a large urban school district in Southern California, we often receive calls about drones flying over schools, students, and staff. We've also experienced nefarious drones dropping items onto the campuses of schools.  We would like the ability to limit drone/UAS flights over schools to approved activities.

Conductor/Engineer

Local Safety and Legislative Representative

I have eight years of service.

The flying of drones by management to observe and test crews has had a very negative impact on the safety and moral of crews while trying to perform their task. I ask that the FFA grounds these types of aggressive testing practices and let our crews get our critical work done with minimal distractions.

I am a freelance photographer who photographs the railroad both commercially and as a hobby. I am in the process of acquiring my Part 107 certification to allow myself to add drone photography to my commercial offerings. I am writing to give my opposition to the Designation—Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility in its current form.

Currently, as the rule is written, the railroad would be able to blanketly ban operations of small, personal drones in airspace over any of their property for hobby use. The drone operations of myself and my fellow hobbyists is not meant to interfere with railroad operations or trespass on railroad property and all FAA regulations are followed during flight operations. Myself and my fellow photographers pride ourselves on safe, respectful operations that aim to document the history of rail travel in this country while being respectful to the crews and railroad property.

Under the current proposal, railroads can request flight restrictions for unmanned aircraft over "fixed-sight facilities", and the FAA asks whether or not the restrictions should extend beyond the property lines. While that could make sense in other scenarios, the concern with implementing those restrictions over railroad property is the sheer length and direction that the tracks follow, often paralleling public roadways and areas where it is legal to watch the trains go by.

While the proposed rule includes provisions to allow operations, they focus mostly on commercial and government use and do not include provisions to protect the operations of hobbyists operating with small unmanned aircraft. Because hobbyists are not protected, the railroads would be given the ability to prevent drone operation not just in areas of security risk but as a blanket ban of aerial photography of railroad property and operations. If that were to happen, a hobby for many railfans across the country would be outlawed for seemingly no legitimate reason.

I urge the FAA to reject the rule as it is currently proposed and to restructure the rule to allow protection for those using small unmanned drones for railroad photography in areas that are not deemed as security threats and allow for the continued hobby for many across the country to continue in a safe, respectful and lawful manner. Thank you.

I work for a short-line railroad in the Port of Stockton and I have 4 years on the job. Like other ports in the nation, we are a MARSEC level 1 secure facility and not open to the public. There are specific rules regulating drone usage at the port. Specifically, the drone operator must be licensed and give 36 hour notice to the port police before flying. Today, as I am writing this, was the first day we saw a drone at the port. We knew who it was and we knew why they were flying the drone: it was our new superintendent watching us work. But nobody told us anyone had permission to fly today and our instinct was to notify our supervisors and the port police. When we notified our supervisors, they too were unaware of anyone using a drone, sanctioned or otherwise. We’re used to this sort of behavior from our superiors: keep us in the dark, obfuscate the truth and their intentions, and keep us guessing. We are professionals and we know how to deal with these circumstances in a productive and safe manner.

There are others at the port who are not as trained as we are. They aren’t railroaders. They are the other men and women who make the port work. They aren’t used to being surveilled and they take the security of the port very seriously.

While switching railcars for a specific customer today, we were working under the direction of an employee of that customer. I will call him Mike. Mike informed me that the drone “buzzed” his head and he was very concerned for not only his safety, but the operational security of the port as well. He found me and pointed to the drone. “Is that yours?” he asked and I replied in the affirmative. It was, again, the first time either one of us had seen the drone. When Mike told me about how close it got to him and expressed his concerns for safety, I implored him to notify his management and spare no detail.

After our safety briefing was done, Mike and I continued with our work and I watched the drone. I watched it go down to the ground level, stopping in front of personal vehicles and commercial vehicles, back up again, and down again. This not only distracted us, but distracted Mike and the other carriers trying to do their work as well (offloading material from railcars into tractor trailers), and in this environment, where we all look out for each other; where we collaborate safely to deliver product to our customers and keep these truck drivers (carriers) moving without delay, we all stopped—and looked at this drone bob up and down. It appeared to be piloted by an amateur.

How embarrassing this was for me and my team. Our management’s cavalier attitude towards the safety of working men and women in the port who aren’t railroaders is unacceptable. All our management team cares about is watching us. They have no regard for anyone else, like Mike, when they buzz right over the top of our heads and fly haphazardly all in some search for a rules violation.

I hereby swear the the above testimony is true to my knowledge and if you don’t believe me, I invite you to subpoena the drone footage from the Port of Stockton, filmed on today’s date, May 13, 2026 at around 11:30 am. Thank you.

As a railroad conductor with approximately 2 1/2 years of experience working in active rail yards, I strongly believe drones have no place operating over switching operations, train movements, and hazardous work environments without serious oversight and input from frontline railroad employees.

Rail yards are already high-risk environments that require constant communication, situational awareness, and concentration between crews on the ground, locomotive engineers, utility employees, and dispatching personnel. Adding drones overhead creates another layer of distraction and introduces unnecessary safety concerns during critical operations.

As a newer railroader, I believe this issue is especially important. Many new employees are already working through the pressures and anxieties that come with learning the craft in an environment where one mistake can have serious consequences. Due to the lack of proper training and the steep learning curve many new railroaders face, our focus needs to remain completely locked in on our surroundings, radio communication, train movements, and instructions from our crews. Drones operating overhead only add another distraction in an environment where our attention already has to be spot on at all times.

I have personally witnessed trainmasters and management personnel operating drones near active crews and switching operations. In many cases, these drones are used for surveillance, efficiency monitoring, or operational testing rather than true safety purposes. This creates tension, distraction, and uncertainty for crews already performing dangerous work around moving equipment, heavy machinery, and hazardous materials.

Railroad workers are the true rail safety experts because we are the individuals physically performing the work every day. Decisions involving drone operations in rail yards should not be normalized without direct consultation from the workers exposed to these risks.

There are also serious security concerns regarding drones flying over critical infrastructure and trains carrying hazardous chemicals. Workers on the ground should never have to question whether a drone overhead is being used for operational testing or represents a legitimate security threat. In today’s environment, normalizing unidentified drone activity around hazardous materials and transportation infrastructure is dangerous and irresponsible.

The FAA should carefully consider the concerns of railroad employees before allowing widespread drone use in active rail operations.

In solidarity,
Monica Jolene Thompson

Re: 14 CFR Parts 1, 74, 91, and 107
[Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03]
RIN 2120-AL33

To Whom It May Concern:

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the authorization of drone activities within the physical confines of the United States transportation infrastructure. As a professional in the industry with 29 years of service, I urge regulatory bodies to evaluate this issue through the lens of national security rather than corporate convenience or cost reduction. The risks of drone deployment in transport environments outweigh the perceived operational benefits for three critical reasons:

1. Vulnerability to Bad Actors: Global conflicts demonstrate that drones are easily weaponized by nefarious groups. Concurrently, our physical and digital transportation infrastructure faces ongoing threats from sophisticated cyber actors. Introducing routine drone usage creates an optimal environment for adversaries to exploit.

2. Employee Confusion and Security Breaches: Workforce personnel cannot reliably distinguish between authorized commercial drones and unauthorized surveillance or weaponized devices. Even if employees are notified of scheduled drone operations, they may misidentify a hostile drone as an official asset. This creates a dangerous security blind spot.

3. Compromising Supply-Line Integrity: Protecting critical supply lines requires physical oversight. Replacing human vigilance with automated aerial systems undercuts our defense posture. Supply-line disruption is the primary objective of hostile forces, and we must not facilitate this risk in the name of labor reduction.

Regulatory oversight must look beyond immediate corporate cost savings. This is a matter of national security and critical infrastructure protection. I wholly urge the government to maintain strict prohibitions on drone operations across all transportation routes, facilities, and yards.

Sincerely,

David Liddle
Regional Insurance Manager
SMART-TD / AJFL

Workers should be judged on actual job performance and established procedures — not by being watched from the air like they are under suspicion.

If drones are used at all near railroad operations, their use should be limited strictly to legitimate operational safety needs, emergency response, or infrastructure inspection — not employee surveillance or testing.

These questions are:

Are you a Part 107 pilot?
14 CFR § 107.12 Requirement for a remote pilot certificate with a small UAS rating.

Who is your visual observer?
14 CFR § 107.33 Visual observer

What is your drone registration number?
14 CFR § 107.13 Registration

Are you complying with Remote ID?
14 CFR § 89.105 Remote identification requirement
14 CFR § 89.110 Operation of standard remote identification unmanned aircraft.

Does your drone’s transmitter have an FCC Part 15 Certification? If
not, what is your HAM license FCC Registration Number (FRN)?
47 CFR Part 15 Radio Frequency Devices
47 CFR Part 97 Amatuer Radio Service

Did you perform a preflight inspection and checklist?
14 CFR § 107.15 Condition for safe operation.

Why was I not informed that I was participating in a drone operation?
14 CFR § 107.49 Preflight familiarization, inspection, and actions for aircraft operation.

Does your drone comply with Category 1, 2, 3, or 4 Operations? If not,
then you can’t fly that drone over me.
14 CFR § 107.39 Operation over human beings

What airspace were you flying in? If any airspace other than Class G,
did you get authority through LAANC?
14 CFR § 107.41 Operation in certain airspace
14 CFR § 107.43 Operation in the vicinity of airports.
14 CFR § 107.45 Operation in prohibited or restricted areas
14 CFR § 107.47 Flight restrictions in the proximity of certain areas designated by notice
to airmen.

During night operations: Does the drone have an anti-collision light
visible for at least 3 statute miles?
14 CFR § 107.29 Operation at night.

Are you flying below 400 feet above the ground level ceiling? Are you
flying 500 feet below the clouds and 2000 feet horizontally from the
clouds?
14 § 107.51 Operating limitations for small unmanned aircraft.

Are you flying with a waiver?
14 CFR § 107.205 List of regulations subject to waiver.
