Employees in the North Little Rock classification yard are raising serious concerns about the use of drones during management testing operations. In a fast-paced hump yard environment where rail cars roll free and switching movements happen constantly, employees must remain fully focused on their surroundings at all times.

The presence of drones flying overhead creates an unnecessary distraction during critical switching operations. Workers are forced to divide their attention between moving equipment, radio communication, and an unidentified aircraft operating above them. In an environment where one missed movement or momentary distraction can result in severe injury or death, this creates a legitimate safety hazard.

Beyond distraction concerns, employees also have no immediate way of knowing who is operating the drone. In today’s security climate, workers should not have to question whether an airborne device over critical rail infrastructure is being operated by management, an outside individual, or someone intending harm. Rail yards are part of the nation’s critical infrastructure, and unauthorized or poorly communicated drone activity can create confusion, fear, and unsafe working conditions.

North Little Rock is an active classification hump yard requiring constant situational awareness. Employees already work around rolling cars, heavy machinery, close clearances, and live tracks. Adding drones into that environment increases cognitive overload and takes workers’ attention away from the hazards that matter most. One distraction during a shove, cut, or switching move could be deadly.

Safety programs should reduce risk — not introduce new distractions into an already dangerous workplace.

In the railroad workplace, so many stationary and moving hazards already exist. The workforce and carriers should always be looking for ways to reduce hazards and the use of UAS will not aid in doing so.

There is also no benefit of another layer of automation to separate constructive interaction between employees and supervisors. Reduction of workforce through technology has psychological implications that reach far beyond the workplace affecting work and home life.

I urge carriers and lawmakers to avoid allowing UAS for any activities of employee monitoring. The mere sound of a drone a considerable deterrent. There is no legitimate reason to permit monitoring of employees performing their duties with UAS.

comments.commenter: Comment from SMART-TD Louisiana State Safety and Legislative Board
comments.organization: SMART-TD Louisiana State Safety and Legislative Board
comments.comment_text:
To Whom It May Concern,

I am submitting this comment in support of stronger restrictions on drone operations around railroad facilities and active rail operations.

I have worked as a railroad conductor for 24 years and currently serve as the Louisiana Safety and Legislative Director for SMART-TD. I am also a member of the SMART-TD National Safety Team. My experience has provided firsthand knowledge of the dangers and operational challenges that exist inside active rail yards and around moving trains.

Rail operations already involve constant safety risks that require complete focus, communication, and situational awareness from train crews and other employees. The growing use of drones by railroad management inside active yards has created additional distractions and unnecessary hazards in environments where safety must remain the top priority.

Railroad workers are the true rail safety experts because we are the ones working on the ground every day. Many employees have witnessed drones being flown during switching operations, near moving equipment, over active crews, and around hazardous materials. These operations create safety concerns, interfere with concentration, and normalize unnecessary surveillance activities in dangerous environments.

There are also serious security concerns associated with allowing routine drone activity over critical rail infrastructure and trains transporting hazardous chemicals. Railroad workers should not have to wonder whether a drone overhead is being operated for a company test, surveillance activity, or something more nefarious.

South Louisiana contains one of the largest concentrations of chemical plants, refineries, and hazardous material facilities in the United States. Railroads serving these facilities routinely transport chlorine, ammonia, petroleum products, liquefied gases, and other hazardous materials through densely populated communities and critical industrial corridors. From a homeland security standpoint, allowing routine drone activity around these facilities and rail operations creates additional vulnerabilities that cannot be ignored.

Given the current instability and ongoing conflict involving Iran and increased global concerns regarding terrorism, infrastructure security, and the use of drone technology in modern warfare, now is not the time to normalize unidentified or unnecessary drone activity over critical American transportation infrastructure. Recent global events have highlighted how drones can be used for surveillance, disruption, and attacks against infrastructure targets. Railroad employees transporting hazardous materials and operating inside critical infrastructure environments should not be placed in situations where distinguishing between a legitimate operation and a potential threat becomes difficult.

Frontline railroad employees should be consulted before these practices become accepted throughout the industry. Safety decisions affecting rail operations should include the workers who understand the real-world conditions and risks involved.

I appreciate the FAA taking these concerns seriously and encourage the agency to adopt strong protections limiting drone operations around active railroad facilities and transportation infrastructure.

Respectfully,


Chris Christianson
24-Year Railroad Conductor
Louisiana Safety and Legislative Director, SMART-TD

I’m a 28 year veteran of the freight railroad industry. Over the years I have been subject to hundreds of tests by railroad officials and DOT officials along with FRA inspectors. It has become a fact of life that all railroad employees go to work each day under the pressure of these heavy handed tests. But now we are subjected to aerial testing as well which is very unnerving and distracting. With all the mayhem and terror threats in the world and in particular the railroad industry it creates a major issue for employees working on or near railroad properties. Having been subjected to this drone testing once already I can say that now I feel as if I’m being watched at all times and it is very unnerving and distracting. This goes against what the railroad industry is supposedly teaching us about distractions. They are creating life and death distractions by using the drone technology. How do I know if it is rail related or some foreign or domestic terror threat. My feelings on this matter are not just mine. Every railroader I know feels the same way. It’s time for the government to put an end to this potentially devastating practice.

I have been a freight train conductor for over 15 years. I am absolutely in favor of the FAA restricting the use of unmanned aircraft in rail yards and other facilities. They do nothing to improve safety. In fact, they would have the opposite effect. Because if we normalize drone activity in these locations, we won’t know when we are potentially seeing an act of terrorism in progress.
I think its very unsafe to use them anywere were your attention can be taken from your tasks at hand its a major safety concern and union pacific dose not think safety is a priority

I’ve worked for the railroad going on two years and the drones and testing have been the only dangerous thing I’ve been a part of it’s put me in uncertain circumstances not knowing if it was a terrorist attack and throwing me off my plan moves and distracted me from carrying out my job numerous times

I am a 19 yr UP employee, con/switchman, I work in Denison TX  where drone Testing is heavy. I feel like it is a huge distraction. The yard is a RCL yard with cameras, the need for drones is to no benefit to employees or the  carrier, except for the sneak attack testing strategy. I've had these things flown overhead close enough that you could hear the thing buzzing, huge distraction while handling hazmat and tonnage that require your full attention.

In the past 20 years I've been a professional railroader, I have watched the railroad industry trade knowledge, skill and proactive managerial engagement for inexperienced office workers watching us with field cameras and drones. The drones are especially invasive and unsafe. These drones are not being used to increase safety on the railroad in any way, they are simply being used a bludgeoning device to further keep the professionals doing the work constantly looking over their shoulders for someone or something watching. We are required to take training about railroad safety, that anyone anywhere, anytime can be a threat to the railroad. The US Government announced "credible threats" of drone strikes by foreign entities. How are we not supposed to think the worst when we pair that information with the railroad flying drones unannounced above us? There is a responsible way for the railroad to engage with their employees to foster a greater understanding of rules and to correct unsafe behavior, flying drones is not the answer.

Public Comment Regarding the Use of Drones for Employee Surveillance

The increasing use of drones by Union Pacific to monitor and test employees raises serious concerns regarding safety, fairness, workplace culture, and accountability.

Railroad operations are already high-risk environments that require constant awareness, communication, and trust between employees and management. Replacing direct, personal leadership with remote drone surveillance creates a disconnected management style that focuses more on catching employees in isolated moments rather than actively participating in safety and problem-solving alongside the workforce.

A drone hovering overhead cannot fully capture all variables surrounding an event or work activity. Video footage from a distance often lacks context, depth perception, environmental conditions, communication between crew members, and other operational factors that are critical to accurately understanding what actually occurred. A single snapshot in time should never outweigh the complete circumstances surrounding railroad operations.

Additionally, drones themselves create distractions in already dangerous work environments. Employees should be focused on train movements, heavy equipment, radio communication, and surrounding hazards — not wondering why an unidentified drone is hovering above them. In today’s environment, where concerns regarding unauthorized drone activity and security threats are real, employees should not be forced to question whether they are being observed by management, private individuals, or potentially hostile actors.

There is also growing concern that drone surveillance is being used less as a legitimate safety tool and more as a form of “sport hunting” for rule violations. True safety culture is built through mentorship, communication, coaching, and visible leadership — not through hidden observation intended to capture failures after the fact. Employees are far more likely to respond positively to managers who engage directly with crews, identify hazards in real time, and help prevent incidents before they occur.

If management observes unsafe behavior through drone technology, immediate intervention should be the priority — not delayed discipline based on remote footage. Safety should never become secondary to building disciplinary cases.

Technology can assist railroad operations when used responsibly, but it must not replace human leadership, fairness, due process, and genuine safety engagement with employees on the ground.

Railroad workers deserve a workplace culture focused on protection, communication, and prevention — not surveillance-driven intimidation.

Drone usage around railroad yards by managers that don’t care about the safety of the employees by being able to fly a distraction in the air and take the attention of employees already trying to keep their mind on task with radio and other distractions don’t need a flying distraction in the air.

I’ve been railroading for 25 plus years. All we hear from the company is we have to be focused on our work in order to do our job safely. I believe that allowing them to use drones for testing purposes is a distraction in itself, worrying that if we take one misstep or if in there interpretation of a rule could lead to time off. We have enough on our plate without drones being a distraction.

Rail yards are already dangerous enough without us having to worry about drones flying above. I am a conductor for Union Pacific railroad and have been here for 23 years the pressure they put on us is at an all time high without using drones. The drones just make it much worse.
The use of drones by the railroad is very distracting. It’s  different that being watched by cameras. The drones  make it difficult to stay focused on your tasks.

Drones are an added distraction to an already over exasperated testing and monitoring system developed by the railroad industry with only one goal! To harass and fire transportation workers into their so called rule compliance. The rail industry only cares about profits and profit losses! Safety is only an obstacle of the two. For 23 years I have constantly been watched and distracted from performing my job in peace. There are cameras covering every square inch of most receiving yards and terminals with one goal in mind, to distract and discipline employees! Drones now take our focus away from the ground task at hand and now have us looking in the skies to stay as compliant as a human being can. Please stop the rail industries harassment on working families. Best regards.  B.J Sanchez

I support this rule, drones are only a distraction and it we look at them during a shove we can be fired, but if we don't look we wouldn't know if it's a terrorist act. We already hav3 enough cameras and managers watching us in person or on cameras, this is just another distraction.

comments.commenter: Comment from Santa Clarita Valley Water Agency
comments.comment_text:
May 14, 2026
Federal Aviation Administration
Docket Operations
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
RE: Comments in Support of NPRM – Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
To Whom It May Concern:
On behalf of Santa Clarita Valley Water Agency, I submit these comments in support of the
Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking titled “Designation –
Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility,”
published on May 6, 2026.
Santa Clarita Valley Water Agency strongly supports the FAA’s implementation of Section
2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 and respectfully urges the FAA
to ensure explicit inclusion of water and wastewater infrastructure within the final rule and
associated eligibility framework.
Water and wastewater systems are federally recognized critical infrastructure. Under 42
U.S.C. § 5195c(e), “critical infrastructure” includes systems and assets so vital to the
United States that their incapacity or destruction would have a debilitating effect on
national public health or safety. Presidential Policy Directive 21 (PPD-21) and National
Security Memorandum-22 further identify the Water and Wastewater Systems Sector as
one of the Nation’s designated critical infrastructure sectors.
Public water systems and wastewater agencies provide essential services necessary to
protect public health, fire protection, sanitation, environmental compliance, economic
stability, and emergency response operations. Unauthorized unmanned aircraft activity
near these facilities presents legitimate and increasing security concerns, including:
• Surveillance and reconnaissance of sensitive infrastructure
• Identification of operational, physical, or cybersecurity vulnerabilities
• Interference with utility operations and emergency response activities
• Risks associated with intentional contamination or sabotage
• Threats to SCADA, communications, and operational control systems

• Public safety risks associated with unauthorized drone operations over critical
facilities and infrastructure
Water and wastewater agencies frequently operate geographically dispersed infrastructure
with limited physical security buffers, making these systems uniquely vulnerable to
unauthorized aerial surveillance and intrusion.
Accordingly, Santa Clarita Valley Water Agency respectfully encourages the FAA to ensure
the final rule:
1. Explicitly recognizes water and wastewater systems as eligible critical infrastructure
facilities under the UAFR framework;
2. Includes a broad interpretation of covered water infrastructure, including treatment
plants, reservoirs, dams, pump stations, recharge facilities, operational control facilities,
and other essential utility infrastructure;
3. Avoids overly narrow eligibility criteria that could exclude local or regional public water
and wastewater systems whose disruption would significantly impact public health and
safety;
4. Recognizes the homeland security and public health implications associated with
unauthorized unmanned aircraft operations near drinking water and wastewater
infrastructure; and
5. Establishes an efficient and practical application process for public agencies
responsible for protecting essential infrastructure.
The proposed rule appropriately balances the continued integration of unmanned aircraft
systems into the National Airspace System with the need to protect critical infrastructure
and public safety. Providing water and wastewater agencies with access to the UAFR
process would strengthen infrastructure resilience and support national security, public
health, and emergency preparedness objectives.
Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments on this important rulemaking.
Respectfully submitted,
Cris Perez
Director of Technology Services

This needs to happen the pros of restriction greatly out weight the easement the corporations use it for to club the baby seals. The risk of these operations in a secure rail yard hold hazardous materials without being able to identify or have normalcy to operations of a remote observation equipment could be used by terriost or other enemies of our nation. This is a basic requirement to protect our country from the foreign and possible domestic threats that we are called upon as citizens to do. Please consider any operational use these have provided has been done without this equipment previously and the risk to reward is just not there. Thank you

Speaking from experience, drones are very distracting and pose and dangerous situation with these devices hovering over myself and my crew. This use by the railroads is specifically only to catch crews doing something wrong. It is not for safety purposes. The real safety issue is the drones being a distracting source. There is more yo say but I am willing to answer any questions that may arise.

My name is Gabe Kelley. I have been a conductor with Union Pacific for 2 years. My job requires a great deal of concentration and accuracy when I’m on the ground directing the movement of thousands of tons of heavy equipment. One wrong move could cost me my life. Being distracted by a drone or a camera could cause me to loose concentration for a split second and end in serious or fatal injury. The elimination of drones is a start to a less distracted work place and a safer work environment.
Thank you for your time.
I believe drones are a distraction when working on railroad

Drones are a distraction to the work environment. They could also pose a danger to the employees if it malfunctions and goes down, or are improperly operated. If the employees know about the drone test during switching or yard operations, the focus isn't the work, its the drone.

I’ve worked as a conductor for over 19 years. I have seen managers use drones flying where they cannot actually see the drone. One day I was protecting a shove in the yard and the manager flew the drone down approximately 15 feet from my face just to say hello. He didn’t mean any harm but it distracted my attention from watching the shove in a yard where we were having to relay for one another and is dangerous in itself. There’s enough noise and others that fly drones around we don’t need our local management to fly drones around. We have worked for many years without them and I think someone could seriously get injured or worse killed because of misuse of drones.

Im a conductor/engineer and now a yardmaster i have and still do deal with the unsafe practice of the drone. Before becoming yardmaster I was always worried about what flying over me and not about the safety of the true risk in the yard switching. I was worried about losing my job due to a drone flying over head because I didnt look both ways long enough before crossing the tracks.

Everytime I think I seen something flying over head or thought I heard something I would lose focus on the job at hand and be hyper focused on where that drone is.

Now as a yardmaster I see the management getting excited to run out fly that drone let charge someone im going to get my failure rate up today always what you hear or them talking about how they can be half a mile away and see the conductor tieing the hand brake and he didnt have 3 points of contact.

Sure there are good reasons to have the drone but to have one flying over your crews while working and cause extra worry when they should focus on the task at hand is not one of them.

I am strongly opposed to drones being used for testing in ANY area of railroad property. All this will do is open the door for anyone, especially terrorists, who will be excited about this happening. This will only create an opportunity to compromise our safety as employees, with potential to be involved in a deadly catastrophe. I have 31 years of railroad experience, and have never felt an urgency to address a safety situation, such as I do with this one. We have no idea who is operating drones over our heads, that alone should cause concern, and put a ban for use of drones over every railroad property, and make it a felony offense if caught.

There is no reason to fly distractions over our heads when we’re trying to stay focused on our jobs. It takes only a split second of distraction to permanently hurt someone, or yourself. It’s been so many instances where these drones and been a problem in our work environment, not to mention how they make you second guess your own day to day decisions. Normally we would just safely do our jobs and if there is a drone around that means your being tested.

20 yrs. Service with class 1 RR, entire career working a yard switching job mostly as a RCO, yard trainmen have enough moving parts to concentrate on in their daily job task and dont the the distraction of drones flying overhead and creating what is already a precarious job environment even more dangerous!

I am a 20 year plus employee for Union Pacific. The use of drones has every employee so nervous, they are more worried about if they are being watched by one, then whatever task they are actually doing. It's extremely nerve wracking to watch an employee have their eyes on the sky, instead of being in control of a cut of cars, throwing a switch,  making a hook onto another car, or watching what is on front of them when they are making a shove move. It's more then just nerve wracking, it flat out makes my job more dangerous than it already is. Thank you

Dear FAA, I'm writing to you to let you know the importance of the proposed rule that could significantly restrict drone operations around transportation facilities, including rail infrastructure. I'm Glenn Duncan 21-year conductor/engineer That active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
That frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.
That railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments. That normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests. Also I've personally seen MTO's violate the already in place FAA drone guidelines when they fly their drones near airports in Evansville, Indiana and Avon, Indiana. When questioning the MTO's about this they acted as if they aren't aware of nearby airports. Which we know are not true since these busy airports have jets flying in and out constantly. Other situations where drones put crews at risk is where crews notice the drones and try to work with the distraction of the drone but it's hard to stay focused with a drone hovering overhead. MTO's like to distract conductors and put pressure on them to make a mistake. They act as if it's for sport and not safety. I've heard MTO's say that drones sometimes lose signal and go haywire and have lost drones due to this. Thankfully nobody was injured due to this to my knowledge.

It’s absolutely ridiculous the managers will go to any length to fire railroad workers to benefit themselves with promotions and bonuses all while pretending to be concerned with our safety. I truly believe the fact just knowing that they are out there  hiding like cowards and flying those drones is extremely distracting in so many ways and effects the mental capacity of the railroad worker trying to do his job as a human being not a robot like they treat us . Knowing that it is possible that they are out there with drones makes me feel unsafe performing my job duties.

I am a 23 year veteran of American rail operations. I worked as a conductor and an engineer and have watched my current conductors being distracted by some company official flying his drone around the train yard cataloging the conductors every move. There are already stationary cameras all over the train yards why would officials need to fly drones 20 ft. over the head of an employee walking around the yard, trying to do his/her job safely while hearing a drone swooping in and out watching their every move just hoping for a misstep so they can get a failure for the day.

This practice is ridiculous, hazardous and causing self doubt from employees just trying to do their job. This needs to end.

I am submitting this comment as an aviation student and future pilot who has vested interests in how the national airspace is managed. I appreciate the FAA's effort to finally implement Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety and Security Act of 2016, and I recognize the importance of balancing airspace security with the continued growth of the unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) industry.
That said, I have two concerns. First, with approximately 125,000 facilities potentially qualifying for restrictions across 16 sectors, the cumulative impact on low-altitude airspace could be significant for commercial operators and delivery services. Second, sector-specific criteria for several facility types remain undefined. The FAA should finalize criteria for all 16 sectors before issuing a final rule to avoid an unpredictable regulatory environment.
Overall, I support this rule making, but urge the FAA to ensure all active UAFRs are integrated into real-time airspace tools like LAANC, and that eligibility criteria are complete and clearly defined before the rule takes effect.

Bottom line, to distracting and dangerous to be in the environment for which we work in. Their testing has gotten ridiculous. You can’t concentrate on the job/task at hand for fearing for your job today or tomorrow. We are professionals, should be treated as such.

My thing about drones, is that they are a serious hazard. With everything going on with the world right now, I could easily think we could be under attack. Also if I hear a buzzing noise above me I look around to see what it is and that distraction takes my attention away from my duty at hand. That includes shove movements, switch lining and pretty much anything in a rail yard. They are unsafe and a distraction for anyone and everyone working in a rail yard. People have eyes and cameras, if that’s not enough, I don’t know what is.

When we as conductors are switching or building trains, we have enough to worry about without a drone buzzing overhead.  We know they are watching and for younger conductors their minds go to rules regulations and am I doing things right and by the book.  This creates an unsafe work environment where they are focused on other things and not the task at hand.   Thusly creating an unsafe workplace and in an environment where a single slip could end your life or someone else’s life.  Drones create unsafe environments and conditions to work in.   Our managers already watch us via the camera systems like lions on a herd of sheep for the slightest infraction. I was told when I hired out that the railroad hires you then spends the rest of your time with the railroad looking for ways to fire you. I genuinely believe that now.  This is a great job but can be managed way better and more efficiently across the board.

To whom it may concern,

Im a 20yr conductor/engineer with a class 1 railroad. Looking back thru the yrs railroad management has always been the dirtiest of the dirty when it comes to T&E employees. From hiding in the bushes and weeds to flying these drones to now they have AI on the engines recording every move. But to these drones, this practice of hiding around the block and flying their drones to try and catch a conductor doing something wrong is one of the most dangerous things that a manager can do. Being pressured by the company and and glorifying the more failures the better, just shows exactly where these executives priorities are at. They preach safety everyday but their actions prove false. Its hard enough for a conductor to do his/her job but add in the added stress and distractions of these drones buzzing over head, it sets up a disastrous senerio. Case in point a couple of the fatalities that a certain class 1 had on new hires riding shoves. New employees unsure of themselves and then add in these managers flying drones over them, certainly took their concentration off the task at hand! Oh, they're not gonna say that, but that is exactly what happened. Im sure I speak for most when I say, its damn dangerous flying these drones on us. It takes ones concentration away where all your worried about is not getting fired from them watching you with their drones. Ill finish with this, its a bad recipe for getting employees KILLED over these managers trying to get their O-test failures!! For railroads the talk safety everyday but rewards their mangers for more o-test failures, just shows you exactly what's important to them. Has nothing to do with being safe and everything to do with liability.
This is a very dangerous situation

I am really tired of always looking around the sky above to see if I am being observed while I am trying to do my job and have more things on my mind than the critical parts of my job such as drones flying around. This makes my job more dangerous than it already is

I am submitting this comment as an individual railroad employee with many years of experience in active rail operations. I am also a licensed private pilot, licensed drone operator, and a licensed Class 1 locomotive engineer. Because of that background, I understand both railroad operating conditions and the rules and responsibilities that come with operating unmanned aircraft.

I support the FAA proposal to create enforceable drone restriction zones around critical infrastructure and sensitive facilities. I believe railroad operations and railroad infrastructure should absolutely be included in those protections.

Modern railroads are not just trains moving freight. Rail operations involve hazardous materials, military shipments, fuel, major supply chain traffic, bridges, tunnels, passenger operations, and infrastructure that is critical to the country’s economy and national security.

From the operating side, drones near active railroad operations create real concerns. Train crews already operate in an environment that requires constant concentration and situational awareness. Engineers and conductors are monitoring signals, radio traffic, crossings, track conditions, equipment, and surrounding activity at all times. Unlike a car, a train cannot simply move out of the way or stop quickly if something unexpected happens.

Low flying drones near moving trains, switching operations, or railroad facilities create distraction and uncertainty during safety-sensitive operations. Even if a drone operator believes they are operating safely, the crew on the ground has no way of immediately knowing the intent of the operation or whether the drone is malfunctioning, improperly operated, or being used for something more serious.

I also believe restrictions should apply broadly to unnecessary drone operations around active rail operations, including the use of drones for routine productivity monitoring or disciplinary observation of operating crews. I am not opposed to legitimate uses such as bridge inspection, maintenance planning, accident investigation, or emergency response. Those can be valuable tools when properly controlled. However, routine airborne monitoring around active crews creates unnecessary distraction and adds another layer of operational stress in an environment where focus and attention already matter greatly.

There is also a legitimate national security concern. Railroads remain critical infrastructure and are part of the nation’s transportation and defense network. Drones can be used for surveillance, mapping, interference, or other malicious purposes by individuals, organized criminals, or foreign adversaries looking to target critical infrastructure.

In addition, the potential consequences of a successful attack involving rail hazardous materials are enormous. A single hazardous materials release in or near a populated area could place tens of thousands of people at risk. Modern unmanned aircraft systems create new possibilities for hostile actors to surveil, coordinate, or potentially facilitate attacks against hazardous material shipments and critical transportation infrastructure. The combination of UAV technology and vulnerable hazmat transportation corridors should be treated as a serious public safety and homeland security concern.

I believe the FAA is moving in the right direction with this proposal and I encourage the agency to provide strong and enforceable protections around railroad facilities and active rail operations.

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to raise the serious safety concern regarding the use of unmanned aircraft systems, commonly referred to as drones, by railroad management to monitor employees and initiate disciplinary referrals against train and engine service employees working in active rail yards.

Railroad operations, whether conducted within a yard or over the line of road, are inherently safety-sensitive. Trainmen are required to maintain constant situational awareness while working around moving equipment, switches, shove movements, close clearances, radio communications, and other employees on the ground. Any unnecessary distraction introduced into that environment creates an unacceptable risk.

The use of drones flying above or near employees actively engaged in switching operations is not merely intrusive; it is dangerous. These aircraft create visual and auditory distractions in an environment where a momentary loss of focus can result in serious injury or death. Employees naturally look toward, react to, or become aware of drones operating overhead, and that distraction can interfere with their ability to safely perform duties that already demand undivided attention.

This practice is especially needless because railroads already maintain extensive fixed-camera surveillance throughout railyard facilities. The use of drones in addition to existing camera systems is absolutely unnecessary for legitimate operational oversight and is solely designed to generate disciplinary referrals. Using aerial surveillance in this manner introduces a new hazard into an already hazardous workplace without any corresponding safety benefit.

In short, the deployment of drones over active rail operations for employee surveillance and discipline is an unnecessary and unsafe practice. It creates foreseeable distractions that can contribute to operational errors with potentially fatal consequences. I respectfully request that the FAA deny, through regulation, this practice as it diverts employee attention in a setting where even a brief distraction can result in catastrophic injury or death.

We are seeing more and more drones in the trains pathways getting near miss from impact. Some of these drones are huge in nature.

To put it in real world explanation. Imagine driving you car around a curve at 70mph and there is a drone as wide as you car hovering in front of you at your windshield level.

Putting a train in an emergency braking action, could very well derail the train. It is not just the train crews life that is at risk. The entire community around the railroad could be efected by release of hazardous materials.

Distractions on and about rail facilities such as cell phone use has been banned for over ten years. That's because distractions near the rails can be deadly. With safety as a number one priority, stopping drone use except in emergency and recovery operations should be banned completely.

I’m a TYE employee of over 12 years experience with not only being a road conductor, but that of a yard foreman as well. I have a serious issue with drone testing and monitoring of not only the employees, but that of the yards as well. If the FAA allows drones to continue to fly over our railyards it could normalize drone flying over hazmat filled rail infrastructure. We would not know who owns that drone, whether it’s BNSF or a terrorist organization. How could we know and how would we be able to act in time if drones are allowed to freely fly over our yards? This could lead to a national security failure and puts me at serious risk.
Working in the rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead and putting me at a safety risk. Drone testing used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns. My fellow railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments, and that normalizing drones flying over thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress or an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests. I will not fail nor will not falter with this new type of testing, but I do have a problem with it for it compromises my job and the way I do it safely.

I am an engineer with 16 years of service as well as local chairperson/union representative . I was recently working the Redding, CA yard, a yard where we have a lot of diesel fuel, propane fuel and other hazmat cars. While working my conductor reported to myself and on-duty manager that a drone was harassing her and it was not the first time. I witnessed myself this drone flying low over her head, within feet. The drone then flew within feet of rail cars as we were actively switching/moving the cars around. Even after reporting to on-duty and on-site manager witnessing the drone movements himself and with the report announced over the active radio channel that local and railroad law enforcement had been notified, the drone and its operator continued to "low buzz" both the conductor and the train/cars. It got to the point both myself and the conductor felt it was necessary for her safety and safety of the train/car switching movement to completely stop all movements for a period of time. We consulted with the manager and stopped all movements for around 10-15 minutes. All the time the drone continued to "low buzz/fly" the conductor (on the ground), the cars, and myself while standing on the platforms of the locomotive.

This drone and this operator had done this too the conductor (whom I represent as a union representative/local chairperson) and NUMEROUS occasions prior to my working with her to see it for myself.

My name is AJ Lewis, and I have been a railroad switchman and locomotive engineer for 12 years. I present my argument for not allowing drones to be flown over railyards and to consider these facilities restricted to unmanned aircraft.

Before the FAA considers normalizing drone activity in the airspace directly above railroad operations, I urge you to fully appreciate the operating environment your rulemaking would affect. Classification yards and active terminals involve simultaneous movement of thousands of tons of rolling stock. Workers on the ground operate in environments with limited sightlines, extreme noise levels, constantly moving equipment, and hazardous track conditions. Federal railroad safety regulations exist precisely because this industry demands unwavering situational awareness from every person on property.
A drone overhead is not a neutral presence in that environment. It is a distraction — an auditory and visual intrusion into the focused attention that railroad workers must maintain to protect their own lives and the lives of their colleagues. FRA data consistently shows that human factors, including distraction and failure to maintain situational awareness, are among the leading causes of railroad accidents and on-the-job fatalities. Introducing drone traffic into the airspace above active yards adds an unnecessary and foreseeable layer of risk to work that already demands everything a worker has.

Regulatory agencies and railroad carriers are quick to invoke the language of safety culture. But safety culture means nothing if the people doing the most dangerous work are not consulted before their operating environment is fundamentally altered. Drone operations over active yards are already being deployed — framed as operational testing, efficiency studies, and surveillance programs — without any formal process to assess the impact on worker safety.
The FAA should require that any rulemaking touching airspace over railroad terminals include formal consultation with labor organizations representing the workers who live and work beneath those flight paths. Management's perspective on the utility of drone technology does not represent the full safety picture. It never has. The workers on the ground — the engineers, conductors, and carmen who walk those tracks every day — are the experts the FAA needs to hear from before these practices are normalized.

Railroad terminals routinely handle tens of thousands of gallons of hazardous materials — chlorine, anhydrous ammonia, ethanol, crude oil, and other substances that, if released or ignited, could kill workers, first responders, and entire surrounding communities. These are not hypothetical risks. They are the daily reality of moving American commerce by rail.
If the FAA normalizes drone flight over railroad terminals — permitting carriers, testing firms, and third parties to routinely operate unmanned aircraft over tank car strings containing dangerous commodities — we will have created a security environment that is effectively impossible to defend. When a drone appears over a train carrying chlorine gas in a Chicago terminal, how will my members, how will yard security, how will law enforcement know whether they are watching a carrier conducting an operational test or watching a terrorist conducting reconnaissance for an attack? The answer is: they will not know. Not in time.
The normalization of drone flight over hazmat-laden rail infrastructure is not an inconvenience. It is a national security failure in slow motion. The FAA must not allow routine commercial or operational drone use over these environments to become so commonplace that a threat vehicle is indistinguishable from a routine one. Once that distinction is gone, it cannot be recovered.
They should not use drones

As a conductor of 14 years  and going into some major city's in the United States. that having drones flying over your head while trying to work as have no idea if it is a company drone a rail fan drone or maybe a drone of someone planning terrorist activities is very distracting! As we deal with cars carrying some of the most hazardous stuff out there and wondering if somebody is watching to figure out how to create a toxic situation is very concerning and believe if we can keep drones out of any transportation would be a great help to national security and a great help to the people who have to work in these situations. Thank you for your time in listening to these concerns!

I think they are very dangerous to us when they use them to test us it takes our mind off doing our work worrying about looking up wondering if you’re being watched in some cases they are good to use for instance if there is a derail and they can’t into places

I am writing to provide public comment on the proposed rule regarding the restriction of drone operations around transportation facilities and rail infrastructure. My name is Matthew Hampton, and I have 18 years of experience as a conductor and engineer.

Active rail yards are inherently dangerous environments that require total concentration. Drones overhead create unnecessary distractions for crews, and their use for surveillance and operational testing introduces serious hazards and security concerns. Furthermore, normalizing drone flights over rail cars carrying thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals is a significant risk; it becomes impossible for workers to distinguish between an official operational test and a potential act of terrorism in progress.

I have personally witnessed Management (MTOs) violate existing FAA guidelines by flying drones near airports in Evansville and Avon, Indiana. When questioned, these individuals claimed to be unaware of the nearby airports, despite the constant presence of low-flying jets. Additionally, drones often cause conductors to lose focus during critical switching operations, and I have even been told by management that they have lost control of drones due to signal failure.

Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. We deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized, and I strongly support the implementation of stricter regulations to ensure our safety and the security of our nation's infrastructure.

Matthew Hampton

I am a 29 year railroad conductor. I work in a rail yard near an airport. Planes fly over the rail yard at low altitude. Managers fly drones at high altitude to test workers. Its a recipe for disaster.

s an aviation student and future pilot, I appreciate the FAA's effort to implement a long-overdue framework for restricting unauthorized drone operations near critical infrastructure. The proposed UAFR system provides much-needed regulatory clarity for both facility operators and drone pilots, and the security concerns motivating this rule including hundreds of documented drone incidents at correctional facilities and unauthorized surveillance of energy sites clearly justify action.
That said, I have two concerns. First, with approximately 125,000 facilities potentially qualifying for restrictions across 16 sectors, the cumulative impact on low-altitude airspace could be significant for commercial operators and delivery services. Second, sector-specific criteria for several facility types remain undefined. The FAA should finalize criteria for all 16 sectors before issuing a final rule to avoid an unpredictable regulatory environment.
Overall, I support this rule making, but urge the FAA to ensure all active UAFRs are integrated into real-time airspace tools like LAANC, and that eligibility criteria are complete and clearly defined before the rule takes effect.

would like to provide my first-hand experience with the carrier I work for (CSX) flying drones in active rail yards, and reasons why in my opinion, this practice should be federally banned. I hope this explanation helps the union's cause in banning drone operations in rail yards, coming from a front-line employee that has to deal with this.

The first reason being the drones the carrier operates are strictly used for malice against employees from the management, looking to capture camera footage of minuscle rules violations and never to improve operational efficiency or safety. I have a first hand experience to testify this. In the summer of 2024, I was the engineer on a southbound loaded auto rack train that had to drop cars in Bellmen's yard near North Bergen, NJ. We pulled in the north end of the yard at CP 5, and the conductor was instructed to get off the train there and stay at the north end to make sure the cars were in the clear and retrieve the EOT. When I started pulling and got to the south end, we had misaligned switches, so I stopped the train and informed the conductor the switches were wrong. While sitting there, I spotted an MTO's company vehicle with the MTO outside with a remote control, meaning they have a drone up watching us. I also was able to spot the drone almost directly above the train hovering above the misaligned switches. Now the carrier will tell you that they never fly drones directly above employees, but I have seen them do this many times. They do that to capture the best quality camera footage they can for the investigation when they try to discipline us for miniscule operating rule violations. The MTO knew the conductor was at the opposite end of the yard and asked me if would get the switches. This MTO has a reputation for malice and knowing that he was out looking for blood, I refused to get the switches and he didn't push the issue. Instead of taking the drone down and driving back to the opposite end of the yard to pick up the conductor, he just sat outside with the drone and made the conductor wait for an hour and a half for the conductor mentor to come pick him up and drive him down to the switches. Meanwhile, our train was strung out, blocking two public road crossings on busy streets during rush hour in New Jersey this entire time. You would think while that MTO had the drone up, he would have seen the switches were misaligned and told the conductor to stay on the head end instead of getting off at the opposite end of the yard. He could have also taken the drone down and picked the conductor up to get the switches, but he couldn't be bothered because that would have interfered with him trying to capture rules violations on the drone's camera footage. This goes to show that the carrier strictly uses drones for malicious employee surveillance and never to improve operational efficiency or safety. He did end up writing the conductor up for a miniscule violation and was there to nit pick rules and not help us with our work in any way, seeing as he made the conductor wait an hour and a half for a ride. I have seen the carrier use drones probably 100 times, and never once did they use them for anything else besides employee surveillance. Never once have I ever seen them used to improve operational efficiency, or improve safety, such as informing us of potential hazards pertaining to our work, like misaligned switches ahead of the train's movement.

The second issue is that when we spot drones, we have no way of knowing whether or not they are being flown by railroad management or someone else, since they never tell us when they are using drones to spy on us. That would blow their cover. Rail buffs often fly unauthorized drones over rail yards to look at the trains. If they can get away with this, then so could a potential terrorist looking to scout out the terminal to orchestrate an act of terrorism. I am sure rail buffs aren't supposed to fly dones over rail yards, but there aren't ever any authorities around to ever enforce this. This shows that anybody can do that and get away with it. Banning drones flying over active rail yards would eliminate that potential threat, because any time we spot a drone flying over the yard, we could report it to the proper authorities knowing that it's not supposed to be there.

To address the drone use I don’t think it’s right by law and as a employee I take my job serious cause this is how I feed my son their just looking for anything to fire people for and they target certain people most being new hires the old heads they let them get away with murder. I vote to get rid of the drones

Drones do nothing but take my focus off of the work I do, to make sure I'm constantly looking up in the sky. That's not safe at all for ke considering I work in close proximity to massive, steel, deadly rolling equipment. End this nonsense, for us and our safety, please!

23 years as a switchman in a hump yard. I spend my days avoiding getting hit by free rolling box cars traveling at up to 15 mph. These yards are hazardous enough, now I’m getting buzzed by drones. They are crazy distracting and honestly very disturbing. With the threat level in this great country being what it is. I don’t know what these drones are doing or who is running them. Drones in rail yards are a dangerous game. I can’t believe we haven’t had any more injuries than we have.

Dear Federal Aviation Administration,
I am a 50-year-old transportation professional with over 30 years of hands-on experience working across Michigan. I have worked with crews handling hazardous materials and all types of rolling stock in real-world conditions. I urge the FAA to explicitly prohibit the use of commercial drones for observing, monitoring, or disciplining employees in workplaces, especially in transportation and industrial settings.
Using drones to watch crews is not about safety or efficiency — it is purely a tool for discipline and control. Companies will claim otherwise, but experienced workers know the truth. This practice creates serious safety hazards that the FAA must not enable.
Deadly Distraction and Reduced Situational Awareness
In our industry, absolute concentration is required at all times. We work around moving equipment, hazardous materials, heavy rolling stock, rail yards, loading docks, and busy transportation corridors. Any distraction can lead to catastrophe.
Drone surveillance makes employees paranoid and nervous. Instead of focusing on the dangerous tasks in front of them, workers will be constantly looking up, wondering if they are being watched or recorded. This divided attention is deadly. It increases the likelihood of serious incidents that can harm not only employees, but also neighbors, nearby businesses, and the general public.
Unnecessary Added Risk in Already Complex Environments
The skies above our work sites are already busy. Introducing persistent drone flights for employee monitoring adds collision risks, distraction hazards, and the potential for drones to crash or lose control over populated or operational areas. Companies already have extensive ground-based surveillance cameras. Those systems are intrusive enough and should themselves be reviewed for safety and privacy impacts in the future. There is no justification for layering aerial surveillance on top of them.
From my decades of experience, I can tell you that the safest crews are the ones who feel trusted to focus on their jobs, not the ones who feel they are being spied on from above. Paranoia does not improve safety — it destroys it.
FAA’s Responsibility: Prioritize Aviation and Public Safety
The FAA’s mission is to ensure the safe and efficient use of the National Airspace System. Approving routine drone flights whose main purpose is employee discipline would directly contradict that mission. These operations would place unnecessary stress on workers in high-hazard environments and expose surrounding communities to added risk.
I respectfully request that the FAA ban the use of drones (especially BVLOS and loitering operations) for employee monitoring, rule enforcement, or disciplinary purposes. Any commercial drone approvals in transportation or industrial areas should strictly prohibit surveillance of personnel.
Please protect workers, the public, and airspace safety by rejecting corporate attempts to turn drones into tools for workplace spying. Do not let companies fool regulators with claims of “improved oversight.” Experience shows this only creates more danger.
Thank you for your attention to this critical issue.
Sincerely,
Matt Delaney
Transportation Professional with 30+ years of experience in Michigan

I am not in support of the use of drones in or near rail yards.  The safety of the railroad employees should take priority over the railroad companies interest in spying on and testing their individual employees. The employees already have enough to worry about while performing their duties safely. The use of drones is a huge distraction to the employees. By having this extra distraction, many bad things could happen resulting in something as little as a minor injuries or major incidents resulting in death. When the railroad companies perform drone testing they don’t notify the railroad employees of such events, resulting in very awkward situations. The railroad employee is never certain if the done is being used for testing or some kind of outside terrorist threat. How is the individual supposed to know with this unidentified aircraft flying over head?  There is also a concern for the general public with the use of these drones. The general public experiences many of the same concerns. They could easily become distracted while drones are in the area causing a wreck or lose their focus while performing a task which involves moving heavy equipment resulting in injury or even death.

I was a rail worker who was injured on the job in 2022. I have continued working up till march 2026. While working in Montpelier Idaho management at Union Pacific observed me for over 90 minutes with a drone. Do to the previously mentioned on duty injury I was haveing trouble walking, my back is seriously injured. Jon Smith was only interested in finding a rule violation and either chose to over look how I was having trouble walking or is ignorant. Drones don’t do anything for safety only hinder our ability to work safely or efficiently.

Comment on Docket FAA 2026 4558
Designation Restricting the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility
Section 2209 NPRM
I am a drone operator based in Temple, Texas. I support the genuine protection of critical
infrastructure, national security facilities, and high risk sites. However, I urge the FAA to
withdraw this proposed rule making and pursue a more narrowly tailored and security
focused approach.
Core Position
The proposed expansion of UAS flight restrictions around fixed site facilities would create
broad geographic restrictions without addressing one of the most significant long term
vulnerabilities in the domestic UAS ecosystem: the continued use of insecure foreign origin
drones and components in United States airspace.
The United States already has systems for responding to domestic threats, law enforcement
concerns, and facility specific risks. The greater strategic danger is not only the physical
misuse of drones, but also the data, supply chain, surveillance, and economic security risks
created by foreign origin systems, particularly those tied to adversarial nations.
If the national concern is security, then the policy response should focus on trusted aircraft,
trusted components, and trusted supply chains. A phased national policy prioritizing NDAA
compliant drones, including secure design, manufacturing, assembly, and component
sourcing, would do more to protect the country than creating hundreds or thousands of new
restricted zones across the low altitude airspace.
Categorical restrictions should remain limited to DOD and DOE facilities already covered by
established national security restrictions unless Congress specifically authorizes additional
categories, defines them narrowly by statute, and requires periodic review. Other facilities
should not be allowed to broadly restrict lawful access to the National Airspace System when
safer, more precise security measures are available.
Analysis
This proposed rule risks creating a framework where fixed site operators can restrict lawful
UAS activity around their facilities while insecure foreign origin drones remain widely
available and operational throughout the country. That is the wrong security tradeoff.
The rule would also fragment low altitude airspace and make commercial drone operations
more difficult, more expensive, and less scalable. Inspections, mapping, documentation,
thermal imaging, LiDAR capture, infrastructure assessment, emergency response support,

and future delivery operations all depend on predictable access to airspace. Broad fixed site
restrictions would create operational uncertainty for lawful American operators while doing
little to address supply chain based security risks.
The United States should not respond to legitimate infrastructure concerns by broadly
restricting American citizens and American businesses from using domestic airspace.
Airspace security should be based on actual risk, trusted technology, and clearly defined
national security needs, not broad geographic exclusion.
The FAA should also recognize that UAS technology is rapidly becoming essential for
property assessment, emergency documentation, disaster response, search and rescue,
maintenance, and public safety. Overly broad UAS flight restrictions may unintentionally slow
the growth of secure domestic capability by making lawful operations more complex, less
predictable, and more expensive. Automated systems and dock based services can be
valuable parts of the future UAS ecosystem, but they should compete on capability and
safety, not become the only practical option because broad airspace restrictions make
smaller lawful operators unworkable.
Recommendations
1. Withdraw the proposed rule making in its current form.
2. Replace broad fixed site UAS restriction authority with a narrower framework limited to
clearly defined national security and critical risk facilities.
3. Rather than relying primarily on broad geographic UAS flight restrictions, the FAA should
coordinate with Congress, DHS, DOD, DOE, and other federal partners to expand the
existing secure drone policy framework beyond federal procurement and federally
funded operations, with the long term goal of removing untrusted foreign drones and
components from sensitive U.S. airspace.
4. Maintain existing protections for DOD and DOE facilities already covered by established
national security restrictions. Do not create additional restricted facility categories unless
Congress specifically authorizes them, defines them narrowly, and requires periodic
review.
5. Provide a temporary exemption for public safety, search and rescue, disaster response,
and emergency response UAS operations from any new fixed site restrictions when no
secure domestic platform can reasonably meet the mission requirement. This exemption
should be time limited, reviewed regularly, and paired with federal grants, procurement
incentives, tax credits, and domestic manufacturing support to accelerate American
made UAS capabilities for operations in remote, mountainous, inhospitable, or
communications degraded environments. The policy goal should be clear: preserve life
saving capability today while rapidly eliminating long term dependence on untrusted
foreign platforms.
6. Establish a distinct regulatory pathway for physically constrained, closed circuit, or
tethered UAS systems used as industrial work augmentation tools. These systems should
have clear ways to remain within non data capturing frameworks when configured to
avoid unnecessary imaging, mapping, transmission, retention, or reporting of data.

Properly structured, this category can expand the use of UAS for spraying, cutting,
painting, lifting, cleaning, inspection support, and similar work while reducing American
worker exposure to hazardous heights, chemicals, unstable structures, confined access
areas, and other dangerous traditional work environments.
7. Require any future fixed site restriction process to include clear public notice, transparent
justification, narrow geographic limits, defined expiration or review periods, and a
meaningful process for lawful operators to request access.
Conclusion
America itself is the critical infrastructure. The United States should protect critical facilities
without unnecessarily restricting lawful American operators or weakening the growth of
secure domestic drone capability.
A better policy would focus on trusted aircraft, secure supply chains, narrowly defined
national security risks, and continued access to the National Airspace System for lawful
operators using responsible technology.
Short term disruption from transitioning toward secure American made UAS technology is
acceptable and necessary. The result will be a stronger, more sovereign drone industry that
protects both national security and public freedom.
Thank you for considering these comments.
Trevor Francis

Drones over railroad yards create distractions. They provide no tangible use other than keeping MTO's from sitting in the weeds to nit pick operations. Conductors and engineers are proven professionals and know their crafts and for years the railroads have disregarded this fact. Due to the railroads ultimate goal of eliminating our crafts, they have implemented this tactic which adds to the hazards that face us on a daily basis. It has become blatantly obvious to us a professional railroaders what the ultimate goal entails. They have installed cameras at every corner, watching us and now they are invading our privacy over head. They are promoting the idea to all future workers to walk away with purpose. Please ban their gross use of drones.

Our company operates as a natural gas midstream operator and is classified as critical infrastructure.  The company has over 33,000 miles of pipeline and 3 LNG Sites with aboveground storage tanks. No-fly zones at the LNG sites would greatly enhance security at these sites.

The flying of the drones are unannounced especially the operators and thier motives. T&E employees are required to train for safety every year including but not limited to unoccupied bags / rigged devices safety breaches suspicious people as well as tresspasers,yet drones havnt been a topic. Whats the fine line between a armed drone or scout drone used to track rail cars and map out attacks vs mto or management just flying a drone to fail and scould a conductor.
