RE: Public Comment on Proposed Rules for Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) Operations Over Critical Infrastructure and Fixed Site Facilities (Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016)

As the Director of Flight Services for Banner Health, with over 30 years of experience in critical care and emergency medicine, I am writing to submit formal public comments regarding the implementation of Section 2209. Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) and fixed-wing air ambulance operations represent a unique, highly vulnerable segment of low-altitude aviation. While we strongly support the technological advancement, economic potential, and standard commercial deployment of the Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) industry, the safety of manned emergency medical aircraft must remain paramount.
The threat environment surrounding low-altitude airspace has evolved dramatically, as seen in both well-documented domestic civilian near-misses and shifting global military security contexts. To preserve the integrity of life-saving medical flights and national security the FAA’s final framework for Section 2209 must mandate absolute deference to manned emergency aircraft and tightly regulate UAS operations over and around critical health infrastructure.
The Problem: Immediate Low-Altitude Conflict
The critical intersection between HEMS operations and unregulated or non-compliant UAS operations is not a theoretical hazard; it is an active, escalating operational crisis.
Earlier today, on June 2, 2026, our flight program experienced a severe near-midair collision that underscores the exact regulatory gaps Section 2209 must address. One of our HEMS aircraft was executing a departure from a rural hospital landing zone in Grand County, Colorado. During the critical phase, a civilian Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) significantly exceeded its altitude limitations, operating directly within the departure corridor. The UAV came within 100 feet of our aircraft.
This incident highlights two fatal flaws in the current low-altitude ecosystem:
1 Inability to Deconflict in Real Time: Manned aircraft crews currently have zero capability to communicate directly with unmanned vehicle operators during an in-flight emergency. When a HEMS crew identifies a visual conflict at 500 feet Above Ground Level (AGL), there is no common radio frequency, digital handshake, or immediate traffic coordination mechanism to resolve the spatial conflict.
2 The Vulnerability of Uncontrolled Landing Zones: Hospital helipads, temporary scene calls (such as highway accidents), and rural landing zones are highly dynamic environments. UAS hobbyists and non-urgent commercial operators routinely operate nearby without awareness of incoming, time-critical medical flights.
The Imperative for Strict Regulation under Section 2209
The FAA must recognize fixed-site healthcare facilities, specifically all hospitals with designated heliports and trauma centers as critical infrastructure under Section 2209. Non-urgent commercial operations and hobbyist flights must be prohibited within these defined airspaces unless specifically authorized and integrated into an automated, real-time deconfliction network.
Our position is anchored on three core principles:
Absolute Right-of-Way and Deference: The FAA must codify an unyielding hierarchy of airspace priority. Manned aircraft, particularly emergency medical and public safety assets, must always be granted automatic deference over unmanned hobbyists and non-urgent commercial activities. A hobbyist photograph or a routine commercial package delivery must never compete with a patient requiring medical transport.
Mandatory, Interoperable Digital Deconfliction: Future rules must move beyond passive "Remote ID" tracking and enforce active, latency-free geofencing around critical infrastructure. If a HEMS asset activates a flight plan or transmits an emergency transponder code, nearby UAS networks should automatically receive a command to yield, descend, or land.
Balanced Industry Integration: We recognize that the UAS industry provides massive societal value, including the future potential for rapid blood, organ, and medical supply delivery. However, the commercial scaling of this industry cannot outpace the foundational safety architectures required to protect human lives in the sky and on the ground.
Conclusion
The near-miss in Grand County today could have resulted in a catastrophic loss of life, the destruction of a vital multi-million dollar air medical asset, and the denial of emergency care to a critical patient. As the FAA finalizes the rules for Section 2209, I urge the rulemaking committee to enact strict, enforceable boundaries around fixed-site healthcare facilities and mandate absolute right-of-way protocols for manned emergency aircraft.
Thank you for your time, consideration, and continued commitment to aviation safety.

Sincerely,
Joe Darmofal

I respectfully oppose the FAA's proposed Part 74 Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) rule.

While protecting critical infrastructure, public safety, and national security are legitimate objectives, this proposal risks creating a nationwide patchwork of permanent drone exclusion zones that will undermine the FAA's parallel obligation to integrate unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) into the National Airspace System (NAS).

The proposal authorizes restrictions for a broad range of facilities, including energy infrastructure, chemical facilities, communications networks, data centers, manufacturing sites, defense contractors, commercial facilities, dams, and prisons. While each category may appear reasonable individually, the FAA fails to adequately analyze the cumulative effect of potentially thousands of permanent or long-term restrictions across the country.

Congress directed the FAA to balance security concerns with airspace efficiency and the public right of transit. This proposal places too much emphasis on speculative risks and not enough on preserving access to public airspace.

A central concern is that the rule effectively allows private entities to seek government-enforced restrictions over airspace above their property. Property ownership does not confer ownership of navigable airspace. Public airspace should remain public unless restrictions are supported by clear, documented, and recurring threats. Many facilities may prefer that drones not operate nearby, but preference alone should not justify limiting access to a public resource.

The proposal repeatedly focuses on what could happen if a drone were misused. However, regulatory restrictions should be based on demonstrated risks rather than theoretical possibilities. Nearly every facility can identify a hypothetical drone threat. The FAA should require evidence of documented incidents, credible intelligence, or repeated unlawful activity before approving a UAFR.

The FAA also overlooks the fact that existing laws already prohibit reckless drone operations, contraband delivery, criminal surveillance, sabotage, interference with emergency response, and other dangerous conduct. Additional restrictions will primarily burden compliant operators while doing little to deter individuals already willing to violate federal law.

The economic impact of this proposal is also underestimated. Commercial drones support agriculture, infrastructure inspection, surveying, construction, environmental monitoring, journalism, and emergency response. Expanding restricted airspace increases compliance costs, flight planning complexity, operational inefficiencies, and barriers to innovation. These burdens will fall most heavily on small businesses, independent operators, and startups rather than large corporations with dedicated compliance staff.

The proposal's reliance on Remote ID monitoring also raises privacy concerns. Facilities seeking UAFRs would be required to receive Remote ID broadcasts, and the FAA is considering data retention requirements. Remote ID was intended to support accountability and safety, not to create a nationwide network of private entities collecting and storing information about lawful drone operators. Any retention requirement should be rejected absent a compelling and demonstrated need.

The strongest justification presented in the proposal concerns correctional facilities, where documented incidents involving contraband delivery by drones are well established. However, evidence supporting restrictions for prisons should not automatically justify restrictions across every sector listed in the rule. Restrictions should be narrowly tailored to demonstrated threats.

The FAA should revise this proposal by requiring documented evidence of need, prioritizing temporary restrictions over permanent ones, limiting restricted areas to the smallest practical footprint, requiring periodic review and automatic expiration, and strengthening transparency requirements.

The National Airspace System is a public resource. Restrictions on access should be narrowly tailored, evidence-based, and demonstrably necessary. As written, the proposed rule is overly broad and risks undermining innovation, economic growth, and the successful integration of UAS into American airspace. I urge the FAA to substantially narrow the proposal before adoption.

These drones are used around employees and have caused close calls landing with the drone coming back to land itself and almost hitting into people. Also managment are using these drones without a second person to be a spotter to assist while they fly. When we are out working around trains and our attention should be focused on the movement of trains and these drones are creating strange sounds that draw our attention away for where it should be. This is very dangerous. With all the movable cameras that are installed in the yards and on the locomotive, there is no reason these attention drawing electronic devices should be allowed to be used. These drones are also flown around airports and other places where untrained drone users should never fly.

I work at two rail yards where the railroad will sometimes use drones to monitor employees and operations. This should not be allowed for both the safety of the public and employees. One yard is located within a mile or two of the approach end of one of the runways at a local airport with frequent passenger flights everyday. The other is only about a mile from a hospital with a heliport for life flight helicopters that is used everyday.

Drones are unsafe in a railroad yard! I am a conductor for a class 1 railroad for 8 years now.

We are being cautious around hazardous rail cars while switching and being distracted by drones are not safe.

The added pressure of micro managing and now above your head is absurd.

Trust your trained employees!!

comments.comment_text:
Montana Department of Transportation
Response to FAA Proposed Rule: Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
June 3, 2026
Docket Operations
Federal Aviation Administration
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590
Subject: Comments on FAA NPRM, Restricting UAS Operations
Near Fixed-Site Facilities
The Montana Department of Transportation (MDT) appreciates the opportunity to
comment on the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking titled
‘Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility,’
published in the Federal Register on May 6, 2026. MDT supports efforts that balance
national security, public safety, and the safe integration of unmanned aircraft systems
(UAS) into the national airspace system.
MDT operates a robust and growing UAS program that supports critical transportation
missions statewide. These include roadway inspections, bridge and structure inspection
support, stockpile measurement, rockslide monitoring along high-risk corridors,
photogrammetric mapping, and incident response. MDT is also evaluating future
automated and remote UAS capabilities to support rapid monitoring rockslides, flooding
hazards, and other emergent transportation issues across Montana’s challenging
operating environment.
While MDT recognizes and supports the FAA’s objective of improving safety around fixed-
site facilities, several aspects of the proposed rule raise concerns regarding potential
impacts on MDT’s ability to conduct essential UAS operations. In particular, the location,
size, and administration of Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) could
unintentionally affect transportation corridors and bridges, tunnels, maintenance areas,
and hazard monitoring locations that fall within or near UAFR boundaries. MDT is
concerned that UAFR boundaries associated with eligible fixed-site facilities may overlap
with or restrict access to operational areas where recurring UAS missions occur,
particularly in mountainous regions prone to avalanches, as well as around bridges,
tunnels, or maintenance facilities. Restrictions in these areas could hinder MDT’s ability
to perform timely hazard assessments, infrastructure inspections, and automated
monitoring flights that protect both agency personnel and the traveling public.

The proposed rule may also create administrative and operational challenges. MDT
manages numerous dynamic and geographically dispersed hazard areas that do not align
cleanly with fixed-site definitions. Requiring individual reviews or UAFR-related approvals
for each site could place significant burdens on both MDT and the FAA, potentially slowing
emergency responses and degrading situational awareness during critical events.
MDT respectfully recommends that the FAA revise the proposed rule to incorporate more
flexible provisions for state transportation agencies and emergency responders. This
should include clarifying operational allowances for infrastructure inspection, public safety,
emergency response, disaster assessment, and transportation hazard monitoring within
or near designated UAFRs, including how those allowances apply to Part 107 operations,
public aircraft operations, and future authorized BVLOS or automated UAS operations.
MDT further recommends that the FAA consider a streamlined process for agencies with
broad statewide operational responsibilities, reducing duplicative administrative
requirements and enabling consistent, efficient UAS mission planning across diverse
operating environments.
MDT also recommends that UAFR locations, boundaries, requirements, points of contact,
and status information be made available in a machine-readable format. This would allow
public agencies to integrate UAFR information into GIS systems, mission planning tools,
forms, dashboards, and other operational workflows.
In conclusion, MDT supports the FAA’s intention to enhance safety and security but urges
the agency to ensure that any final rule incorporates practical allowances for
transportation-related UAS operations. MDT remains committed to collaborating with the
FAA to strike a balance between safeguarding sensitive facilities and ensuring that
essential public-service UAS operations can continue without unnecessary restrictions or
delays.
Sincerely,
Montana Department of Transportation
Unmanned Aircraft Systems Program

From working in RailYards where drones are constantly flying to observe worker working.  it is a major distraction to employees conducting dangerous work.  From being an ATP jelicopter pilot for the last 35 years I believe to operators of these drones are not following the rules set forth by the FAA.  Not only airspace infringments but fluing over private properties at very low altitudes.  I have observed several instances that apperances that rules were broken.  I think that there are places drones could be more useful to railroads vs flying in communities where public safety is at risk

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to raise the serious safety concern regarding the use of unmanned aircraft systems, commonly referred to as drones, by railroad management to monitor employees and initiate disciplinary referrals against train and engine service employees working in active rail yards.

Railroad operations, whether conducted within a yard or over the line of road, are inherently safety-sensitive. Trainmen are required to maintain constant situational awareness while working around moving equipment, switches, shove movements, close clearances, radio communications, and other employees on the ground. Any unnecessary distraction introduced into that environment creates an unacceptable risk.

The use of drones flying above or near employees actively engaged in switching operations is not merely intrusive; it is dangerous. These aircraft create visual and auditory distractions in an environment where a momentary loss of focus can result in serious injury or death. Employees naturally look toward, react to, or become aware of drones operating overhead, and that distraction can interfere with their ability to safely perform duties that already demand undivided attention.

This practice is especially needless because railroads already maintain extensive fixed-camera surveillance throughout rail yard facilities. The use of drones in addition to existing camera systems is absolutely unnecessary for legitimate operational oversight and is solely designed to generate disciplinary referrals. Using aerial surveillance in this manner introduces a new hazard into an already hazardous workplace without any corresponding safety benefit.

In short, the deployment of drones over active rail operations for employee surveillance and discipline is an unnecessary and unsafe practice. It creates foreseeable distractions that can contribute to operational errors with potentially fatal consequences. I respectfully request that the FAA deny, through regulation, this practice as it diverts employee attention in a setting where even a brief distraction can result in catastrophic injury or death.

Drones are not the answer. They are very disruptive! All they are doing is having conductors pay more attention to the drones then concentrating on the task at hand. They aren’t a safe way to conduct testing. In my opinion it’s just laziness on the company’s behalf. The management needs to talk to crews face to face not watch them with a drone!
If the thing crashes what damage will it cause? I’m not a fan of them.

Drones are cool and useful but should only be used when it's appropriate. Hovering over rail workers while they are switching in a yard is not one of those places. Instead of the worker looking down watching their footing, they are now looking up because of buzzing they hear. We as employees don't know who is behind the drone as we have not been trained on how to distinguish an approved use - it could be anyone!

I've heard of my coworkers who have been sitting on a locomotive and a drone just comes flying up to the window to snoop on them. How many times can a manager do that before it hits the employee in the face? The carrier's will say that never happens, but the only way to make that statement true, is to not allow them in the first place.

Conductor for Class 1 RR

Executive Summary of Attached Public Comment

Please see the attached document for my full, comprehensive analysis of the Section 2209 NPRM. Below is an executive summary of the critical logical, legal, and operational flaws identified in the proposed rule:

1. The "Slippery Slope" of Connectivity: Defining "critical facilities" by their systemic connections (e.g., sewers feeding a plant, or distant runoff pipes) creates an absurd chain logic. Because much of our infrastructure is subterranean, broad interpretations would allow entire municipalities to lock down public skies. Recommendation: Restrictions must be strictly limited to the immediate, physical footprint of the core asset.

2. Data Security Inconsistency: Restricting low-altitude sUAS out of espionage or privacy fears is redundant. High-powered ground-based cameras, manned Part 91 aircraft, and commercial satellite imagery already legally collect superior data. Restricting drones protects nothing; it only penalizes a single technology sector.

3. The Remote ID Paradox: Blanket geographic bans undermine the FAA's own Remote ID (RID) framework. If a drone transparently broadcasts its identity and pilot location, it poses no covert threat. Because bad actors will simply disable RID, blanket airspace bans solely punish and ground compliant pilots.

4. Erosion of Federal Preemption: Handing veto power over public airspace to private railroad executives, corporate entities, and local utility boards effectively outsources federal sovereignty. It threatens to turn the unified National Airspace System (NAS) into a fragmented patchwork of corporate fiefdoms.

5. Economic and Maintenance Self-Sabotage: Infrastructure operators are the primary beneficiaries of drone inspections. Overreaching restrictions will trap third-party environmental teams, emergency services, and facility contractors in a bureaucratic logjam waiting for FAA waivers to conduct safety-essential maintenance.

6. The "Neighborhood Watch" Blind Spot: Compliant drone pilots act as a decentralized layer of public oversight. They are often the first to spot and report brush fires, chemical leaks, structural failures, or trespassers. Total bans create a security vacuum by clearing the sky of the "good guys."

7. The Solution (Micro-Airspaces & Passive Disruption): Infrastructure is deeply integrated into the public spaces it serves. Rather than granting sweeping, multi-mile exclusion zones that paralyze local economies, the FAA should mandate tight micro-airspaces combined with localized, automated, and passive perimeter-disruption systems that target threats without grounding the public.

Conclusion:
The FAA must protect the unity of the NAS, honor the utility of Remote ID, and require an extraordinary burden of proof from facility managers before privatizing public skies.

Full arguments, examples, and detailed recommendations are provided in the attached PDF.

Eric Nicolaysen
Owner / HighRes LLC

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
ALDEN WHEELER DETECTIVE AGENCY & GPS
Private Investigation • Drone-Supported Field Operations • GPS Monitoring • Cyber Threat Assessment
Anderson, South Carolina
May 19, 2026
Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington, DC 20591
Re: Public Comment on Notice of Proposed Rulemaking - Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33
91 FR 24650 {May 6, 2026); Comments Due July 6, 2026
Dear Administrator and Docket Operations:
I respectfully submit these comments on the Federal Aviation Administration's {FAA) proposed rule to
establish a process for designating Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) under proposed 14
CFR part 74. I am the owner of Alden Wheeler Detective Agency & GPS, a licensed South Carolina private
investigation firm whose service lines include drone-supported field operations (search and rescue,
hazardous-materials response support, surveillance, and infrastructure inspection), GPS monitoring, and
a Cyber Threat Assessment practice. My current operational fleet includes a DJI Matrice 30T, a Mavic 3T,
and a Chasing Ml Pro tethered surface platform, all flown under 14 CFR part 107 with appropriate
waivers when required.
I commend the FAA for working to implement section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security
Act of 2016 (FESSA), as amended, and for attempting to balance the legitimate protection of critical
infrastructure with the public right of transit under 49 U.S.C. § 40103. The proposed rule reflects
substantial coordination with the Sector Risk Management Agencies (SRMAs) and a clear effort to apply
restrictions only where necessary. The comments below are offered in support of that balance - not
against the rule, but to identify specific provisions that, as drafted, would impose disproportionate
burdens on small commercial Part 107 operators, public-safety support providers, and licensed
investigators without meaningfully advancing the rule's safety and security objectives.
Page 1 of 7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
burdens on small commercial Part 107 operators, public-safety support providers, and licensed
investigators without meaningfully advancing the rule's safety and security objectives.
I. Summary of Comments
My principal points are as follows:
• Scope and cumulative footprint. A program contemplating more than 9,000 designated fixed
site facilities, layered onto existing controlled airspace, restricted areas, and Special Security
Instructions, risks an aggregate "patchwork" effect that materially reduces low-altitude airspace
available to compliant commercial and public-safety operators. The FAA should publish, before
any UAFR program goes live, a national low-altitude availability analysis and commit to revisiting
the rule if cumulative coverage in any region exceeds a defined threshold.
• Transit standard in § 74.250 is unconstitutionally vague. The requirement that a Part 91, 107,
108, 135, or 137 operator transit a UAFR "in the shortest practicable time" provides no objective
metric, creates strict-liability enforcement exposure, and will chill lawful overflight. The FAA
should replace this with an objective standard tied to airspeed, altitude, or duration.
• Notification under§ 74.255 must be centralized. Requiring operators to notify a "site manager"
at each facility individually is unworkable when a single mission may transit multiple UAFRs. The
FAA should adopt a single FAA-hosted notification portal modeled on LAANC, with API access
and machine-readable acknowledgments.
• Public-safety, SAR, and exigent operations need an enumerated access pathway. Proposed §
74.250 does not clearly accommodate Public Safety operations conducted under 49 U.S.C. §
44807, Certificates of Waiver or Authorization (COA), or exigent-circumstances SAR and hazmat
support. Lives are lost in minutes; access cannot depend on a 5-business-day update cycle or
facility-by-facility permission.
• Licensed private investigators conducting lawful, court-authorized investigations should have
a defined credibility pathway. The rule asks (Question E) what information an operator should
provide to establish credibility. A state-licensed Pl with an active Part 107 certificate, registered
aircraft, and verifiable insurance is a known, accountable operator who can be vetted in the
same manner as Part 135 carriers.
• Remote ID is already mandatory and is sufficient identifying technology. Layering additional
broadcast or coordination requirements on lawful operators - while malicious actors will
simply disable Remote ID - punishes the wrong population. The FAA should resist proposals to
expand Remote ID obligations beyond 14 CFR part 89.
• Notice-and-comment on Special UAFRs must remain the rule, not the exception. The "good
cause" carve-out in§ 74.6 should be narrowly construed and accompanied by mandatory post
promulgation comment within 30 days, to preserve transparency.
Page 2 of 8

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• Sector eligibility criteria should be public and challengeable. Where SRMA security
assessments cannot be disclosed in full, the FAA should publish a redacted basis document for
each UAFR so commenters can meaningfully evaluate proportionality.
II. Detailed Comments
A. Cumulative Airspace Impact and the 9,000-Facility Scenario
The FAA's own Regulatory Impact Analysis contemplates a scenario of over 9,000 eligible fixed-site
facilities obtaining UAFRs, with annualized program costs of $21-$31 million. While the per-facility
lateral footprint is bounded by the property line and an altitude ceiling generally at 400 feet AGL, the
cumulative effect on Part 107 commercial operations - utility inspection, real-estate photography,
agricultural mapping, journalism, and the rapidly growing SAR and hazmat-support sectors - has not
been adequately quantified.
I recommend that the FAA, prior to issuing any final rule, publish a quantitative analysis of: (1) projected
cumulative UAFR coverage in representative urban, suburban, and rural counties at 5-year, 10-year, and
steady-state buildout; (2) the percentage of low-altitude(~ 400 ft AGL) airspace expected to be
restricted in each; and (3) the projected effect on Part 107 commercial revenue. Without that analysis,
the public cannot meaningfully evaluate whether the rule preserves "the public right of transit"
mandated by 49 U.S.C. § 40103(a)(2).
8. The "Shortest Practicable Time" Transit Standard (§ 7 4.250)
Proposed § 74.250 permits a Part 91, 107, 108, 135, or 137 operator to transit a Standard UAFR
provided the operator broadcasts Remote ID and transits "in the shortest practicable time." This phrase
is undefined. In an environment where a single enforcement action can result in suspension of a Part
107 certificate under FAA Order 2150.3C and civil penalties under 14 CFR part 13, vague terms invite
selective enforcement and discourage lawful flight in or near a UAFR even when transit is otherwise
permitted.
I recommend the FAA replace the phrase with an objective standard, such as:
• Direct routing at or above the operator's normal en-route ground speed for the aircraft class,
not to exceed a defined maximum dwell time (e.g., 90 seconds) within the UAFR; or
• A maximum lateral deviation from the great-circle transit path of, e.g., 200 feet, absent ATC
instruction or operational necessity (wind, obstacles, emergency).
Either approach would give operators an objective compliance target and give enforcement personnel a
defensible metric.
C. Notification Mechanics(§ 74.255)
Page 3 of 8

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
Requiring case-by-case notification to a designated site manager at each UAFR is operationally infeasible
for any mission that traverses more than one UAFR - a routine occurrence on linear-infrastructure
inspections (pipelines, rail corridors, transmission lines) and on SAR or hazmat operations that may cross
multiple energy and water-sector facilities in a single sortie.
I recommend a single, FAA-hosted notification mechanism modeled on the Low Altitude Authorization
and Notification Capability (LAANC). The system should: (1) accept machine-to-machine notifications; (2)
automatically route them to the registered site manager(s) of each UAFR transited; (3) return a digital
acknowledgment that satisfies the operator's record keeping obligation; and (4) be available 24/7 with
documented uptime targets. Building one durable national system is more efficient than requiring tens
of thousands of independent operator-to-facility communication channels.
D. Public-Safety, SAR, Hazmat, and Exigent-Circumstance Operations
Proposed§ 74.250 lists Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 as allowed-operation categories. It is silent or
unclear regarding: (1) Public Safety entities operating under a Certificate of Waiver or Authorization
(COA) or under 49 U.S.C. § 44807; (2) operators supporting state or county Emergency Management or
Hazmat teams on a contract basis; and (3) any operator responding to an exigent SAR or life-safety
emergency.
Lives can turn on minutes. A drowning, a wildland search, a chlorine release at a water-treatment plant,
a derailment with multiple casualties - in each of those scenarios, a five-business-day update cycle and
a static notification list are inadequate. I recommend the FAA add an explicit§ 74.250 paragraph that
authorizes exigent-circumstance access for:
• Aircraft operating in direct support of a federal, state, tribal, or local Public Safety entity actively
responding to a life-safety, SAR, or declared emergency incident, provided Remote ID is
broadcast and the incident commander or appropriate ATC facility is notified as soon as
practicable; and
• Aircraft operating under an active Public Safety COA or 49 U.S.C. § 44807 authorization, when
transiting in support of the authorized mission.
This pathway is consistent with the FAA's long-standing practice of authorizing Public Safety operations
in otherwise restricted airspace and would not undermine the rule's security purpose.
E. Credibility Pathway for Licensed Private Investigators and Small Commercial
Operators
The FAA expressly invites comment (Executive Summary, Section F) on what information an operator
should provide to establish credibility and demonstrate they are not a security threat. From the
perspective of a licensed investigative firm, the following identifiers are already verifiable through
existing federal and state systems and should be sufficient, individually or in combination:
Page 4of 8

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• An active 14 CFR part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate, with TSA Security Threat Assessment on file
(already required for issuance);
• An aircraft registered under 14 CFR part 47 or 48 with broadcast Remote ID per part 89;
• A state-issued private investigator license in good standing (verifiable through the issuing state
agency - in South Carolina, through SLED);
• Evidence of commercial aviation liability insurance at a defined minimum (e.g., $1,000,000 per
occurrence) naming the FAA or facility as additional certificate holder, where applicable;
• For Public Safety support contracts, a letter of designation from the contracting public agency.
Verification of these credentials should be a one-time enrollment in a national "Trusted Operator"
registry, not a per-facility, per-flight ordeal. Operators in the registry would be eligible to transit UAFRs
subject to the notification and Remote ID requirements above.
F. Remote ID Is Sufficient; Do Not Layer Additional Broadcast Mandates
The FAA expressly asks(§ 74.56 commentary) whether more stringent Remote ID requirements should
apply to operations within a UAFR beyond 14 CFR part 89. My answer is no, for two reasons. First, lawful
operators are already burdened with the cost and complexity of compliant Remote ID modules; the
marginal security benefit of imposing further broadcast requirements on this population is minimal.
Second, and more important, malicious operators - the population the rule purports to address - will
simply disable Remote ID, fly a non-compliant aircraft, or modify a compliant one. Adding requirements
that only the law-abiding will follow is a classic case of regulatory mismatch. Resources are better spent
on detection, attribution, and counter-UAS authority for the facilities that need it.
G. Special UAFRs (§ 74.6): Preserve Notice-and-Comment as the Default
I support the proposed framework for Special UAFRs as a transparent, durable alternative to repeated
short-term 14 CFR § 99.7 Special Security Instructions. I urge the FAA, however, to construe the "good
cause to forgo notice and comment" exception narrowly. Where good cause is invoked, the FAA should
commit to: (1) publishing a redacted basis document at promulgation; (2) opening a 30-day post
promulgation comment period; and (3) sunset review of every Special UAFR at year three of its five-year
term.
H. Sector-Specific Criteria Should Be Publicly Reviewable
The preamble correctly notes that classified threat information may limit the FAA's ability to publish
sector-specific eligibility criteria in full. Where that is the case, I recommend the FAA, in coordination
with the relevant SRMA, publish a redacted basis document at the NPRM stage for each requested
UAFR. Without some publicly reviewable articulation of the threat and the proposed remedy, the 30-day
comment period contemplated by§ 74.15 is reduced to a formality. The Administrative Procedure Act's
Page 5 of 8

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
notice-and-comment obligations are not satisfied by an opportunity to comment on an undisclosed
factual record.
I. Obligation to Update(§ 74.20) and Cyber Considerations
The five-business-day update obligation in § 74.20(a) is reasonable in concept, but the rule does not
address the cybersecurity of the system into which those updates are filed, nor the cybersecurity of the
notification system contemplated in§ 74.255. A centralized notification system that announces, in near
real time, that a particular drone is about to overfly a particular hardened facility is itself a target. I
recommend the FAA: (1) require the operating system to meet, at a minimum, Fed RAMP Moderate; (2)
restrict site-manager access to the minimum required information; (3) prohibit retention of operator
flight data beyond a defined period; and (4) subject the system to annual third-party penetration testing
with summary results published. As an operator who provides cyber threat assessment services, I view
this as a foreseeable and meaningful risk, not a theoretical one.
J. Environmental Review(§ 74.70)
I support a streamlined NEPA approach for UAFRs that, by design, do not authorize any new physical
activity. The principal environmental considerations - noise and wildlife disturbance - are unaffected
by adding a UAFR, because the rule restricts overflight rather than authorizing it. A categorical exclusion
with documented findings is appropriate for the typical UAFR. Where a particular facility raises unique
environmental considerations (e.g., overlap with a designated wilderness area or critical habitat), an
Environmental Assessment should be required.
Ill. Direct Responses to Questions Posed by FAA
Question: What additional types of unmanned aircraft operations should FAA
allow through a UAFR?
In addition to Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137: (a) operations under an active Public Safety COA or 49
U.S.C. § 44807 authorization; (b) operations by, or under direct contract to, a federal, state, tribal, or
local public-safety agency for SAR, hazmat, fire, or law-enforcement support; (c) operations by Trusted
Operator registrants (see Section 11.E above); and (d) exigent-circumstance overflights tied to a
documented life-safety emergency.
Question: What is the economic impact to commercial UAS operators if they
cannot transit UAFRs?
For my agency specifically, projected revenue losses from inability to transit critical-infrastructure UAFRs
on linear-corridor inspection, SAR support contracts, and insurance-related investigative work would
meaningfully impact a small-business gross margin, in addition to displaced indirect spending on aircraft,
batteries, training, insurance, and connectivity (Starlink). Scaled across the Part 107 commercial
population, the aggregate effect of the 9,000-facility scenario is likely to be substantial. The FAA should
Page 6 of 8

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
quantify this directly in the final Regulatory Impact Analysis, including impacts to small entities under
the Regulatory Flexibility Act.
Question: What information should an operator provide to establish c ibility?
See Section 11.E above. In brief: Part 107 certificate (with TSA STA on file), aircraft registration and
Remote ID, state Pl license (where applicable), commercial aviation liability insurance, and - for Public
Safety contracts - a letter of designation from the contracting agency. These data are already collected
by federal and state systems; the FAA should leverage existing records rather than build duplicative
ones.
Question: Are additional technological or procedural requirements appropriate
(e.g., Remote ID, site coordination, law-enforcement coordination)?
Beyond the existing Remote ID broadcast requirement under 14 CFR part 89, the FAA should consider:
(a) a machine-readable LAANC-style notification with digital acknowledgment; (b) a Trusted Operator
registry; and (c) optional voluntary coordination with local law enforcement for sensitive missions. The
FAA should not impose additional broadcast or transponder requirements that exceed part 89, for the
reasons set out in Section 11.F.
IV. Conclusion
Section 2209 of FESSA reflects a legitimate congressional judgment that certain fixed-site facilities
deserve a defined process for restricting unmanned-aircraft overflight. The FAA's proposed rule is, on
the whole, a thoughtful implementation of that mandate. With the targeted refinements outlined above
- an objective transit standard, a centralized notification system, an enumerated Public Safety and
exigent-circumstance pathway, a Trusted Operator credibility track for state-licensed investigators and
small commercial operators, narrow construction of the Special UAFR good-cause exception, and
meaningful cybersecurity safeguards around the notification system - the final rule can fairly protect
critical infrastructure without unduly burdening the lawful commercial and public-safety operators on
whom communities increasingly rely.
I appreciate the opportunity to comment and am available to discuss any of the foregoing in greater
detail.
Alden Wheeler
Owner, Alden Wheeler Detective Agency & GPS

I am a railroad employee with 22 years of experience in the field as a conductor and engineer.  When the railroads 1st advised that they were going to be using drones it sounded like they were going to use this new technology to improve safety by using them to scan tracks, trains, and property to identify defects, locate cars, and even trespassers on trains or railroad property to protect employees. In reality, 99% of the drone use that I know of has been used for the sole purpose of spying on their own employees.  Now every day I work I see conductors spending time looking to the skies worried about the next “drone strike” as we call it.  These actions are jeopardizing their own safety. We are taught during annual testing to recognize, record, and report strange activity on and around our workplace. I once reported a drone that was flying around me while I was switching a hazardous car loaded with propane.  The drone turned out to be railroad managers watching me but I had no idea of the drone’s intentions.  The managers subsequently approached me, and were very unhappy that I (1) stopped working and (2) alerted others about the drone (although I was only following rules that they put in place). Docket: FAA-2026-4558
NPRM: Designation, Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Subject: Integrating Non-Cooperative Passive Surveillance into UAFR Layered Security

INTEREST OF THE COMMENTER
As a developer of passive radar detection technology for critical infrastructure, I submit this comment on the technical and operational criteria required for facilities to implement, monitor, and enforce Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Eldaeon Inc. is registered with the U.S. Department of State's Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC) and is pursuing a Commodity Jurisdiction determination for our flagship system, Nemesis, under ITAR. Nemesis is a non-emitting, non-cooperative drone detection platform that uses ambient radio frequency signals to detect, track, and log unmanned aircraft activity. This comment focuses on how non-cooperative passive surveillance can meet the rule's layered security objectives without compromising the National Airspace System.

THE SECTION 74.250 VALIDATION PROBLEM
Section 74.250 preserves transit access for compliant operators under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137, provided they broadcast Remote ID, notify the facility, and transit in the shortest practicable time. This allowance is appropriate but creates an operational challenge the rule's protective security measures do not fully address. A facility security operator must, in real time, distinguish between (i) authorized Part 107 or 135 transit broadcasting compliant Remote ID, and (ii) non-cooperative or modified aircraft that pose the actual threat.

A Remote ID receiver alone can confirm the compliant aircraft. It cannot, by definition, detect the non-compliant aircraft. The result is a partial picture that may generate false alarms against authorized traffic or miss the actual intrusion entirely. The 2018 Gatwick incident ($1.7M in losses, 33 hours of operational shutdown from a single non-cooperative drone) illustrates the cost.

Non-cooperative passive surveillance resolves this by providing a complete real-time spatial picture of all aircraft in the protected volume, regardless of broadcast status. Fused with Remote ID data, the facility can confirm authorized transit and concentrate response on actual non-cooperative intrusions.

THE UNIQUE VALUE OF PASSIVE RADAR AT 2209-ELIGIBLE FACILITIES
Active radar and active RF counter-UAS sensors face significant hurdles at the facilities the rule covers. At airports, active sensors risk interference with Air Traffic Control, terminal surveillance radars, and certificated avionics. Passive radar is structurally different:

Zero RF Footprint: emits no signals; uses existing ambient illuminators (digital broadcast, cellular). Cannot interfere with ATC, surveillance radars, Remote ID, or any licensed spectrum user.
Non-Cooperative Tracking: detects targets by physical presence regardless of broadcast status. Reliably catches low-altitude UAS that disable, spoof, or never carried Remote ID.
Audit-Grade Telemetry: generates time-stamped, position-accurate tracks suitable for forensic review and regulatory reporting.
Compatibility with No-Mitigation Posture: the rule explicitly bars jamming, geo-fencing, drone capture, or any mitigation. Passive radar is pure detection and reporting, fully compatible with the rule's design.
SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS FOR THE FINAL RULE
To ensure UAFRs are effective and safe, I urge the FAA to incorporate the following into the final rule:

Expand Eligible Security Criteria (14 CFR § 74.54): Explicitly recognize non-cooperative passive surveillance, such as passive radar, as an approved methodology for fulfilling the facility's "protective security measures" requirement.
Encourage Passive Tech for Shared Environments: Draft guidance that favors or fast-tracks UAFR applications using passive tracking, since these ensure complete situational awareness without threatening the NAS through signal emissions.
Define Data Integration Standards: Ensure future UAFR notification systems and digital flight-restriction feeds can ingest telemetry from non-cooperative passive sensors for a unified operating picture between facility operators and air traffic management.
CLOSING
The proposed rule's emphasis on layered security and prohibition on active mitigation inside UAFRs are sound design choices. These comments are intended to ensure the final rule recognizes the unique value of non-cooperative passive surveillance as a qualifying technology. We thank the FAA for the opportunity to comment and welcome follow-up technical engagement.

Respectfully submitted,

Jonathan Wagner
Eldaeon Inc.
jonathanwagner@berkeley.edu
June 8, 2026

I notice myself (15yr conductor) always looking for drones, essentially out of fear of being fired for picking my nose with the wrong finger.  Lately though, with current world events, I’ve began to wonder what will happen when a new conductor comes into a railroad after time in the military, gets buzzed by a drone, has a moment of PTSD-related panic, and ends up in a dangerous, if not deadly, situation.

To Whom It May Concern,

I have worked as a freight railroad conductor for a Class I railroad for the past 14 years. During that time, I have operated safely in one of the most hazardous work environments in the transportation industry and have always done my best to perform my duties professionally and in compliance with company rules.

My concern is not with drone technology itself, nor with reasonable efforts to improve safety. My concern is whether the increasing use of surveillance technology is reaching a point where it may begin to interfere with the very safety it is intended to promote.

Over the years, railroads have added extensive monitoring systems. Employees are observed by supervisors, cameras throughout rail yards, locomotive cameras, and now drones capable of monitoring areas previously outside camera coverage. While each addition may appear reasonable on its own, the cumulative effect is a workplace where employees are under near-constant observation.

Rail yards are dynamic and dangerous environments. Safe operations depend on situational awareness, sound judgment, communication, and attention to the hazards immediately surrounding us. Yet many employees now spend increasing amounts of mental energy wondering whether they are being observed, recorded, or evaluated rather than focusing solely on the task at hand.

This raises an important question: At what point does additional surveillance stop providing meaningful safety benefits and begin creating new distractions?

When a drone appears overhead, employees often have no idea who is operating it or why it is there. Is it conducting an inspection, monitoring employee activity, performing a security function, or operated by someone outside the railroad entirely? In an industry that routinely discusses security threats, theft, trespassing, and operational risks, that uncertainty naturally attracts attention.

My concern is that modern organizations often assume more surveillance automatically results in greater safety. However, there may be a point of diminishing returns where additional monitoring contributes less to safety and more to employee distraction, stress, and second-guessing.

As someone who works in the field every day, I believe regulators should consider not only the benefits of surveillance technologies but also their impact on worker focus and situational awareness in high-risk environments. Safety is not achieved solely by monitoring workers. It is achieved by ensuring workers can concentrate on the hazards around them and perform the jobs they were trained to do.

I respectfully encourage the FAA to consider these factors when evaluating the use of drones in active industrial and transportation workplaces.

Respectfully,

Christopher M. Brown

I work as a trainman for Union Pacific railroad in the Los Angeles circ.  I have daily duties in train yards building trains, switching cars, riding on the outside of cars, getting on and off moving equipment, working on and around multiple track and moving trains.  It is dangerous work that requires strict, unwavering focus and attention to remain safe.  I have been in yards in Long Beach, city of Commerce, Colton, City of Industry and LA where managers sit in their work vehicles and operate drones directly overhead to monitor and record our movements and work, trying specifically to catch us making any mistakes they can. As UP has  admittedly overhired recently and garaunteed the union there will not be any layoffs or furloughs, they have made verbal statements they have a target to fire 60 workers to reduce manpower. The drones are used for this purpose.  As such, the drones distract, break our concentration and are increasing the danger of our workplaces. They should absolutely be restricted from being used to “spy” on us and increase the danger of our jobs.

As a 27 year Railroad Trainman for the Union Pacific Railroad, I can tell you that that Drone surveillance is distracting and unsafe. UP managers are not well trained or safety compliant in their zeal to find a rule infraction making operations less safe than if they would just do in person observations. If secrecy and intimidation is the goal, drone operations are great, if safety and education are important, they are an abject failure.

There are several concerns I have regarding the use of drones in railroad yards. First and foremost, drones can create unnecessary distractions for employees working on the ground. Whether it is the flashing lights of the drone or the constant sound of it hovering overhead, these distractions can interfere with tasks that require a high level of concentration. Yard operations demand constant focus, whether employees are making shoves, reading switch lists, lining switches, or conducting critical job briefings with other crew members.

At the end of the day, trainmen already operate under extensive rules, regulations, and oversight. In many cases, the use of drones appears less focused on improving safety or operational efficiency and more focused on monitoring employees in an effort to identify rule violations. This creates the perception that management is using drones as an easy way to generate disciplinary actions rather than as a tool to support railroad operations.

Furthermore, these drone operations are rarely utilized in ways that could genuinely benefit crews. For example, drones could be used to help locate railcars, identify closed angle cocks, inspect hard-to-reach areas, or assist with other operational tasks. Instead, their primary use often appears to be the surveillance of trainmen, with little direct benefit to the employees performing the work.

Another significant concern is equipment reliability and the potential safety hazards associated with drone failures. What happens if a drone experiences a GPS malfunction, communication failure, or other internal hardware issue? As the saying goes, what goes up must come down. A malfunctioning drone could quickly become a hazard to employees working below. There is also the possibility of battery damage or failure, which could result in a fire. These are serious safety considerations that should be carefully evaluated when implementing drone operations in active railroad yards.

While drones have the potential to be valuable tools when used appropriately, their current application in many rail yards raises legitimate concerns regarding workplace distractions, employee surveillance, and operational safety.

My name is David Bunting, and I have served as a railroad conductor for over 21 years. Throughout my two decades on the rails, I have witnessed firsthand that active rail yards are inherently dangerous environments. We work around heavy machinery and hazardous materials. Adding drones to this mix is not "modernization"—it is a significant safety hazard.

Railroad work requires absolute focus. When I am on the ground, my attention must be on the movement of equipment and the safety of my crew. An unexpected drone overhead is an unnecessary distraction that diverts attention away from my assigned job. We do not need, nor can we afford, to have our focus compromised by technology hovering over active switching operations or other safety sensitive tasks.

I am deeply concerned by the use of drones for surveillance and operational testing. We are tasked with transporting hazardous chemicals through communities every single day. If a drone is hovering over our facility, how am I—or any of my coworkers—expected to differentiate between an "operational test" and an act of terrorism or sabotage?

By normalizing the presence of drones, management is creating a "boy who cried wolf" scenario. If we are conditioned to ignore drones as routine operational testing, we lose the ability to identify and respond to real, malicious threats. This is a security failure that puts both the workforce and the surrounding public at risk.

Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. We understand the specific risks, layout, and operational realities of our yards better than anyone else. Yet, these practices are being normalized without meaningful consultation with the people actually doing the work.

We deserve to have a seat at the table before our workplaces are turned into testing grounds for surveillance technology. I urge you to prioritize the safety of the workforce and the security of the critical infrastructure we manage by keeping drones out of rail yards and facilities.
Sincerely,

David Bunting
Railroad Conductor (21 Years of Service)

Drone Operations Around Transportation Facilities
I am a TE&Y employee with 22 years of service in train, engine, and yard operations. I am writing in support of restricting drone operations around rail facilities, because I have seen firsthand the safety hazard they create.
A rail yard is one of the most dangerous workplaces in the country even on a normal day. Crews are switching cars, riding equipment, lining track, and working constant radio and hand-signal communication in tight quarters where one missed signal or one moment of divided attention gets someone killed. Our entire job depends on focus and clear communication. A drone flying overhead breaks that focus at exactly the wrong moment.
These drones are not being flown by aviation professionals. In my experience they are operated by trainmasters and managers running surveillance and operational testing on crews, flown directly through and around live switching moves — often from the parking lot. When a crew is in the middle of a shove or a kick and a drone drops into their line of sight or their airspace, attention goes up instead of staying on the equipment and the people on the ground. That is a distraction we cannot afford in an active yard.
After 22 years, I can tell you the workers in the yard are the ones who actually know what is safe there, and we were never consulted before this practice started spreading. Drones overhead add risk without adding any safety value to the people doing the work. I urge the FAA to adopt meaningful restrictions on drone operations around rail facilities and to recognize frontline railroad workers as the safety experts on this issue.

I have over 28 years of railroading experiencand we need help on this topic.
Unmanned aerial vehicles (drones) can pose significant safety risks in railroad operations. Unauthorized drone activity in railyards and along active track corridors may distract employees, interfere with situational awareness, and increase the potential for injuries or serious bodily harm. To help maintain a safe working environment, we believe drone operations should be restricted in railyards and near railroad tracks except where specifically authorized for railroad, maintenance, inspection, emergency response, or other approved operational purposes. Thank you for your attention to this important safety matter.
That active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
That frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety.
That drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.
That railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.
That normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests.

As a new trainee I seen multiple drones on numerous occasions flying around.  During times when the guys on the ground need to be focused on the train moves.  Which is anytime trains are being kicked, shoved, trains being built, switching, basically anytime a train is moving, that's where the focus needs to be.  Being distracted by a drone overhead is a problem the men on the ground don't need and shouldn't have to put up with.  The MTOs should be going out of their way talking with conductors and engineers, trying to foster working relationships.  Instead, they seem to be preoccupied with playing gotcha and writing guys up for safety violations.  Then everybody wonders why morale is so low and why management and union employees can't get along.  Common sense, look out for your fellow man.

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to raise the serious safety concern regarding the use of unmanned aircraft systems, commonly referred to as drones, by railroad management to monitor employees and initiate disciplinary referrals against train and engine service employees working in active rail yards.

Railroad operations, whether conducted within a yard or over the line of road, are inherently safety-sensitive. Trainmen are required to maintain constant situational awareness while working around moving equipment, switches, shove movements, close clearances, radio communications, and other employees on the ground. Any unnecessary distraction introduced into that environment creates an unacceptable risk.

The use of drones flying above or near employees actively engaged in switching operations is not merely intrusive; it is dangerous. These aircraft create visual and auditory distractions in an environment where a momentary loss of focus can result in serious injury or death. Employees naturally look toward, react to, or become aware of drones operating overhead, and that distraction can interfere with their ability to safely perform duties that already demand undivided attention.

This practice is especially needless because railroads already maintain extensive fixed-camera surveillance throughout rail yard facilities. The use of drones in addition to existing camera systems is absolutely unnecessary for legitimate operational oversight and is solely designed to generate disciplinary referrals. Using aerial surveillance in this manner introduces a new hazard into an already hazardous workplace without any corresponding safety benefit.

In short, the deployment of drones over active rail operations for employee surveillance and discipline is an unnecessary and unsafe practice. It creates foreseeable distractions that can contribute to operational errors with potentially fatal consequences. I respectfully request that the FAA deny, through regulation, this practice as it diverts employee attention in a setting where even a brief distraction can result in catastrophic injury or death.

To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing to express serious concerns regarding the use of drone testing and operations within active railroad yards while railroad employees are performing work on the ground.
The railroad industry is inherently dangerous and unpredictable. Employees must remain constantly aware of their surroundings and maintain complete focus on their tasks to safely perform their duties. Our work environment requires the use of all our senses to identify hazards, monitor train movements, communicate with coworkers, and react to changing conditions.
The presence of drones operating overhead creates unnecessary distractions. Employees can both see and hear these devices, which naturally draws attention away from the task at hand. Any distraction, no matter how brief, can increase the likelihood of an accident in an industry where mistakes can have severe consequences, including serious injury or loss of life.
Additionally, drones introduce another potential hazard into an already hazardous work environment. Equipment failures, loss of control, or unexpected incidents could result in a drone falling into active work areas, creating additional risks for employees working below.
Rather than improving safety, drone testing in active work zones may increase risk exposure by diverting workers’ attention from critical tasks and introducing new hazards into the workplace. Railroad employees need the ability to maintain 100 percent focus on their work without unnecessary distractions or concerns about objects operating overhead.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that drone testing and operations not be conducted in active railroad yards or in areas where employees are performing work on the ground. Employee safety should remain the highest priority, and eliminating avoidable distractions and hazards is essential to maintaining a safe work environment.
Thank you for your consideration of these concerns and for your commitment to protecting the safety of all railroad employees.
Respectfully,
Chad Galyean

I have been a railroad conductor for twenty years. Focus on the task at hand is of the utmost importance to keep our co workers and the general public safe.

Loss of life and limb is a major possibility in our line of work and, having something flying over your head while trying to keep your mind 100% on task is a dangerous distraction.

The drones the railroad uses have ALWAYS caused a distraction to my co workers, especially at night when it's pitch black and suddenly there are flashing lights buzzing around in the sky.

Thank you for taking the entirety of railroad workers safety into account and hopefully not allowing these drones to distract us anymore. V

Most of the uses of drones by management of the railroads are not used in an appropriate manner. They are used to spy on active crews and see if they make mistakes. Many times the manager spying does not even stop the crew members when they are doing something potentially dangerous they just allow them to continue to do the wrong thing and get it on video with the drone. I personally have trouble doing my job because I have to continually look to the sky to see if I am being spied on instead of looking at the task at hand. I that flying drones for the sole purpose of watching active switching operations should not be allowed at customer facilities or in rail yards.

I am writing to formally express concerns regarding the increasing use of drones by Trainmasters and MTO personnel within active railroad operating environments.

Numerous employees have observed drones being operated during switching operations, around working crews, and in areas where employees are engaged in safety-critical duties. These observations raise serious concerns regarding workplace safety, distraction, and the potential impact on employees performing their assigned responsibilities.

Railroad operations require constant situational awareness, effective communication, and unwavering attention to the task at hand. The presence of drones operating overhead or in close proximity to crews can create unnecessary distractions and may interfere with employees’ ability to safely perform their duties. This is especially concerning during switching operations and other activities where hazards are already present and concentration is essential.

In addition, questions exist regarding the policies, qualifications, risk assessments, and operating procedures governing the use of drones in these environments. Employees deserve to know:

What training and certifications are required for personnel operating drones in railroad operating territories.
Whether formal risk assessments have been conducted regarding drone operations around active crews and equipment.
What restrictions exist concerning drone proximity to employees, moving equipment, and hazardous work locations.
How employee safety and privacy concerns are being addressed.
Whether the use of drones has been reviewed by the appropriate safety committees and labor representatives.

The purpose of this letter is not to oppose technology or legitimate safety initiatives. Rather, it is to ensure that any use of drones within railroad operations is conducted in a manner that prioritizes employee safety and does not introduce additional hazards into an already demanding work environment.

Safety must remain the primary consideration in all operational decisions. Employees should never be placed in a position where surveillance activities create distractions or additional risks while performing safety-sensitive duties.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Respectfully,

Rob Cavazos
SMART-TD Local Chairman Temple, Texas

While in the process of pulling my train forward, a drone appeared within 20' of the front of my locomotive as a huge distraction. On the shove onto the yard,the same drone followed within 20'of the cab and sides of the cab moving back and forth creating a very unsafe condition to where I had to stop the move. Upon doing so,the drone veered off into the yard. A trespasser crossed over 2 hot main lines and I to an active yard to try to find his drone. I hade to tell him to leave the premises. A BNSF crew heard us on the radio talking about the drone and said it apparently hit a power line in the yard and fell on top of their crew hauler vehicle. This individual put the safety of multiple train crews at risk because there aren't regulations keeping all drone activity out of railroad airspace regardless of company drones or private drones. They are a distraction and an unnecessary component to rail safety.

Regarding drone usage in railroad terminals.

I am a locomotive engineer, having worked as a brakeman/switchman, remote control operator (remote locomotives) and conductor. My time working in transportation has been from 05/2004 - 06/2012, and 11/2023 - present

From 06/2012 - 10/2023 I was a manager of operating practices and qualified to operate a drone per railroad rules. I have seen the drone usage from both sides of this argument.

Rail yards are a dangerous place to work. The equipment is big, heavy and unforgiving. A lighter car may way 32 tons (64,000 lbs) and a loaded car 130 tons (230,000 lbs). You don't get scratches and bruises, you lose limbs, become physically disabled or die when accidents happen.

Crews are required to focus on safety and the task at hand. Railroad managers use drones to observe trainmen performing switching moves, looking for rule violations. As the railroad I work for has become more heavy handed with discipline, train crews are often more concerned about getting disciplined than the task at hand. When you add a drone into the equation it increases anxiety and loss of focus resulting in an even more unsafe environment.

Management has demonstrated their willingness to sacrifice safety for rule violations. I have personally witnessed, when I was a manager, another manager who covered a white strobe on the bottom of the drone with electrical tape to conceal his drone from anyone on the ground looking up.

Railroad rules requires drone operation for testing purposes to be at elevations of 200+ feet AGL. I have witnessed others operating their drones below this threshold to get a better view of the crew with the camera.

At the inception of the drone program, any drone operation required two managers. One operating the drone and a second look out who was required to have a visual on the drone at all times. This rule has been relaxed and now a single drone operator performing the test is permitted. Nobody is required to observe the drone to ensure it is not at risk of a mid-air collision with other aircraft or stationary objects.

Crews have no way to identify a railroad operated drone or one operated by the public. Railroads haul millions of gallons of chemicals that fall under the TIH/PIH (Toxic/Poison Inhalation Hazard). One car can hold as much as 33,000 gallons. This is the stuff when spilled that kills people and evacuates communities. Imagine a crew derailing a tank car with 30,000+ gallons of anhydrous ammonia or chlorine and the tank rupturing because the conductor is distracted by a drone. Perhaps it's a terrorist attack in progress and conductor thinks it's just a manager testing him with a drone, so he doesn't report it.

In summary, we, as rail workers, don't need added distractions to an already unsafe industry. We appreciate the FAA investigating our concerns and encourage the FAA to put in place rules that will protect our crews, our infrastructure and our communities.

Thank you.
