*The FAA does well to improve efforts to protect our National Airways System and Critical Infrastructure from intentional (security-based) threats and/or unintentional (safety-based) harm posed by unmanned aircraft systems.

*Owners/operators of critical infrastructure (CI) facilities must maintain a reasonable expectation of privacy when submitting standard or special unmanned aircraft flight restriction (UAFR) requests for their locations.

*CI requests for UAFRs and their corresponding records must be kept confidential, be treated with absolute privacy protections, and remain indefinitely exempt from the Freedom of Information Act public disclosure requirements.

*We urge the FAA to ensure these protections to CI stakeholders to foster good will and sustainable confidence in this UAFR request process.

I was working the bowl area (classification tracks rail cars are being humped or sorted in to) of my rail yard one day while in the middle of a track I first hear a buzzing sound. Thinking I’v stumbled upon a near by bee hive or something I start moving a little more apprehensively. I finally noticed there was no hive around or anything as far as dangerous insects, and seen the drone hovering above. Now this is the scary part. While I couple (tie) rail cars together in tracks I always stay out of the foul of the track because it’s a rule, it’s safe, and it’s a great practice. Even if for whatever reason you lose your concentration you will at least be out of the foul of the track and more than likely not be stuck by equipment. So, I’m staring at this drone and it’s staring at me. The whole time both tracks on both sides of the track I’m coupling together are hot (rail cars free rolling from the hump operation). So basically at this point I lose concentration while staring at this drone, not knowing who is operating it. I did not give consent to be in a video and pedestrians are prohibited from flying drones over our yard for safety reasons. All of a sudden I take my eyes off the drone and look forward to the cars rolling towards me on the adjacent track when I see a shifted load on a rail car coming right at me. The metal sheets on this rail car had shifted and were now in the foul of my walkway. I quickly moved in between two of my rail cars where I had protection against movement to let these cars pass by. Had I not moved I would have been struck with shifted metal around the neck area. Needless to say, we know how this could have ended. This whole story is just to show how much of a distraction drones flying around a rail yard can be. The railroad is a dangerous profession and sometimes seconds count. Any distraction for even a couple seconds can be fatal. Please don’t allow drones over rail yards.

I strongly oppose the use of drones to monitor railroad workers. While safety is an important goal, constant aerial surveillance creates new risks and unintended consequences.
Railroad employees already work in high-pressure environments where concentration and communication are critical. Knowing that drones are continuously watching and recording their every movement can increase stress, distract workers from their tasks, and contribute to a culture of mistrust between employees and management.
There are also serious concerns about privacy, data security, and how collected footage may be used. Without clear limits, drone surveillance could be used for disciplinary purposes unrelated to safety, discouraging workers from reporting hazards or speaking up about unsafe conditions.
Most importantly, safety is best improved through proper training, adequate staffing, well-maintained equipment, and open communication—not by expanding surveillance. Before implementing drone monitoring programs, regulators should carefully evaluate the impact on worker safety, privacy, morale, and labor relations.
Railroad workers deserve safe workplaces, but they also deserve reasonable expectations of privacy and respect while performing their jobs.

Drone operations are considered unsafe due to the risk of mid air collisions with manned aircraft, property damage, and severe physical injuries from crashes.  Drones are a distraction, loss of situational awareness, and sensory interruption are critical factors in keeping railroad workers focused on there job.  All it takes is a second to have a catastrophic event; I.e. lose of limb or life. Managers are inadequate pilots without the proper training to operate these drones. None of them have participated at a college or certified instructors course to operate these drones.  A few hours course doesn't constitute the proper training to operate these drones.  If a drone suffers battery failure, structural malfunction, or is clipped by high winds it turns into a free-falling debris field and railroad personnel become directly threatened with severe injury or death.  Power lines, building heights, cell phone towers in populated and urban areas that aren't mapped out or studied become hazards as well.  Railroad workers have no idea who is actually flying these drones.  are they enthusiastic railfans, terrorists, or possibly railroad management.  There are railcars that literally carry thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals and flammable materials that have the ability to kill hundreds of thousands of people in seconds if there is a mishap with a drone.  Railroads also use drones as an intimidating factor to enforce rules by claiming operational testing which creates a toxic work environment with the constant anxiety and the threat of being fired on a daily basis.  The only occupation in America where management on a daily basis is trying to fire you and Omaha HQ of union Pacific wants 8 failures per month per manager.  How are we suppose to actually function in this type of work environment and still serve the nation on time with there products.

Drones are a huge distraction! I see them daily while switching 250+ cars a day to build a train. The CN preaches safety and to not get distracted… yet they let this nonsense continue. I have no idea whether or not it is management using these to watch their employee work, or if it is just simply a person with a hobby for trains. Nonetheless, huge distraction.

Drones over railroad operations are categorically unsafe for those working under them while in the air.  It is a critical distraction while we (railroaders) need to be paying close attention to our work at hand.  For example: I hear something and look up and step in a hole or worse.  Ankle/knee/back injury follows shortly thereafter.  And do you believe the railroad will “take care” the injured party.  Nope.  I can go on and on with examples.  It is just dangerous on many levels (dangerous to railroaders I mean!).  Also, they fly them in restricted airspace too (around prisons, etc).  The fed and state law enforcement officials have had to respond to make them ground their drones.  The point?  The railroad doesn’t care about rules that apply to them, and they will do what they want.  Please ban and make drone usage unlawful as soon as possible.  Thanks.

Drones have no place in a rail yard. I am sure the faa is looking at this for the safety of the aircraft and the public that would be affected. But as a railroad employee I believe that drones are an unsafe and unnecessary risk. The job that we perform is not a safe job. There are tons of moving parts and risks that you have to be aware of at all times. We cannot afford distractions. When a manager is flying a drone right above your head and following you around that is a major distraction. The railroad is notorious for trying to find any reason to fire employees. With a drone following you around the yard, your head is definitely not in the game because you know they are looking for any mistake that can be made.

Any drone activity while railroaders are working would be a complete distraction and create a very unsafe work environment.  With almost 30 years of railroad service I am absolutely opposed to any drone use!

To have drones flying over us while trying to perform our job safely is nothing but a hazard due to distraction and mentally stressful which those two together do nothing but create issues mistakes and problems for ourselves and the company

As a freight conductor I think drone use around rail operation should be illegal due to its unsafe interference. Rail operations is one of the deadliest professions and a distraction from any drone could be fatal. Our heads are on a swivel already. A buzzing noise or in flight failure of a drone in a Critical Focus Zone could be a death sentence for a railway worker. There are plenty of stationary cameras for them to observe that don't interfere with our safety

When I was working, I became very paranoid, and worried more about being watched than doing my job. I was almost hit by a rolling car, because I was walking and saw a drone from my peripheral vision and stopped for a split second. The stress that it causes when you don’t feel like you can do your job safely in the work environment weighted on me daily to the point that I lost sleep. I fear for others safety, and their lives. Constantly looking at the sky when you see something out of your peripheral vision takes away from the task at hand, and can cause serious implications and possible death.

Thank you
Please do not support drones on railroad property.  They are distracting to workers that are already in a dangerous situation on the jobsite.  This policy by the railroads should be stopped at once.

I think a drone flying above, around, or near me, while trying to work around heavy equipment would be very dangerous. In our line of work, you need 100% focus, or you may lose a limb, or your life. Its already distracting having a plane or helicopter flying by, but to have the constant buzzing of a drone near by, or seeing it moving around in the air while trying to keep focus on the task at hand, without looking at it by reaction, would be quite difficult. Aside from the major distractions, what happens when the "pilot" of the drone loses connection, or if the battery dies and the drone falls out of the sky and injures an employee, or even kills one due to the shear size and weight of most drones today. Considering they would be hovering above or near employees in a single spot, watching them, the increased risk of them being in the line of fire of a drone failure, is far above just a drone flying by. Im not sure why we need drones constantly monitoring our work as we already have cameras everywhere in our yards or on the locomotives. I think having someone following me around watching me as I work, is equally as distracting as a drone, and unsafe for focus. Why are we not having management do that? I think the question answers itself.

As a seasoned railroader with 15 years of service with the the Union Pacific railroad I know for a fact that drones have no business in rail yards where safety is supposed be the number 1 focus. I know when I’m I’m coming in a yard me and my coworkers are looking for drones flying around while we’re trying to focus on switches and derails. If I have to get down and perform work I’m always looking up to see if drones are watching me. I don’t ride rail cars anymore because of drones being around. This slows production but it’s the safest course. Drones are unnecessary. If management wants to test then they need to get off their lazy butts and get in the field and do their jobs. They preach safety but us that live this life on a daily basis know that the only thing that matters is shareholders and profits!!!!!

comments.commenter: Comment from Drone Service Providers Alliance
comments.comment_text:
Vic Moss
2100 S. Carr St.
Lakewood, CO 80227
vic@dspalliance.org
303-748-1133
dspalliance.org
15 June, 2026
RE: Public Comment on NPRM for Proposed 14 CFR Part 74 UAS Facility Restrictions
(2209 NPRM)
To Whom It May Concern:
The Drone Service Providers Alliance (DSPA) is a national, nonprofit advocacy organization
representing U.S.-based commercial drone operators. DSPA advocates for policies that promote
safe integration of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) into the National Airspace System while
protecting the ability of professional drone operators to conduct lawful commercial operations.
Our membership and stakeholder community includes nearly 34,000 FAA-certificated Remote
Pilots across the United States who rely on predictable, uniform, and federally managed access to
the National Airspace System to serve clients in industries including infrastructure inspection,
energy, agriculture, public safety, construction, surveying, real estate, media, and transportation.
On behalf of these operators, DSPA respectfully submits the following comments regarding the
proposed establishment of Unmanned Aircraft Facility Restrictions (UAFRs) under 14 CFR Part
74.
I appreciate the opportunity to comment on the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking concerning the
establishment of Unmanned Aircraft Facility Restrictions (UAFRs). The FAA faces the difficult
task of balancing legitimate security concerns with maintaining safe, efficient, and equitable
access to the National Airspace System (NAS) for unmanned aircraft operators. The proposed
framework is a positive step, but several provisions should be clarified and strengthened in the
final rule.
Classification of UAFRs
The NPRM appropriately recognizes that not all facilities warrant the same level of protection.
The proposed distinction between Standard UAFRs (sUAFRs) and Special UAFRs (SUAFRs) is
well-founded and should be retained.
Standard UAFRs should be reserved for facilities that have operational concerns but do not
require national security-level protection. Examples include:

• Power generation facilities exceeding 500 megawatts;
• Railroad facilities;
• Petroleum refineries;
• Amusement parks;
• Zoos; and
• Similar private-sector facilities.
Special UAFRs should be limited to facilities with demonstrable national security, public safety,
or corrections-related concerns, including:
• Nuclear power plants;
• Department of Defense facilities;
• Correctional facilities and prisons; and
• Other federally designated national security sites.
Maintaining this distinction will ensure that restrictions remain proportional to the risks
presented.
Real-Time Notification and Access Procedures
For Standard UAFRs, the FAA should require facility operators to maintain a simple, reliable,
and real-time notification process that enables legitimate drone operations. The Low Altitude
Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC) system provides an excellent model for
streamlined access and should serve as the benchmark for any notification process established
under this rule.
The final rule should ensure that all unmanned aircraft broadcasting compliant Remote ID are
eligible to access Standard UAFRs after completion of the required notification process. Access
should not be limited by operational category and should include operations conducted under:
• 14 CFR Part 107;
• 49 U.S.C. §44809 recreational operations;
• Part 108, when implemented;
• Part 135 operations;
• Part 137 agricultural operations; and
• Any other FAA-authorized operation.
Demonstrated Need for Standard UAFRs
Applicants seeking Standard UAFR designation should be required to demonstrate a legitimate
operational need. Mere concern about drone activity should not be sufficient.
Applicants should provide documented evidence of verifiable unmanned aircraft incursions that
have materially disrupted day-to-day operations, safety, or security. This requirement will help
ensure that airspace restrictions are imposed only where justified by actual operational impacts.

Operator Contact Requirements
Every facility granted Standard UAFR status should be required to maintain a single, publicly
accessible point of contact available to qualified drone operators. Operators must have a reliable
means to obtain clarification regarding notification procedures, operational requirements, and
facility-specific questions.
Boundary and Buffer Zone Standards
Standard UAFR boundaries should be limited to the actual fence line or property boundary of the
protected facility. Restrictions extending beyond facility boundaries should be avoided unless
clearly justified.
If the FAA determines that a buffer zone is necessary in the final rule, the buffer distance should
be standardized nationwide rather than varying by facility. Uniform standards improve
compliance, reduce pilot confusion, and promote predictable airspace management.
Prohibition on Private UAS Mitigation Activities
The final rule should explicitly state that designation as a Standard UAFR does not confer
authority for private entities or private security personnel to engage in UAS interdiction,
mitigation, disruption, or take-down activities.
Accordingly, 14 CFR Part 74 should specifically deny any implication of authorization for
private UAS mitigation activities by facilities granted Standard UAFR status. Such authorities
should remain exclusively within the scope of federal law and authorized federal agencies as
defined under the SAFER SKIES Act.
Avoiding Fragmentation of the National Airspace System
The FAA should ensure that Part 74 does not create a fragmented and inconsistent operating
environment for unmanned aircraft.
Access through and within Standard UAFRs should not be limited solely to transit operations.
There are legitimate circumstances where a drone operator may need to operate over a facility to
photograph, inspect, map, or document adjacent properties or infrastructure. Restricting
operations exclusively to transit would unnecessarily limit legitimate and otherwise safe uses of
unmanned aircraft.
Likewise, any altitude restrictions established over Standard UAFRs should be reasonable,
standardized, and clearly defined. Facility-specific altitude requirements would increase
complexity and reduce compliance.

Federal Preemption
The final rule should include explicit federal preemption language to prevent the creation of
overlapping state and local airspace restrictions that would undermine the FAA's exclusive
authority over the National Airspace System.
I recommend inclusion of the following provision:
§ 74.240 Federal Exclusivity and Preemption
(a) Exclusive Federal Authority.
The regulation of unmanned aircraft operations and airspace restrictions established under this
part is an exclusive Federal function.
(b) Preemption.
This part preempts any State, Tribal, territorial, or local law, regulation, ordinance, or other
requirement that restricts, prohibits, conditions, or penalizes unmanned aircraft operations within
the National Airspace System based on proximity to critical infrastructure or fixed-site facilities.
No State or local government may establish or enforce an unmanned aircraft exclusion zone,
stand-off distance, or operational restriction except as authorized by Federal law or regulation.
Any existing or future conflicting State or local requirement shall have no force or effect.
(c) Preservation of Traditional State Authority.
Nothing in this section affects State or local authority over privacy, land use, nuisance, reckless
endangerment, or other ground-based conduct not involving regulation of airspace or flight
operations.
Conclusion
The FAA has an opportunity to create a balanced framework that addresses legitimate facility
security concerns while preserving access to the National Airspace System for responsible
unmanned aircraft operators. By maintaining clear distinctions between Standard and Special
UAFRs, requiring demonstrated need, establishing simple notification procedures, prohibiting
private mitigation activities, standardizing boundaries and buffer zones, and reaffirming federal
preemption, the FAA can enhance security without unnecessarily burdening lawful drone
operations.
Thank you for considering these comments.
Sincerely,
Vic Moss
CEO/CoFounder DSPA

I am a drone pilot from Florida who flies both recreationally and under Part 107, and I build my own FPV drones. I support protecting infrastructure that genuinely needs it, and parts of this proposal are reasonable. But the transit provision, as written, doesn't work the way I think the FAA intends — and there's a cleaner way to do it.

Start with the notification requirement. A Standard zone can cover many kinds of facility — substations, cell towers, and other everyday infrastructure that ends up scattered across ordinary neighborhoods. Letting an operator cross one after notifying the facility sounds fine until you picture an actual flight. It works when the facility is the point of the flight — an inspection crew heading to that exact site can call ahead. But the facility is rarely the point. Take a substation: it sits at the edge of a park, and the person flying is filming a birthday or a sunset. The zone just happens to be there.

In that situation the requirement breaks down for everyone, not just recreational flyers. A Part 107 pilot filming a kids' game next to a substation has no more practical ability to identify and notify that facility in advance than a hobbyist does. Neither one is visiting the facility; they're just near it. Requiring advance contact with a site that has nothing to do with the flight, just to clip the corner of its airspace, isn't a workable condition — it's a closure with a courtesy step attached. And these zones won't be rare. Substations and cell towers are nearly everywhere, many beside the exact parks where people have always flown — and the map fills with small pockets of restricted airspace, each demanding a reroute or a phone call.

It's also worth being honest that not all of this "critical infrastructure" carries the same risk. The rule treats the airspace over a neighborhood substation much like a chemical plant capable of a toxic release, though the two sit at opposite ends of the consequence scale. The worst realistic outcome of a small drone near a substation is a localized, recoverable disruption — not a catastrophe — and the airspace rules should reflect that difference instead of flattening it. It's also worth noting the rule restricts a 250-gram quad over these sites while a manned paramotor, carrying far greater payload, may overfly the very same site with no restriction at all — which suggests the line is being drawn around the wrong thing.

There's a straightforward fix that requires no new drone rules at all: add these facilities to LAANC. LAANC is the FAA's existing automated authorization system. It already checks each request against multiple data layers in the FAA UAS Data Exchange and either auto-approves instantly or routes the request for coordination. A critical-infrastructure facility would become one more layer in that same exchange — a marked zone with its own ceiling and a handling flag: auto-approve with automatic notification, or coordination required. This reuses infrastructure that already exists and adds nothing new for the operator to learn.

The operator does nothing new: open LAANC as usual, see the zone, request, get an instant answer. Skirting a substation by a park clears automatically, and the facility receives an automatic notification — the same way air traffic control is already notified of every LAANC authorization today. No one has to identify and contact the facility separately in advance. The facility gets exactly the awareness it wanted, and the Remote ID receiver this rule already requires confirms what's overhead. This is not a new system to build; it is a new participant on one that already runs every day. The only real mechanic is keeping the facility layer synced to the UAFR database as sites are added, renewed, or canceled — data plumbing, not new authority, and no change to how anyone flies.

Finally, leave sub-250-gram recreational aircraft out of the everyday low-sensitivity zones — the substations and cell towers, not the genuinely sensitive Special sites, which should still apply to everyone. The FAA already treats these aircraft as low enough risk to be exempt from Remote ID and registration when flown recreationally, and that judgment doesn't stop being true near a transformer. It makes no sense to exempt them everywhere else and then bar them from crossing one, or to invent a new Remote ID mandate just so they can. Someone flying a sub-250-gram drone recreationally over a park shouldn't be one gust over a fence line from a federal violation for passing a transformer behind the tree line. If an aircraft is light enough to be left alone everywhere else, it's light enough to be left alone here.

Protect the sites that genuinely need it, put the rest where pilots already look, and keep the lightest, most common flying out of the crosshairs. That balance is what will make this rule one people can actually follow.

A Florida Part 107 pilot and recreational flyer.

To the FAA Docket:

I am a commercial drone operator based in Fort Worth, Texas, operating under Part 107. My company, Steal Shot Aerials, provides aerial photography, videography, inspection, and construction documentation services to clients throughout Dallas-Fort Worth — including work at critical infrastructure sites, commercial developments, and large-scale industrial facilities. I write as a working operator who navigates airspace restrictions daily.

I support the FAA's intent to establish a clear, petition-based process under Section 2209. A transparent framework is preferable to the current patchwork of ad hoc restrictions. However, the NPRM as drafted raises several concerns for small commercial operators, and I urge the FAA to address the following points before finalizing this rule.

1. The Transit Carve-Out in § 74.250 Must Be Preserved and Strengthened

The provision allowing Part 107, 108, 135, and 137 operators to transit UAFR airspace with Remote ID, the shortest practicable route, and advance notification is essential. Without it, UAFRs become de facto no-fly zones that block legitimate commercial work — including critical infrastructure inspection, surveying, and emergency response. I support this carve-out and urge the FAA to retain it in the final rule.

However, the "advance notification" requirement needs a standardized, streamlined mechanism. If every UAFR has its own notification process, operators will face an unmanageable compliance burden — particularly small operators without legal or dispatch teams. The FAA should establish a single electronic notification portal and require that UAFR holders accept and respond to transit requests through that portal within a reasonable timeframe. Without this, the transit right becomes theoretical.

2. UAFR Boundaries Must Be Machine-Readable and Real-Time

For Remote ID compliance and flight planning, UAFR data must be published in a machine-readable format (GeoJSON, NOTAM-style digital feed) and updated in real time. Relying on static PDFs or manual NOTAM review is incompatible with modern flight operations. I request that the FAA require digital publication of all UAFR boundaries, altitudes, effective dates, and contact information — integrated into LAANC and B4UFLY so operators can plan and adjust in the field.

3. Guard Against Overly Broad UAFR Definitions

Sixteen critical infrastructure sectors is a wide net. If facility operators can petition for UAFRs that extend well beyond their actual security footprint, the cumulative effect will be a fragmented national airspace — particularly in dense urban and industrial corridors like DFW, where multiple qualifying facilities exist in close proximity. I request that the FAA:

(a) Require petitioners to demonstrate specific, evidence-based risk rather than relying on categorical designation alone.

(b) Limit UAFR horizontal and vertical dimensions to the minimum necessary to address the demonstrated risk.

(c) Reject petitions where an existing airspace restriction (TFR, NOTAM, controlled airspace) already addresses the concern.

4. The Cumulative Burden on Small Operators

Each UAFR adds another layer of preflight research, notification, and routing. For a single-pilot operation with no support staff, the compliance overhead adds up fast — and every minute spent navigating restrictions is a minute not spent on the job I'm being paid for. The FAA should consider the cumulative operational impact on small businesses, not just each restriction in isolation, and include a small business flexibility analysis that acknowledges this reality.

5. UAFRs Must Not Become a Tool to Block Commercial Competition

I am concerned that facility operators could use the UAFR petition process to exclude commercial drone operators for competitive reasons rather than legitimate security concerns. The FAA's review process must include scrutiny of the petitioner's stated rationale and reject petitions that appear to serve anti-competitive purposes.

6. Emergency and Time-Sensitive Operations

The rule should include clear exceptions for emergency response, disaster assessment, and time-critical infrastructure inspection. If a bridge or pipeline needs immediate aerial inspection after a severe weather event, the operator should not be blocked by a UAFR notification backlog.

Summary

I urge the FAA to:

• Retain and strengthen the § 74.250 transit carve-out
• Require machine-readable, real-time UAFR data publication
• Limit UAFR dimensions to evidence-based minimums
• Build a single, standardized notification system
• Include meaningful small business impact analysis
• Prevent anti-competitive use of the petition process
• Provide clear emergency access provisions

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Respectfully,
Jonathan Smith
Pilot / Owner, Steal Shot Aerials
Fort Worth, Texas
Part 107 Certified Remote Pilot

Re: Comments on Notice of Proposed Rulemaking — Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No.
26-03; RIN 2120-AL33 — Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (91 FR 24650, May 6, 2026)
I. Introduction and Statement of Interest
My name is James Gurney. I submit these comments as an individual certificated remote pilot
operating under 14 C.F.R. Part 107, doing business as Johnston Aerial. My commercial operations
include aerial media production for events and marketing clients, construction progress
documentation, and photogrammetric mapping services performed as a subcontractor. My work
regularly takes me to a wide variety of locations across multiple states, including areas in close
proximity to critical infrastructure facilities of the kind addressed in this rulemaking.
I am supportive of this rulemaking. The FAA's proposal to establish Unmanned Aircraft Flight
Restrictions (UAFRs) under a new 14 C.F.R. Part 74 fills a regulatory gap that has been evident
to anyone who operates UAS in the field. I operate near a nuclear generating facility whose cooling
water reservoir is also a public recreational lake where I fish regularly and have conducted drone
flights — both recreational and in anticipation of future commercial work such as aerial
videography for charter fishing operators. For years I have been aware that there was no formal
airspace restriction mechanism protecting that facility from UAS intrusion. That gap is a security
problem. I strongly support closing it.
At the same time, I write to urge the FAA to ensure that the access and notification framework in
proposed Subpart G is designed in a way that preserves meaningful operational access for
certificated, vetted Part 107 commercial operators. Specifically, I recommend that the FAA
leverage the existing Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC) system as
the primary authorization mechanism for Part 107 operators seeking access to Standard UAFRs. I

also write in support of strong federal preemption of the patchwork of state critical infrastructure
drone laws that have accumulated in the absence of this federal rule.
II. The UAFR Framework Is Necessary and Overdue
Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 directed the FAA to establish
this process nearly a decade ago. The delay has had real consequences. During that decade, the
UAS industry has grown dramatically, the threat landscape has evolved, and critical infrastructure
operators have been left without a coherent federal mechanism to protect their facilities from UAS
intrusion. At the same time, state legislatures — filling the vacuum left by federal inaction — have
enacted a disjointed set of drone laws that create compliance uncertainty for commercial operators
who cross state lines.
The two-tier framework proposed in Part 74 — Standard UAFRs for facilities such as electrical
substations, water treatment plants, and nuclear generating stations, and Special UAFRs for
sensitive federal facilities — is a rational and proportionate structure. The requirement that
applicants under proposed § 74.66 demonstrate a documented need, including evidence of existing
UAS activity in proximity to the facility and an articulation of the consequences of an exploited
vulnerability, appropriately filters out facilities that do not genuinely require protection while
providing a clear pathway for those that do.
From my perspective as a working pilot, I can attest that the absence of any restriction around
certain categories of critical infrastructure is not theoretical. I have flown, legally and without any
required notification or approval, in the vicinity of a nuclear generating facility simply because the
lake adjacent to its cooling water infrastructure is a public recreation area where I and others fish
and recreate. A certificated commercial operator conducting legitimate work in that area today has
no established process to self-identify, no mechanism to distinguish himself from a bad actor, and
no way to obtain clarity from the facility operator. A UAFR framework addresses all of these
problems simultaneously — for the facility, for the FAA, and for the legitimate operator.
III. The FAA Should Use LAANC as the Authorization Mechanism for Part 107 Access to
Standard UAFRs
The most significant practical question this rulemaking must resolve for working commercial
pilots is: what does the authorization or notification process under proposed § 74.255 actually look
like? The answer to that question will determine whether Part 107 operators have workable access
to UAFR airspace or whether legitimate commercial operations are effectively foreclosed by
bureaucratic friction.
I urge the FAA to adopt LAANC — the Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability
already in operation across the national airspace system — as the authorization mechanism for
certificated Part 107 operators seeking access to Standard UAFRs. LAANC is not a new system.
It is already deployed, already integrated into the planning workflows of virtually every
commercial Part 107 operator in the country, already familiar to the FAA's own air traffic

organization, and already capable of processing authorizations in seconds rather than days.
Requiring commercial operators to use a new, separate portal or manual approval process for
UAFR access when an automated, proven system already exists would be redundant and would
impose unnecessary compliance costs on small operators like me.
The case for LAANC is not merely one of convenience. Part 107 certification itself represents a
meaningful layer of vetting. A certificated remote pilot has passed an FAA aeronautical knowledge
test, is registered in FAA systems, and operates subject to regulatory oversight. Using LAANC to
authorize Standard UAFR access leverages that existing vetting infrastructure. A Part 107 operator
who obtains LAANC authorization to fly in a Standard UAFR airspace has identified himself to
FAA systems, agreed to applicable operational parameters, and created an auditable record of the
planned operation — all automatically, at no additional cost to the FAA or the facility operator.
I note that the NPRM's own list of abbreviations recognizes LAANC as a relevant system. The
framework for using LAANC in controlled airspace under Part 107 — where digital authorization
is granted automatically up to applicable ceiling limits — is directly analogous to what is needed
for Standard UAFR access. I encourage the FAA to make explicit in the final rule, whether in §
74.255 or in accompanying guidance, that LAANC authorization satisfies the access notification
requirement for certificated Part 107 operators operating within Standard UAFR boundaries.
I do not suggest that LAANC is appropriate for Special UAFRs covering sensitive federal
facilities. Those designations plainly require a more rigorous access process, and I do not object
to that. But for Standard UAFRs — the tier designed to protect facilities like substations, water
treatment plants, and industrial sites — automated Part 107 authorization through LAANC is both
technically feasible and operationally appropriate.
IV. The Final Rule Should Expressly Preempt Inconsistent State Critical Infrastructure
Drone Laws
As a commercial Part 107 operator, my work is not confined to a single state. I have performed
aerial services across state lines for clients in different jurisdictions, and I anticipate that as the
commercial UAS market grows, multi-state operations will become increasingly routine for
operators like me. The current regulatory environment is untenable for interstate commercial
operators: approximately 25 states have enacted their own critical infrastructure drone restrictions
in the absence of a federal rule, and those laws vary significantly in scope, covered facility types,
prohibited operations, and penalties.
A Part 107 operator planning a multi-state project today must conduct a state-by-state legal survey
before every job — a compliance burden that falls most heavily on individual operators and small
businesses that lack in-house legal counsel. This is precisely the kind of regulatory fragmentation
that federal preemption is designed to address. The FAA has exclusive authority over the navigable
airspace of the United States under 49 U.S.C. § 40103, and this rulemaking presents a direct and

appropriate vehicle for the agency to exercise that authority to establish a single, uniform national
standard.
I urge the FAA to include express preemption language in the final rule making clear that 14 C.F.R.
Part 74, once effective, preempts inconsistent state and local laws purporting to restrict UAS
operations over or near critical infrastructure. This is not a request to override legitimate state
interests — it is a request for the clarity and uniformity that interstate commercial operators need
to do their jobs lawfully and efficiently. A patchwork of 50 different airspace regimes governing
the same category of operations is incompatible with the nature of aviation as an inherently
interstate activity.
V. Conclusion
I support the FAA's proposal to establish a formal UAFR framework under 14 C.F.R. Part 74. This
rule addresses a genuine security gap that I have observed firsthand in my years of operating
commercially under Part 107. The framework is well-structured, the two-tier approach is
proportionate, and the requirement that applicants demonstrate documented need is an appropriate
safeguard against overreach.
I ask the FAA to finalize the rule with two enhancements that will make it work for the commercial
operators who will live with it every day: first, designate LAANC as the authorization pathway for
Part 107 access to Standard UAFRs, leveraging existing infrastructure rather than creating new
bureaucratic friction; and second, include express preemption of inconsistent state critical
infrastructure drone laws to give interstate commercial operators a uniform, workable national
standard.
Thank you for the opportunity to comment. I am available to provide additional information if it
would be useful to the agency.
Respectfully submitted,
James Gurney
Operating as Johnston Aerial
Part 107 Certificated Remote Pilot
Submitted via regulations.gov
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558

Public Comment Recommendation: Pro-Drone / Low-Restriction Framework for 14 CFR §74

1. Implement a Streamlined "Notify-and-Fly" System for Standard UAFRs
The FAA proposes a "Standard UAFR" category for lower-risk critical infrastructure (such as electrical substations, power grids, and railroad facilities). If every single one of these common sites requires a manual, drawn-out approval process, commercial scalability will grind to a halt.

The Recommendation: Have an automated, transparent infrastructure similar to the Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC).

The Mechanism: For Standard UAFRs, certified Part 107 pilots operating Remote ID-compliant aircraft should be granted automatic access via a "Notify-and-Fly" protocol. Submitting a flight notification through a LAANC-enabled app should serve as immediate clearance, avoiding manual vetting by facility owners who may not understand aviation needs.

2. Strictly Limit the Scope and Vetting of Applicants (Subpart B)
There is a high risk of "airspace creep" if private utility companies or critical infrastructure owners overreach and apply for sweeping, massive restriction zones. For example, the Association of American Railroads previously requested restrictions covering 140,000 miles of track.

The Recommendation: FAA to strictly enforce the requirement that all UAFR applications must be narrowly tailored and backed by compelling, site-specific security justifications.

The Mechanism: Establish clear boundary limits. Standard UAFR lateral boundaries should be confined to the actual property footprint of the asset, rather than wide lateral buffers (e.g., rejecting arbitrary 50-foot or 100-foot buffer zones around linear infrastructure like tracks or pipelines) to preserve the use of adjacent low-altitude airspace.

3. Establish Absolute Federal Preemption over Critical Infrastructure Airspace
Currently, 25 states have passed a patchwork of fragmented, confusing local critical infrastructure drone laws. This creates an impossible compliance environment for regional and national commercial operators.

The Recommendation: The final rule should explicitly assert total Federal exclusivity over the National Airspace System (NAS) regarding critical infrastructure.

The Mechanism: Include the addition of a dedicated preemption clause (e.g., a proposed § 74.240) stating that 14 CFR §74 supersedes and invalidates all existing and future state, local, or tribal airspace restrictions near critical infrastructure. Local authorities should retain control over ground-based conduct (trespass, privacy) but have zero jurisdiction over the air.

4. Separate Airspace Restrictions from Counter-UAS (C-UAS) Mitigation Authority
Allowing localized, private, or state critical infrastructure entities to take active drone mitigation (jamming or downing aircraft) into their own hands poses a severe safety hazard to legitimate commercial flights.

The Recommendation: Strongly state that 14 CFR §74 must remain strictly an airspace restriction framework and must not grant any hardware mitigation or counter-UAS authority to designated facilities.

The Mechanism: The FAA must oversee active drone mitigation and authorities must remain strictly regulated under separate federal legislation, such as the SAFER SKIES Act, to prevent localized entities from unsafely interfering with commercial aircraft.

5. Create a Single, Centralized Digital Map Interface - and Include with LAANC.
Commercial UAS pilots cannot safely comply with rules if they cannot easily see where the restrictions begin and end.

The Recommendation: The FAA must mandate that all approved Standard and Special UAFRs be published in real-time on a single, centralized digital registry - with LAANC.

The Mechanism: This data must be seamlessly integrated into existing FAA UAS Data Maps and third-party LAANC provider apps. If a UAFR zone is not mapped and viewable digitally, it should not be legally enforceable against a remote pilot

comments.commenter: Comment from Jon C. Haverstick Photography
comments.comment_text:
SUBJECT: 2209 Notice of Proposed Rule Making: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
To Whom it May Concern:
I am writing to express my concerns and opposition regarding the proposed 2209 NPRM, which
as currently written would create a new 14 CFR Part 74 establishing a process by which
designated critical infrastructure facilities may apply for protected status and defining who may
conduct unmanned aircraft operations over those sites and under what conditions.
As an FAA Part 107-certified commercial drone pilot since 2019 who uses drones extensively in
my southern California-based architectural photography business, the rule as currently
proposed would make it extraordinarily difficult (and in many cases impractical) to conduct
operations in urban environments.
The challenge is that many of the facilities potentially eligible for protected status are woven
throughout the urban landscape. Railroads, electrical substations, transmission corridors,
power generation facilities, refineries, and similar infrastructure are common features in the
areas where I work. Rail lines and power infrastructure, in particular, crisscross virtually every
metropolitan area. If a significant number of these facilities were granted protected status,
large portions of urban airspace could effectively become inaccessible to legitimate commercial
drone operators such as myself conducting lawful business activities.
While my business represents only one segment of the commercial UAS industry, the impacts
would extend far beyond architectural photography. Surveying, construction documentation,
real estate marketing, infrastructure inspection, mapping, public safety support, media
production, and countless other commercial drone applications could be affected by a
regulatory framework that creates overlapping restrictions throughout populated areas.
For standard UAS Facility Restrictions (UAFRs), a system patterned after the existing LAANC
framework could provide a more balanced solution. The current LAANC system has
demonstrated that a streamlined, digital "notify-and-fly" or authorization-based process can
safely facilitate drone operations while protecting sensitive airspace. For operators such as
myself, who have no need to fly near highly sensitive federal facilities or national security sites,
a predictable and standardized approval process would provide both operational flexibility and
appropriate security safeguards.
I am also concerned about provisions that would grant protected facilities counter-UAS
authority. Congress has already addressed cUAS authorities through legislation such as the
Safer Skies Act of 2025. Expanding or duplicating these authorities through this rulemaking risks
creating confusion regarding enforcement responsibilities, operator rights, and the scope of
authority granted to individual facilities.

Another significant concern is the potential creation of a patchwork authorization system. If
approvals, restrictions, or operating requirements vary by facility, state, tribe, territory, or local
jurisdiction, drone operators such as myself would face a confusing and extraordinarily
burdensome compliance environment. This would be particularly problematic when federal
systems already exist to communicate airspace restrictions and facilitate approvals, including
LAANC and the FAA UAS Data Map.
A fragmented regulatory structure would increase costs and administrative burdens not only
for small businesses like mine, but also for larger-scale operations such as beyond visual line of
sight (BVLOS) drone delivery networks, utility inspections, infrastructure monitoring, and other
emerging commercial applications that depend on consistent national standards.
For that reason, I strongly support the inclusion of explicit federal preemption language, such
as:
"§ 74.240 Federal Exclusivity and Preemption
(a) Exclusive Federal Authority.
The regulation of unmanned aircraft operations and airspace restrictions established under this
part is an exclusive Federal function.
(b) Preemption.
This part preempts any State, Tribal, territorial, or local law, regulation, ordinance, or other
requirement that restricts, prohibits, conditions, or penalizes unmanned aircraft operations
within the National Airspace System based on proximity to critical infrastructure or fixed-site
facilities.
No State or local government may establish or enforce an unmanned aircraft exclusion zone,
stand-off distance, or operational restriction except as authorized by Federal law or regulation.
Any existing or future conflicting State or local requirement shall have no force or effect.
(c) Preservation of Traditional State Authority.
Nothing in this section affects State or local authority over trespass, privacy, land use, nuisance,
reckless endangerment, or other ground-based conduct not involving regulation of airspace or
flight operations."
The United States has become the global leader in commercial drone innovation in large part
because the National Airspace System is governed by a uniform federal framework. The success
of the drone industry depends upon predictable, consistent, nationwide regulations that allow
operators to understand and comply with the rules wherever they fly.
The decisions made in the final 2209 rule will have long-lasting consequences for thousands of
public agencies, infrastructure operators, emerging UAS technologies, and small businesses like

mine who know the laws and abide by them. Once a complex network of protected facilities,
approval processes, and operational restrictions is established, it will be difficult, confusing, and
costly to reverse unintended consequences.
For that reason, it is critical that the final rule strikes the proper balance between security and
access, preserves federal control of the National Airspace System, avoids creating a patchwork
of local restrictions, and incorporates streamlined authorization mechanisms that allow
legitimate commercial drone operations to continue. As we have seen even at the beginning of
the FIFA World Cup events, bad actors have no regard for the rules. So the economic impact of
the 2209 rule as currently written is only upon law-abiding businesses like mine.
Getting the 2209 Final Rule right the first time is essential to protecting both national security
interests and the continued growth and viability of the U.S. drone industry.
Thank you for your consideration.
Jon Haverstick
JON C. HAVERSTICK | PHOTOGRAPHY
Commercial Photographer • FAA Part 107-Licensed Drone Pilot

Good evening, I work for a class 1 railroad in Tampa Florida. The company has been using drones to conduct operational testing for a number of years now and constantly violate FAA part 107 regulations. They violate state and federal regulations when flying over critical infrastructure. Company managers fly drones at night constantly without the anti collision lights turned on or with them not visible for up to one mile and have said they have waivers from the FAA to operate with the lights off at night. I have personally made multiple calls and reports to my local FAA person Tom Fecks. He guided me to http://faa-aae.my.site.com/s/hotline to make my concerns official. I have not once heard anything back about my allegations. These drones are a major safety concern to rail safety. Rail yards are incredibly soft targets for terrorism and with managers constantly flying drones over our heads, we will become desensitized to their presence. There’s plenty of videos online now showing how effective drones are in combat scenarios and we don’t need anything like that happening in America. Another thing is rail worker safety. Managers crash these drones occasionally and it’s only a matter of time before a worker gets hit with one. Another safety concern is if workers are always scanning the skies for the drones, that takes their attention off of what the should be focused on which is moving rail cars. This more than likely has already caused injury somewhere in the country. We must keep our railroads and our country safe WITHOUT flying drones around active rail facilities.
Drone operation in the rail industry is a distraction in an already dangerous environment.  When crew are being monitored or followed by drones, the drone becomes the focus and not the task at hand.

My main concerns with this regulation are:

1. Drones are already highly regulated

Each year, more regulations are proposed that would restrict legitimate unmanned aircraft usage further. The reason behind the regulations is apparent—people are constantly violating existing regulations. However, why will people decide to honor new regulations surrounding “critical” infrastructure?

Every time new regulations are introduced, it only hurts the people who are legally operating an unmanned aircraft under 14 CFR Part 107 regulations or USC 44809 recreational exception.

2. This regulation will hurt businesses

My first point with this regulation is the impact it will have on legitimate businesses that use drones for business functions. On a daily basis, a business might need to operate a drone to gather photos. An inspection company might need to photograph the roof of a home for insurance claims. With this regulation, if there’s a “critical” power grid two houses down, that business would be unable to perform their duties from the sound of this regulation.

3. The inconsistency between manned and unmanned aircraft restrictions

I was recently traveling through the Rocky Mountains. As expected, Grand Teton National Park is off limits to drone operations. Ironically, amidst this National Parks is an KJAC airport with commercial jets landing and taking off frequently, disturbing the environment. Yet, drones which are significantly quieter, smaller, and less dangerous are prohibited.

My point here is this: If “critical” infrastructure is to be off limits to drones, it should also be off limits to manned aircraft. In reality, a regulation is not going to stop someone with malicious intent from flying a drone or aircraft into an area. A criminal is not going to stop committing a crime because a law exists. “Critical” infrastructure should be off limits to manned aircraft as well.

A manned aircraft has the capacity to do immense damage to critical infrastructure like a dam or power grid. A drone wouldn’t be able to do comparable damage unless it was equipped with weaponry (which violates other FAA regulations).

4. Requests through FAA drone zone to fly in controlled airspace already takes 90+ days for approval
When LAANC approval is unavailable in controlled airspace, authorization had to be done through the FAA drone zone website. FAA drone zone takes well over 90 days to be approved or rejected. If it is required to give 90+ days of notice to operate within “critical” infrastructure, that becomes unreasonable. Waiting a quarter of the year to conduct compliant and legal aerial imagery for business purposes is ridiculous.

As I understand it, the proposed regulations would be a notification-based system rather than approval-based. This helps partially with this problem, but there are two remaining questions I see:
1. If I launch a drone and a “critical” infrastructure is added to the airspace in my flight path in the middle of an operation, would I be in violation of this new regulation?
2. How much notice must Part 107 operators provide to facilities? Even a small delay can directly impact businesses. It seems appropriate that it be allowable to submit a notice anytime prior to the start of the operation.

5. If someone is intent on damaging “critical” infrastructure, this regulation won’t stop them

Currently there are two categories of drone operators (outside of cases where the FAA makes exceptions). You either fly under the standard 14 CFR Part 107 regulations or USC 44809 recreational exception. Anyone operating a drone outside of those two cases is already violating the law.

If a rule is added to restrict the airspace near “critical” infrastructure, who do you think is going to follow that rule? Those who are already legally operating drones under 14 CFR Part 107 regulations or USC 44809 do not have the intention to damage “critical” infrastructure. The people who would fly near and potentially damage that infrastructure are those who are already breaking the existing laws. Since they’re already breaking the law, why would they honor a new law that protects “critical” infrastructure?

The proposed regulations would only hurt those who respect and operate in compliance with existing regulations.

Please restrict drone and surveillance equipment on railroad workers. I have been harassed and bothered by these actions of railroad carrier causing safety issues.
By having them in safety sensitive work zones cause workers to tense up and deviate concentration from tasks to cause injury, strain, or mental distraction.
Drones in the work place being used for testing and discipline rather than assisting. Major distraction in the workplace when trying to stay focused and vigilant of work tasks.

I am a rail freight conductor who works both yard and main line assignments.

The reason for eliminating drone observation in yards is obvious: normalizing flights overhead while working runs counter to the carriers mandate to remain vigilant for suspicious activity. The idea that we are supposed to have the ability to discern friendly from unfriendly drone flight is ridiculous. The reason managers used to test while hiding in the weeds is because they don't want to be a distraction. But drones don't hide in the weeds, they are right overhead and always within a line of sight. If they can see me, I can see them. That's a distraction.

It's a bit different out on the main. Work there is inconsistent and as such can be considered non-routine. A bit of extra focus is required because the task is not something we do every day, so learned safety behavior is still being refined. And as such the presence of a drone may cause us to look away at precisely the wrong time. Accruing discipline would be bad, but it would pale in comparison to sustaining an injury because of focus shifted away due to sudden movement or sound.

In talking to old heads I know that hiding in the weeds has been a testing staple since the 1940's. What happened that it's no longer valid? Just because something can be done doesn't mean it should be done.

comments.commenter: Comment from Northern Plains UAS Test Site
comments.comment_text:
June 1st, 2026
Submitted electronically via Federal eRulemaking Portal: www.regulations.gov
Docket Operations, M-30
United States Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W12-140, West Building Ground Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Re: Northern Plains UAS Test Site Comments on the Federal Aviation Administration,
Department of Transportation Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, “Designation of Unmanned
Aircraft Flight Restrictions [Docket No. FAA-2026-4558]
To Whom It May Concern:
The following comments are respectfully submitted in response to the Federal Aviation
Administration’s (FAA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking related to proposed Part 74. These
comments are intended to support regulatory clarity, operational scalability, and efficient
implementation of unmanned aircraft system (UAS) operations within restricted airspace
environments. The recommendations below are focused on improving consistency,
streamlining operational approval processes, and ensuring the final rule remains practical
for both operators and regulators.
Respectfully Submitted,
Trevor Woods
Executive Director, Northern Plains UAS Test Site

1. Introduction
The comments below identify areas where specific revisions, additional criteria, and
operational processes would strengthen the effectiveness and implementation of proposed
Part 74. These recommendations are organized by regulatory section and provide
suggested revisions intended to improve usability, transparency, and scalability.
2. Recommendations
2.1. Evaluation (§74.100)
Comment
We recommend the FAA define "good cause" as circumstances in which immediate action is
necessary to address a demonstrated safety, security, or national defense concern and
where the delay associated with notice-and-comment procedures would materially increase
operational risk. Proposed §74.6 would permit the FAA to forgo notice-and-comment
procedures when good cause exists but does not define the circumstances under which that
authority would be exercised. Because the use of the good-cause exception directly affects
public participation and operator awareness, we recommend the FAA adopt objective
criteria for its application. Specifically, good cause should be limited to circumstances
involving a demonstrated national security, homeland security, or immediate public safety
threat where delay associated with notice-and-comment procedures would materially
increase risk. Establishing these criteria would improve transparency and consistency while
preserving the FAA's ability to respond rapidly to urgent situations.
Resolution
Define "good cause" as circumstances in which immediate action is necessary to address a
demonstrated national security, homeland security, or public safety threat and where delay
associated with notice-and-comment procedures would materially increase risk. The FAA
should document the basis for any good-cause determination and limit use of the exception
to situations where the restriction is narrowly tailored to the identified threat.
Rationale and Data
Clear and objective evaluation standards will improve regulatory predictability, reduce
inconsistent interpretation, and provide operators with greater confidence in the approval
process.
2.2. Term (§74.200)
Comment
Proposed §74.6 would establish a five-year duration for Special UAFRs. We recommend the FAA
establish a recurring review or revalidation process at least once every three years to confirm
the continued necessity and appropriateness of approved restrictions or operational authorities.

This would align with existing FAA internal practices that generally reevaluate orders and
regulations on a 3-year basis and would ensure approvals remain current with evolving
operational, technological, and safety considerations. The review should confirm that the
operational need for the restriction continues to exist, that the facility remains eligible under
Part 74, and that no significant changes have occurred to the facility, surrounding airspace, or
operational environment that would materially affect the original approval.
Resolution
Require an administrative review every three years to confirm continued operational need,
facility eligibility, and that no material changes have occurred to the facility, surrounding
airspace, or operational environment.
Rationale and Data
Periodic review aligns with existing FAA practices that routinely reevaluate orders and
regulations and ensures authorizations remain current, justified, and operationally relevant.
2.3 Allowed Operations (§74.250) – “Sustained Operations”
Comment
Proposed §74.250(a)(2) requires that an unmanned aircraft operating within a UAFR
"transition the UAFR in the shortest amount of time practicable." While this requirement
may be appropriate for operators merely transiting through a restricted area, it appears to
preclude sustained operations conducted within a UAFR except through discretionary
authorization by the Administrator. We recommend the FAA establish a formal sustained-
operations approval process within Part 74 rather than requiring operators to seek
individual approvals or exemptions for recurring activities. As written, the proposal does
not provide a clear pathway for long-duration or recurring operations that must remain
within the restricted area to accomplish the intended mission. A standardized approval
framework would provide greater regulatory certainty while maintaining appropriate
oversight and safety controls.
Resolution
Establish a sustained-operations approval process allowing operators to conduct recurring
or long-duration activities within a UAFR under a single authorization when the operation
remains within an approved scope, geographic area, and set of operating procedures.
Rationale and Data
Providing consistent expectations for sustained operations would improve predictability for
operators while reducing administrative burden for both industry and the FAA.

2.4. Access Procedures (§74.251)- “Re-Occurring Operations”
Comment
Proposed §74.251 establishes a mechanism for operators to access/transit UAFRs but does
not clearly identify how recurring or long-term operations will be managed once initial
approval is granted. As written, operators may be required to repeatedly seek authorization
for substantially identical operations. We recommend the FAA develop a streamlined,
automated access and authorization system similar in concept to the Low Altitude
Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC) framework. Such a system would
improve operational efficiency by enabling faster approval processing while maintaining
appropriate oversight and safety controls.
Additionally, we recommend:
•Streamlining approval processes for Part 146 operational areas through the use of
multi-year approvals where appropriate.
•Providing additional clarification, potentially through an Advisory Circular or other
means, regarding what constitutes a “form and manner acceptable to the
Administrator.”
•Ensuring all submission processes and authorization systems are designed to be
practical, accessible, and user-friendly for the typical operator.
Resolution
Develop a streamlined, automated approval system similar to LAANC to enable rapid
authorization processing. The system should allow operators to submit requests
electronically, track application status, obtain recurring authorizations for approved
operations, and receive automated approvals for predefined low-risk activities that meet
established criteria. Additionally, provide clarification regarding the required “form and
manner acceptable” through an Advisory Circular and ensure the process remains practical
and accessible for typical operators. Multi-year approvals for Part 146 operational areas
should also be considered where appropriate.
Rationale and Data
A scalable and automated approval process would improve operational efficiency, reduce
delays, and better support routine UAS operations while maintaining FAA oversight.

2.5. Altitude Ceiling (§74.60)
Comment
Proposed §74.60 generally limits UAFRs to 400 feet AGL but permits the altitude ceiling to
extend above 400 feet when the tallest component of the protected facility exceeds 300 feet
AGL. Under §74.60(c), the restricted airspace may extend from the surface to the height of
the tallest structure plus 100 feet, rounded up to the next 50-foot increment. While the
proposed restriction would therefore extend into airspace that may be routinely utilized by
manned aircraft operating in accordance with applicable regulations, the associated
operational restrictions would continue to apply exclusively to unmanned aircraft.
We recommend the FAA provide a clear risk-based justification for extending UAFRs above
400 feet AGL while limiting the resulting restrictions solely to unmanned aircraft
operations. If the FAA has determined that unmanned aircraft present unique safety,
security, or operational concerns at these higher altitudes that are not similarly presented
by manned aircraft, that rationale should be explicitly documented in the final rule.
Providing this explanation would improve stakeholder understanding, enhance regulatory
consistency, and help demonstrate that the vertical scope of the proposed restrictions is
appropriately tailored to the risks the rule is intended to address.
Resolution
Proposed §74.60(c) permits UAFRs to extend above 400 feet AGL when the protected
facility contains structures exceeding 300 feet AGL. We recommend the FAA provide a
detailed explanation of the safety, security, or operational basis for applying restrictions
exclusively to unmanned aircraft within these higher-altitude portions of a UAFR, where
manned aircraft may also routinely operate.
Rationale and Data
The NPRM generally aligns UAFRs with the operational environment of unmanned aircraft
by limiting the altitude ceiling to 400 feet AGL. However, §74.60(c) allows restrictions to
extend above 400 feet AGL based on the height of structures located within the facility
boundary. At these altitudes, the restricted airspace may overlap with airspace routinely
occupied by manned aircraft operating under existing regulations. Despite this overlap, the
proposed restrictions would continue to apply only to unmanned aircraft operations. While
there may be valid reasons for this distinction, the NPRM does not clearly explain why
operations above 400 feet AGL warrant restrictions on one class of aircraft but not another.
Additional explanation would improve transparency, support regulatory consistency, and
help ensure the scope of the restriction remains proportional to the risks being addressed.
3. Conclusion

We appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed Part 74 rulemaking.
These recommendations are intended to support the FAA’s efforts to establish a clear,
efficient, and scalable regulatory framework for UAS operations while maintaining safety
and operational effectiveness within the National Airspace System.

comments.commenter: Comment from HUNTRAK Defense Systems, Inc.
comments.comment_text:
Re: Docket FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; FR Doc. 2026-08943, "Designation - Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility"; implementing Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016.

HUNTRAK Defense Systems, Inc. respectfully submits these comments on the FAA's NPRM implementing Section 2209. HUNTRAK is an early-stage, U.S.-based developer of passive, receive-only, multi-sensor systems for small uncrewed-aircraft-system (UAS) detection and airspace awareness. Its work is limited to detection and situational awareness; it does not transmit, jam, decrypt, disable, interdict, or otherwise affect any aircraft or signal.

HUNTRAK supports a transparent, consistent process for eligible fixed-site facilities to request Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) while preserving the FAA's authority over the National Airspace System. We support the proposed Section 74.56 requirement that eligible facilities be able, directly or through a contracted service, to receive broadcast Remote Identification messages.

We respectfully recommend that the final rule:

1. Expressly recognize lawful passive detection. Clarify that new Part 74 does not prohibit fixed-site operators from deploying lawful, passive, receive-only detection technologies for situational awareness, compliance monitoring, and incident documentation, and that passive detection confers no airspace-control or enforcement authority.

2. Treat the passive-RF Remote ID minimum as a floor, not a ceiling. Because Remote ID reception alone cannot detect non-cooperative aircraft (drones not broadcasting Remote ID, or autonomous or RF-silent drones), confirm that more advanced lawful passive approaches (acoustic, electro-optical, infrared, and multi-sensor fusion) are acceptable means of meeting and exceeding the requirement.

3. Maintain a clear distinction between detection and mitigation. Passive detection receives information without transmitting toward, interfering with, controlling, or disabling an aircraft or signal. Mitigation raises materially different legal, safety, and spectrum considerations. The rule should address the two separately and make clear that authorizing a UAFR does not authorize mitigation.

4. Provide an accessible, workable application process usable by smaller facilities as well as large operators. Consistent with Section 74.56(4), the FAA should permit reliance on qualified contracted monitoring services while keeping responsibility for the UAFR with the eligible operator or proprietor.

5. Address Remote Identification data retention proportionately. If retention is required, set a reasonable, clearly-defined period with privacy, cybersecurity, and access-control safeguards; hosted platforms can support compliant, auditable retention. Any requirement should be proportionate to the cost burden on smaller facilities.

6. Use technology-neutral, standards-based requirements rather than favoring a single sensor type, protocol, or vendor; emphasize measurable functions, interoperability, cybersecurity, and data governance.

HUNTRAK appreciates the FAA's work to implement Section 2209. Clear recognition of lawful passive detection (distinct from mitigation, and able to extend beyond the passive-RF Remote ID minimum) will help operators understand their responsibilities and adopt appropriate awareness tools.

Respectfully submitted,
HUNTRAK Defense Systems, Inc.
Office of the Chief Executive Officer

drones are unsafe and creepy and can cause a freight conductor to become distracted while protecting shove movements in the freight yard, potentially causing death or dismemberment, derailment, etc.

I am a Trainman, a conductor, for Union Pacific. I'm not against rules testing. I'm not against safety evaluations, I'm not against the FRA. But drones are a different can of worms. I've seen countless videos of suicide drones crashing into transport vehicles, and drones dropping grenades into enemy positions.
